By . Damien Gayle . The destruction of Brazil's Amazon rainforest has slowed to its lowest level since monitoring began, it was announced yesterday. Satellite images showed that 1,798 square miles of the Amazon were slashed and burned between August 2011 and July 2012, said Brazil's environment minister Izabella Teixeira. That's 27 per cent less than the 2,478 square miles cut down a year earlier. Scroll down for video . Smoke billows as flames burn trees in the Amazon rainforest: Brazil today announced that the rate of deforestation in the Amazon has fallen to its lowest levels since records began . Brazil's National Institute for Space Research said the rate of deforestation is the lowest since it started taking measurements in 1988. The Amazon constitutes more than half of the planet's remaining rainforest, and it is also the largest and most species-rich tract of tropical rainforest in the world. Brazil contains 63 per cent of the vast rainforest's 2.4million square miles. The space institute said the latest figures show Brazil is close to its 2020 target of reducing deforestation by 80 per cent from 1990 levels. Through to July 2012 rates of deforestation have dropped by 76.26 per cent. George Pinto a director of Ibama, Brazil's environmental protection agency, said stricter enforcement of environmental laws and improved surveillance technology are behind the drop in deforestation levels. Virgin Amazon jungle in Mato Grosso State, one of the Brazilian states of greatest deforestation: The Amazon constitutes more than half of the planet's remaining rainforest and is home to a huge range of species . The destruction of the Amazon began in earnest in the Sixties with farmers slashing and burning forests to make space for crop cultivation. Colonists found soils were only productive for a limited time, spurring them to clear more and more forest and causing widespread environmental damage. However, even before the introduction of large scale agriculture, humans have long had an impact on the Amazon basin. Archaeological evidence suggests the first inhabitants settled the region at least 11,200 years ago, and by 1500AD some 5million people may have lived in the region. Francisco de Orellana, the first European to travel along the Amazon river, reported a complex civilisation flourishing on its banks in the 1540s. Other evidence suggests the forests, rather than being pristine wilderness, have in fact been shaped by man for at least 11,000 years through practices like forest gardening. Terra preta (black earth), which is distributed over large areas in the Amazon, is now widely accepted as a product of soil management indicating portions of the forest are the result of centuries of human shaping. It is thought many of the region's original indigenous inhabitants were wiped out by the spread of diseases like smallpox carried by European explorers. Mr Pinto said that in the 12-month period a total of 2,000 square meters of illegally felled timber were seized by government agents. The impounded lumber is sold in auctions and the money obtained is invested in environmental preservation programs. Ms Teixeira said Brazil will next year start using satellite monitoring technology to detect illegal logging and slash-and-burn activity and issue fines. Adalberto Verissimo, a senior researcher at Imazon, an environmental watchdog agency, said: 'Over the past several years Brazil has made a huge effort to contain deforestation and the latest figures testify to its success. 'The deforestation figures are extremely positive, for they point to a consistent downward trend.' 'The numbers disprove the argument that deforestation is necessary for the country's economy to grow', he said by telephone from his office in the Amazon city of Belem. 'Deforestation has been dropping steadily for the past four years while the economy has grown,' he said . 'But the war is far from over. We still have a lot of battles to fight and win.' Marcio Astrini, Greenpeace coordinator in the Amazon region, said the lower figures show that reducing deforestation is perfectly possible. But he added that 'the numbers are still too high for a country that does not have to destroy one single hectare in order to develop.'

For much of Saturday, ING's Romanian customers could not access their money.
The bank said the discharge of its gas-based fire suppression system had caused "unexpected" damage to its computer servers.
A report by Motherboard suggests that the equipment was too noisy.
A spokeswoman for ING was unable to confirm this detail.
But she did acknowledge the problem had lasted from 13:00 to 23:00 local time and the bank had been unable to explain the situation to customers as its own communications system had been affected.
"Due to the magnitude and complexity of breakdown, the time required to restore the activity through the back-up system was unfortunately longer than during the tests we perform regularly," said Iosefina Frolu.
"Currently, we are finalising the diagnostic analysis of this exceptional event."
The fire extinguisher system used at the Bucharest site worked by expelling a mix - known as inergen - of nitrogen, argon and carbon dioxide.
These gases are normally not harmful to computer equipment.
But, according to Motherboard, the high-pressure release created a sound that topped 130 decibels.
A study last year by the German engineering group Siemens warned the nozzles used by some fire suppression systems could cause "fatal" damage to hard disks because of sound wave vibrations they sent through the air.
"The hypothesis is that sound causes vibrations in the HDD case that are ultimately transmitted to the disk spindle and head assembly, causing misalignment of the read/write heads to the data tracks," it said.
"Because there are many different designs of storage system chassis and cabinets on the market, the issue of vibration caused by noise as well as structure-borne vibration has not yet been fully investigated."
Siemens's research indicated that different server models were vulnerable to different noise frequencies, and if there was a match:
Although ING said the accident was "abnormal and unprecedented" in its experience, there have been other suspected incidents.
In 2013, the French media reported that accountancy software used by the country's government had become temporarily unavailable after a fire protection system had been accidentally triggered at a data centre, causing a loud noise.
The following year, inergen extinguishers were used to put out a fire at a facility in Sydney, Australia.
When several of the disks failed, some experts blamed noise vibrations caused by the discharge.

The money will see the repair and re-use of up to 30 buildings within the city's conservation area.
The buildings have not yet been identified but Upper English Street and Russell Street are among the areas set to benefit from the funding.
HLF said the five-year project will create 20 jobs and five new businesses.
They added it will also secure a further 10 jobs and four existing businesses.
Support was awarded through the fund's Townscape Heritage (TH) programme.
Lord Mayor of Armagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon, Darryn Causby, has welcomed the award.
"This will enable us to build on the excellent work already completed in the city," he said.
"Armagh has a rich built heritage and the funding will help restore and enhance this culture for future generations."
Paul Mullan of HLF said the plans would "play an important role in regenerating the city's commercial core, restoring its economic heart and opening up its unique heritage for both local people and visitors to enjoy".

**★★★★☆ A Gripping Psychological Thriller That Keeps You Guessing**

"The Silent Partner" delivers a masterfully crafted psychological thriller that will leave readers questioning everything they think they know. Author Sarah Chen weaves a dark tale of deception, betrayal, and deadly secrets that unfolds across the seemingly perfect suburban landscape of Millbrook Heights.

The story follows Detective Rachel Morrison as she investigates the brutal murder of prominent businessman David Kellerman, only to discover that his picture-perfect life was built on lies. As Rachel delves deeper, she uncovers a web of extramarital affairs, financial fraud, and blackmail that implicates nearly everyone in Kellerman's orbit. Chen's ability to plant red herrings while maintaining logical plot progression is impressive—I found myself suspecting different characters with each chapter.

The adult content is integral to the story rather than gratuitous, exploring themes of sexual manipulation, domestic violence, and the psychological toll of keeping dangerous secrets. Chen handles these mature elements with appropriate gravity while maintaining the book's relentless pacing.

The character development is particularly strong. Rachel Morrison is a complex protagonist whose own troubled past mirrors the case she's investigating, creating compelling internal conflict. The supporting cast feels authentic, each harboring believable motives and secrets.

My only criticism is that the final reveal, while shocking, relies on a coincidence that feels slightly contrived. However, the journey to that revelation is so expertly crafted that this minor flaw doesn't significantly detract from the overall experience.

At 340 pages, "The Silent Partner" is a page-turner that respects its readers' intelligence while delivering genuine surprises. Fans of Gillian Flynn and Tana French will find much to appreciate here.

By Sam Webb. A 23-year-old woman has been arrested for murder after a seven-year-old girl died. Police were called to a house in Nottingham's Bestwood Park around 5:10 AM today. They were worried about the child. People in the area say the woman arrested is Kay-Ann Morris, who wanted to be a police officer. Police are still at the scene in Bestwood Park today. A shocked neighbor said the woman, who went to church, was 'strict.' He added that the sad young girl was a 'stunning little girl.' The neighbor, Geoff, said: 'I didn't know much about her, and no one else here did either. She tried to keep to herself. I think she had two little girls. You only saw them when they went to or came home from school. The girl was beautiful, just a stunning little girl. It's so sad she's gone. She was very shy and quiet. It was clear she lived under strict rules.' People in the area say the 23-year-old woman is Kay-Ann Morris, who wanted to be a police officer. Doctors will check the body tonight to find out how she died. A picture shows some bags outside the front door. Geoff added: 'About eight police cars and at least two ambulances were there. I saw the man and Kay-Ann taken to a police van. A little girl was carried out wrapped in a blanket. This is all so tragic. We never have trouble here. The woman once had a small argument with a neighbor who doesn't live here now. It was about kids throwing snowballs and ended quickly. I think I'll get some flowers now because it's just so sad. Nothing like this ever happens here. It's usually a nice area.' Another neighbor described the woman as 'strict' and the little girl as 'quiet.' Someone from the Seventh Day Advent Church in Nottingham said Morris went to their church. Morris reportedly wanted to be a police officer. When she was 17, she was part of a Nottinghamshire Police program to get more young people to join the force. Back then, Kay-Ann said: 'Since I was four, I've wanted to be a police officer. Being on Tap The Gap made me want to do this even more. It also gave me more self-confidence.' A 47-year-old man has also been arrested for assault. A police spokeswoman said doctors would perform tests later to find out how the girl died. She wouldn't say if the man and woman, who are still with the police, are related to the child. We are not taking comments on this story right now.

**Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind: A Masterpiece of Memory and Love**

★★★★★

Charlie Kaufman and Michel Gondry have crafted something truly extraordinary with "Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind" – a film that operates simultaneously as a heartbreaking romance, a surreal science fiction adventure, and a profound meditation on the nature of memory itself.

Jim Carrey delivers a career-defining performance as Joel, stripping away his comedic persona to reveal raw vulnerability and emotional depth. Kate Winslet is equally magnificent as Clementine, bringing manic energy and genuine pathos to what could have been a mere manic pixie dream girl archetype. Their chemistry feels lived-in and authentic, making their relationship's dissolution all the more devastating.

The film's central conceit – erasing painful memories through a medical procedure – serves as both compelling plot device and rich metaphor. As we journey through Joel's fragmenting mind, Gondry's inventive direction transforms the landscape of memory into something tangible and surreal. Practical effects blend seamlessly with emotional storytelling, creating moments of pure visual poetry: houses collapsing into sand, people without faces, childhood memories bleeding into adult relationships.

What elevates this beyond typical sci-fi romance is Kaufman's sophisticated script, which refuses easy answers. The film doesn't simply argue that painful memories are worth preserving; instead, it explores how our sorrows and joys are inextricably intertwined, how the very experiences that hurt us also define who we are. The supporting cast – including Kirsten Dunst, Mark Ruffalo, and Tom Wilkinson – adds layers of complexity, showing how the central themes ripple outward beyond just Joel and Clementine's story.

"Eternal Sunshine" is that rare film that rewards multiple viewings, revealing new emotional truths and narrative details with each encounter. It's a movie about the beautiful messiness of human connection, arguing that love isn't about finding perfection, but about choosing someone despite – and perhaps because of – their flaws.

Fifteen years later, this remains one of cinema's most honest and innovative explorations of love, loss, and the stories we tell ourselves about both.

**Unlocking Wireless Gridlock: UB Researchers Develop Cognitive Radio Technology for Faster, Smarter Connections**

It’s rush hour, and every single car is crammed into the right lane of the highway, while the center and left lanes sit completely empty. Sounds bizarre, right? Yet, this is essentially how most of today’s wireless communication systems operate — with a sea of smartphones, laptops, and other gadgets all competing for the same crowded part of the spectrum, while other channels lay idle.

Thankfully, a team of researchers at the University at Buffalo (UB) is working on a solution to this digital traffic jam. Their focus: cognitive radio, a cutting-edge wireless communication technology that intelligently and instantly searches for, and uses, the best available alternative channels.

This game-changing technology still has some development ahead, but the potential benefits are enormous. Imagine wireless communication that’s 10 times faster — for example, taking just three minutes instead of 30 to download a movie.

“We’re not fully using the radio spectrum that’s allocated to wireless devices,” explains Dimitris Pados, PhD, an electrical engineering professor at UB. “The system we’re developing eliminates those inefficiencies, allowing the transfer of as much information as possible while minimizing cross-interference.”

Pados is leading a four-year, $2.72 million grant project sponsored by the U.S. Air Force Research Laboratory. Joining him are fellow UB faculty members Professor Stella Batalama and associate professors Tommaso Melodia and Weifeng Su.

From smartphones and laptops to sensors monitoring crops, a growing number of wireless devices are jostling for space in the radio spectrum. This congestion means slow data transfer, wasted energy, dropped calls, and people being kicked offline. But the real issue isn’t a lack of spectrum; it’s how that spectrum is used.

Much of the spectrum reserved for wireless devices often sits unused, even as other sections overflow. For instance, AM radio frequencies are far less used in the U.S. compared to FM. Cognitive radio aims to automatically detect these empty channels across the spectrum and put them to work transferring data.

“It’s like an air traffic control system where the maximum amount of planes are taking off and landing at each airport throughout the world,” Pados says.

UB’s team will develop software algorithms to optimize, model, and simulate how this new platform works, and, in collaboration with ANDRO Computational Solutions (based in Rome, N.Y.), they’ll conduct real-world tests using small unmanned aerial vehicles. Funding from the grant will support four graduate research assistants and four undergraduate researchers.

The project brings together experts from UB’s Signals, Communications and Networking Research Group, part of the Department of Electrical Engineering in the School of Engineering and Applied Sciences. The team includes professors Adly T. Fam and Mehrdad Soumekh; associate professors Michael Langberg and Leslie Ying; and assistant professors Nicholas Mastronarde, Gesualdo Scutari, Zhi Sun, and Josep M. Jornet.

Their research topics range from wireless communications and cognitive radios to big data, machine learning, data security, and communications in extreme environments like underwater or underground. With innovations like cognitive radio, they’re working to reshape how wireless communication will serve our increasingly connected world.

*Release Date: May 5, 2014*

"This isn't what you promised me." I stared at my new hands and shook my head. "You said I'd have super strength. You said I'd be able to do things I'd only dreamed of. That I'd be superhuman." I glanced over at the doctor, his metal body gleaming under the light—he had the latest robot model. The same one I was supposed to have now.

The doctor paced nervously, monitoring the machines I was attached to. "I'm sorry," he said quietly. "You didn't adapt to the new body. We almost lost you. We had to improvise, and this was the only option we had." He refused to meet my eyes. No surprise there.

I slid off the bed and shuffled to the window, my body slow to respond. If things had gone as planned, I'd be outside right now. Instead, I faced a long recovery. "How long will I have to stay like this? Is another transfer possible?"

He hesitated, mouth opening and closing, suddenly fixated on the chart in his hands. "Doctor!" I slammed my fist on the windowsill. He flinched, then stood still.

"Robot bodies are complicated," he said finally. "Sometimes things just don’t work out. Right now, we can't transfer you again. Maybe in the future—technology changes fast. For now, though, you'll need to live in this body."

He guided me to the wall mirror, his metal hands firm but gentle on my shoulders. "You're human again. There’s nothing wrong with that. We all started out this way."

I stared at my reflection. The body looked to be in its early twenties, muscles shifting as I flexed. Human again.

Ernest Goult, 72, was found guilty of a racially aggravated public order offence following Middlesbrough and Blackburn Rovers' game in 2014.
However, he claimed the gesture meant "the pits" or "smelly".
An appeal panel at Teesside Crown Court said they could not be sure the gesture was racist.
Blackburn players Lee Williamson and Markus Olsson and Aston Villa striker Rudy Gestede, who was at the club at the time, gave evidence against Mr Goult.
Mr Williamson said he noticed the "pathetic" 72-year-old as he was walking over to the Blackburn supporters, who were seated in the away section of the Teesside club's Riverside Stadium, while Mr Gestede said he was "shocked" to see the gesture.
Mr Olsson said the French forward had got angry as a result.
Representing Mr Goult, Giles Grant said the one-armed gesture was derogatory but meant "the pits" or "smelly", adding that a monkey gesture would usually be accompanied by facial expressions and an "oo-oo" noise.
Giving evidence, Mr Goult denied he held "negative or poor views" of the footballers because their race.
The retired steel worker said the gesture was used in that industry to express displeasure, often in a noisy environment, a claim which was confirmed by another former worker.
Judge Simon Bourne-Arton, who heard the appeal alongside two magistrates, said the players genuinely felt it was a racist gesture, but the appeal panel could not be sure.
He added that while it had been proved that Mr Goult caused alarm and distress, he had not made the typical "utterly objectionable" monkey gesture, where both arms were used along with the "whooping sound of a baboon".

One year ago today. One year since I saw Mr. J take out those corporate tools I worked with at the bank. When he wiped the smug smile off their faces and replaced it with a much...wider one. One year since I joined his organization. Most of the assholes who followed him had stupid ideas of what his big plan was. The skinheads talked about how he was going to cleanse the nation of "subhumans," the druggies thought he was going to tear down the government, but one by one I'd seen all of those assholes die, either in one of Mr. J's plans or just for opening their mouths at the wrong time. I knew better, I knew what he was planning all along and I kept my mouth shut and did what I was supposed to do. I watched the idiots try to fit his plans into their own narratives, until one by one they all died and here I was, watching from a few steps back as Mr. J surveyed the wreckage of Wayne Manor burning below us. I had been watching with mounting glee as yet another corporate tool burned in the pyre of his own opulence. Things like this had been so much easier since the Bat had disappeared last month. The rumors were that he'd finally been killed, but I didn't buy it. I figured whatever corporation that had been funding him to keep the status quo had just decided he wasn't worth the money anymore.

"You've been here quite a while, huh?" Oh shit, he's talking to me! Mr. J doesn't talk to any of us and he's actually talking to me!

"U-uh, yes sir Mr. J. Been a year about now."

"You know who's burning down there?"

"That young corporate guy who had that stroke right? Newspaper said he fell over at a dinner someplace fancy." Crap, I sound like a dumb thug from a comic book. Everyone knows him, he's been the subject of every news report for the last few weeks with the media falling over themselves to "mourn" another trust fund baby even though he was still alive.

"...You know I don't think I've ever had someone last a year with me. I think a performance review might be in order." Turning quickly he threw me to the ground before pouncing on me and pressing his scalpel to my cheek. "Now then, just one small question. A simple question for a simple man. What. Am. I. Doing?"

Terrified, I could only stammer a weak and stupid "What?"

"You've been, as it were, here for it all. You've seen all my plans. What am I doing it all for? Review my performance for me here above this viking funeral." The blade of the scalpel seemed to caress my skin as he said this.

"You-you're eliminating corporate greed, you're gonna show those fat cats running everything that us little guys can—AARRGH!" The blade turns from caress to a line of fire from one side of my mouth to my jaw. In a whisper he says, "Try again."

"But, that's what you've been doing right?" I struggle to talk, my face is on fire and my mouth keeps filling with blood. "Why else would you destroy everything, nothing else makes sense!" The other side of my face explodes in pain and through it, I scream.

"Closer that time, closer. You'll have plenty of time to think about it as those heal. Now if you'll excuse me..."

And with that he threw himself over me and down into the fiery blaze below us. I can still hear him laughing as he fell into the waiting flames. It keeps me up at night.

**The Laundry Incident**

Maya froze in the doorway, her ice-blue suit dripping wet in her hands. There, standing at the washing machine with a distinctive black and silver cape, was her roommate Jake.

"Oh," Jake said, his voice small. "Hi."

"Hi," Maya squeaked back.

They stared at each other for a long moment. Maya's mind raced through the past six months of living together—Jake's mysterious late nights, his collection of "costume jewelry" that looked suspiciously like stolen gems, the way he always had exact change and never seemed to have a job.

"So," Jake cleared his throat, gesturing at her suit. "Frostbite, I presume?"

"And you're..." Maya's eyes traveled to a familiar silver mask peeking out of the washing machine. "Oh god, you're Shadowstrike. I've been trying to arrest you for months!"

"Arrest me? You froze my escape route last Tuesday! I nearly got caught by actual cops!"

"That's the point!"

They glared at each other across the small laundry room, hero and villain, roommate and roommate. The washing machine churned peacefully between them.

"This is so awkward," Jake muttered.

"Did you eat my leftover Thai food last week?" Maya demanded suddenly.

"What? We're having an identity crisis and you're worried about pad thai?"

"Answer the question, Shadowstrike."

"Yes, fine! I was stressed about the museum heist and I stress-eat, okay?"

Maya's shoulders sagged. "I knew it. I blamed Mrs. Henderson from 4B."

Another long silence.

"So," Jake ventured, "what happens now? Do we fight? Do you turn me in? Do I have to find a new apartment? Because the rental market is brutal right now."

Maya considered this, looking at her nemesis—who remembered her coffee order, who always saved her the good Netflix password, who had covered her half of rent when she'd been injured in costume and couldn't work her day job.

"I mean," she said slowly, "you do keep the apartment pretty clean for a villain."

"I'm very organized. Crime doesn't pay if you don't keep good records."

"And you did help me assemble that IKEA bookshelf..."

"Which you froze in frustration and I had to thaw with a hair dryer."

They looked at each other again, and Maya felt some of the tension ease.

"New rules," she said finally. "No bringing work home. No stolen goods in common areas. And replace my food when you stress-eat it."

Jake blinked. "That's it?"

"That's it. But I'm still going to try to arrest you."

"And I'm still going to escape dramatically and probably insult your costume choices."

"My costume is iconic!"

"It squeaks when you walk."

Maya threw a dryer sheet at him, which he dodged with supervillain reflexes.

"Roommate truce?" Jake offered, extending his hand.

"Roommate truce," Maya agreed, shaking it. "But I'm putting a lock on my Thai food."

As they sorted their respective costumes into separate loads—even nemeses shouldn't mix colors with darks—Maya couldn't help but think that this might actually make things easier. At least now she knew why he was always hogging the bathroom mirror.

A fancy restaurant owned by famous chef Raymond Blanc has been banned from serving a liver dish that made two customers very sick.


The restaurant, called Blanc Brasserie in London, was serving lamb's liver that wasn't cooked enough. Two people who ate it got food poisoning on different days this summer.


City health officers warned the restaurant to stop serving the dish because it was dangerous. But the restaurant didn't listen to the warning.


A court decided the restaurant was right to be banned from serving this dish. The liver wasn't cooked properly and could make people very sick.


The food safety rules say lamb's liver must be cooked at a very high temperature for at least two minutes. The restaurant wasn't doing this.


Raymond Blanc is a well-known French chef who owns 18 restaurants in Britain. His main restaurant has won top awards for its food.


After this happened, all 18 of his restaurants stopped serving liver dishes. The company said if they cooked the liver the safe way, it would be too overcooked and customers wouldn't want to eat it.


The restaurant had to pay £3,103 in court costs.


The type of food poisoning the customers got is called campylobacter. It's very common - hundreds of thousands of people get it each year in England and Wales. Some people have to go to the hospital, and some people even die from it.

High school was really tough for me. I usually try to forget the bad memories of being bullied, but there's one thing I can't forget. My friend - my only friend - killed himself. I can still hear the teachers screaming when they found his body. I knew other kids picked on him, but I didn't realize how much pain he was in.


When I got to college, I had almost forgotten about that terrible day. Then I walked into my dorm room and froze. There was someone who looked EXACTLY like my dead friend. Same messy hair, same thick glasses. Someone must be playing a sick joke on me - they really did their research.


I said, "Hey, so we're roommates now?"


He answered, "Yeah, didn't expect to see you here. High school was pretty rough, right? But we made it through."


I gave him a fist bump, but it felt really creepy. The only thing that makes sense is that someone is pranking me because I'm new. But how could anyone know about my friend on my very first day? I made sure to go to a different college than everyone from my old school so I could start fresh.


I'm going to figure out what's really going on here.

By . Steve Robson . PUBLISHED: . 19:56 EST, 17 April 2013 . | . UPDATED: . 06:45 EST, 18 April 2013 . Twenty-five German factory workers have been hospitalised after eating sandwiches laced with rat poison. So far none of the victims has shown symptoms of illness but they are being kept under observation as a precautionary measure. A box of filled sandwiches were left on the doorstep of Mueller-Technik, a car parts company in the north-western town of Steinfeld, Lower Saxony, on Tuesday morning. Managing director of Mueller-Technik, Helmut Kohak gives a press conference in front of the company's office in Steinfeld, Germany . Employees helped themselves to the sandwiches, filled with cheese and sausage, before one of the workers noticed a strange substance on them. Emergency services were called and anyone who had eaten a sandwich was taken to hospital. A Berlin laboratory confirmed overnight that the food had been sprinkled with rat poison. Police spokesman Frank Soika said the employees had not thought the gesture unusual as it was apparently quite common for workers to buy sandwiches for each other. 'We don't believe the amount of poison used would have been deadly, but it could have caused serious illness,' he said. Police have interviewed some of the affected workers, but as yet have no indication of who left the sandwiches or why. Mystery: Police have interviewed some of the factory workers but it is not yet known who left the poisoned sandwiches or why (file image) Helmut Kohake, managing director of Mueller-Technik, told a press conference yesterday there had been no problems with staff, and no one had been made redundant. He added that his employees were upset by the incident: 'You don't know whether something like this will happen again.' Mueller-Technik, founded in 1984, makes plastic parts for use in cars, and had around 240 employees.

New research is taking an early look at Alzheimer's disease and the effects it may have on a baby's brain.
Researchers have identified the gene variant APOE ε4 and its link to late-onset Alzheimer's disease. According to health experts, about 25 percent of Americans have the APOE ε4 variant. Not all of them will develop Alzheimer's disease, but of those who do, about 60 percent will carry at least one copy of the gene variant.
Researchers from Brown University and Banner Alzheimer’s Institute recently evaluated infants both for the presence of the APOE ε4 variant and any effects it may have on a baby's brain development. The study, published in JAMA Neurology, showed that infants who carry the gene show altered brain development compared to babies who do not carry the gene.
The researchers imaged the brains of 162 babies between the ages of 2 months and 25 months to determine which babies, if any, carried a variant of the APOE gene. They found that 60 of the infants had the ε4 variant that has been linked to an increased risk of late-onset Alzheimer’s. Researchers then compared the brains of babies with the ε4 variant with those who did not carry the gene. They found that children who carry the APOE ε4 variant had increased brain growth in areas in the frontal lobe and decreased growth in the middle and rear of the brain — areas of the brain that tend to be affected in elderly patients who have Alzheimer’s disease.
“It may sound scary that we could detect these brain differences in infants,” said Dr. Eric Reiman, executive director of the Banner Alzheimer’s Institute in Arizona and lead researcher of the study. “But it is our sincere hope that an understanding of the earliest brain changes involved in the predisposition to Alzheimer’s will help researchers find treatments to prevent the clinical onset of Alzheimer’s disease — and do so long before these children become senior citizens.”
Source: Bioscience Technology

**Series of ATM Thefts Hits Glasgow and Surrounding Areas**

*April 23, 2024 – Glasgow, Scotland*

Police are investigating a spate of thefts targeting ATM users across Glasgow and nearby areas on April 21 and 22. Authorities report that suspects approached individuals as they withdrew cash, using distraction tactics or physically pushing users aside to steal their money.

Several incidents were reported between Sunday and Monday at ATMs in central Glasgow as well as adjacent neighborhoods. Victims described suspects engaging them in conversation, asking for assistance, or abruptly shoving them away just as cash was dispensed from the machines.

Police Scotland has confirmed heightened patrols and has urged the public to remain vigilant when using ATMs. “We are taking these incidents extremely seriously and are reviewing CCTV footage from affected locations,” a police spokesperson said. Officers are appealing for witnesses and urging anyone with information or who may have seen suspicious behavior near cash machines to come forward.

A 67-year-old woman, one of the victims, said, “I was just about to take my cash when someone bumped into me and grabbed it before running off. It happened so fast—that’s my pension money gone.”

Community leaders and law enforcement are encouraging locals to shield their PINs, avoid counting cash at machines, and, when possible, use indoor ATMs or attend in pairs.

Anyone with information is asked to contact Police Scotland on 101 quoting the relevant incident, or to make a report anonymously via Crimestoppers.

The investigation is ongoing.

The alleged terror incident aboard a passenger flight from Amsterdam to Detroit has raised questions as to how a  Nigerian man carried explosives through stringent security measures. Umar Farouk Abdulmutallab has been charged with attempting to destroy a passenger plane after he detonated a device on board a jet on Friday. Authorities in the United States are investigating whether Abdulmutallab had any connections with terrorist organizations or was acting alone. Airports intensify security after plane attack . With Dutch officials scrutinizing security procedures at Amsterdam's Schiphol airport that allowed the 23-year-old man to smuggle the explosives on the aircraft -- here are some of the key questions still hanging over the incident. Where did the explosives come from? The man charged with igniting the device claims he obtained the explosives in Yemen, along with instruction on how to use it. He carried these onto a flight from Lagos, Nigeria, to Amsterdam, Netherlands where he transferred to Northwest Flight 253 to Detroit. Was he on any security watch lists? U.S. authorities say they were contacted by the man's father ahead of the attack, but whether he was placed on any so-called No-Fly list maintained by the United States is unclear. Dutch officials say the passenger's name appeared on a manifest supplied by the airline, which was passed to U.S. authorities, who cleared the flight to depart. Were the proper security checks performed? Yes, say Dutch authorities, who say he passed through normal procedures and that "security was well-performed." He is known to have passed through a metal detector and his luggage was X-rayed. Extra attention is normally applied to passengers arriving from Nigeria because of concerns over fraud and smuggling. Nevertheless airports around the world have stepped up security procedures in the wake of the incident, increasing pat-downs and secondary searches. So how did the explosives get through? Dutch authorities are at pains to point out that if the passenger had powders concealed upon his person or secreted bottles of liquid somewhere else, they certainly would not have been picked up by the metal detector. Is there any way of detecting these kind of explosives? A preliminary FBI analysis indicates the device contained PETN, also known as Pentaerythritol Tetranitrate, a highly explosive chemical. Experts say this would have been picked up using a swab commonly used in secondary screening. A body scan, particularly the new 3D imaging scanners being trailed at some airports, would also have spotted something strapped to his body, even in the crotch area. Why were there no U.S. air marshal security officers on the flight? U.S. Homeland Security Secretary Janet Napolitano told CNN federal air marshals are not posted on all flights. She said this was not due to budgetary constraints, but down to standard procedures, which see marshals posted randomly on certain routes. CNN's Jeanne Meserve, Richard Quest and Barry Neild contributed to this report.

So I wanted to tell you about this really cool project that’s been gaining a lot of attention lately. It’s called the Little Urban Garden Initiative, and it’s making waves in cities across the country. The basic idea is simple: transform underused urban spaces into thriving pockets of greenery, where communities can grow their own food, learn about sustainability, and connect with their neighbors.

What makes this initiative stand out is how inclusive and accessible it is. Anyone, regardless of gardening experience, can get involved. The organizers provide soil, seeds, and even workshops on composting and organic gardening. Volunteers from all walks of life come together on weekends, turning grey patches of land into vibrant gardens bursting with vegetables, herbs, and flowers.

But it’s not just about the plants. The Little Urban Garden Initiative is also about building stronger, healthier communities. The gardens have become gathering spots, hosting everything from yoga classes to potluck dinners. There’s this awesome sense of pride when people harvest the food they’ve grown together—meals taste a little better when you’ve had a hand in growing the ingredients!

Another cool aspect is the ecological benefit. Bees and butterflies that had all but disappeared from these neighborhoods are returning, thanks to pollinator-friendly plant choices. Rainwater collection systems help reduce runoff, and compost heaps cut down on waste.

If you’re interested in getting involved, it’s surprisingly easy. Many cities now have similar programs, or you can start your own with just a few friends and a shared vision. Even a small balcony or rooftop can become a tiny oasis.

So, next time you see an empty lot or a forgotten corner of your neighborhood, imagine what it could be. With a few seeds, a bit of effort, and some community spirit, it might just turn into something really cool.

The Syrian Observatory for Human Rights said that the Ahrar al-Sham, Jund al-Aqsa and Nusra Front groups had taken the city on Saturday.
Idlib is only the second provincial centre to fall into rebel hands, after Raqqa was seized by Islamic State (IS).
Syria's civil war, which began four years ago, has killed more than 200,000 Syrians and displaced 11 million.
The UK-based observatory said the militant groups seized the city after four days of intense fighting.
Syria has not confirmed the loss.
State television said: "The army is fighting fierce battles to restore the situation back to what it was."
The capture of the city, with a population of 100,000, would be a serious blow to the government of President Bashar al-Assad, correspondents say.
Idlib has been the subject of severe fighting between the government and opposition forces for months
Idlib is close to the strategically important main highway linking the capital Damascus to the commercial capital Aleppo. It is also close to the coastal province of Latakia, a stronghold of President Assad.
Supporters of Ahrar al-Sham and the Nusra Front have posted pictures of fighters celebrating in the city.
It is the second defeat of government forces in the space of a week after rebels in southern Syria seized the historic city of Busra in Deraa province.
On Saturday, UN Secretary General Ban Ki-moon said he was angry and shamed by the failure to halt the Syrian civil war.
Speaking at an Arab League summit in Egypt, he promised to step up diplomatic efforts to stop the fighting.

**Angelina Jolie and Amy Pascal Embroiled in Sony Hack Fallout Amidst Hollywood Power Struggle**

LOS ANGELES - The 2014 Sony Pictures hack continues to send shockwaves through Hollywood as leaked emails reveal tensions between A-list actress Angelina Jolie and former Sony Pictures Entertainment co-chair Amy Pascal.

Internal communications exposed in the cyberattack show heated exchanges regarding creative differences on multiple projects, with sources close to the situation describing a clash between Jolie's vision as both star and producer against Pascal's executive priorities.

The leaked correspondence reveals disagreements over budget allocations and casting decisions for several high-profile films, including disputes about Jolie's directorial projects and her involvement in major studio tentpole releases.

Pascal, who stepped down from her Sony position in February 2015 following the hack's revelations, had previously worked with Jolie on multiple occasions. Industry insiders suggest the professional relationship soured over conflicting approaches to storytelling and market positioning.

"This represents a larger conversation about creative control versus corporate interests in modern Hollywood," said entertainment industry analyst Sarah Chen. "The hack pulled back the curtain on how these power dynamics really work behind closed doors."

Representatives for both Jolie and Pascal declined to comment on the specific nature of their professional disagreements. Sony Pictures has maintained its position that private communications should not define business relationships or future collaborations.

The fallout continues to influence project development decisions across major studios as executives navigate the new reality of potential digital exposure.

Hey, let's talk about something we've all been there with - that moment when you're scrolling through Facebook and thinking, "Why am I friends with half these people?" You know what I mean - between the TMI posts, the political rants, and those photos that make you go "yikes," it might be time for a little spring cleaning of your friend list!

Dr. Andrea Bonior, who wrote "The Friendship Fix," has some great advice: just ask yourself why you're really friends with these people online. Are you genuinely interested in staying connected, or is it just because they sent a request?

Let me walk you through some common scenarios we all face:

**Your kid's teacher**
I get it - you chatted at the school carnival and she seems really cool. But here's the thing: mixing your social media life with your child's education can get messy fast. What if there's a problem at school later? Your teacher might feel weird about being objective, or you might worry she's reading too much into your posts. Probably best to keep that relationship in the classroom.

**The babysitter**
She's awesome with your kids and lives right down the street - seems like a natural friendship, right? Well, it's totally fine to check out someone's social media before you hire them (lots of employers do this), but once she's working for you, you might want to pass on that friend request. Trust me, you don't want to see a post that makes you question your childcare choice!

**Other parents from school**
This one's a maybe! It can be great for staying connected and keeping up with what's happening with your kids' friends. Just be picky about it - stick to the moms you actually click with in real life. You don't need to be friends with every single parent, and honestly, your posts might not be everyone's cup of tea anyway.

**Your pediatrician**
Okay, I know you see her all the time between checkups, sick visits, and those endless ear infections. But think about it - you're already sharing plenty during those appointments! Facebook really isn't the place for medical stuff, and unless you're genuinely hanging out socially, it's probably better to keep that relationship professional.

**An ex-boyfriend**
Oh boy, this is a big one! Sure, he was fun back in high school, and maybe you're curious about what he's up to now. But if either of you is married or in a serious relationship, this could spell trouble. Be really honest with yourself about why you want to reconnect - sometimes we have hidden motives we don't want to admit.

The good news is that Facebook has been updating their privacy tools to make things easier:
- There's a new shortcut to privacy settings
- You can ask people to remove photos of you that you didn't approve
- You'll get alerts if something you tried to hide might still show up in feeds
- Apps have to ask permission twice before accessing your info

The bottom line? Your Facebook feed should make you happy, not stressed. Don't be afraid to be selective about who gets a window into your life!

'Tripp has bacterial meningitis, which is very life threatening. Words can’t describe what we are feeling except total heartbreak.' Fighting for his life: Both sides of two-year-old Tripp Halstead's brain were injured when a large branch was blown off a tree and hit him in the head . When he was rushed into Children’s . Healthcare of Atlanta on the day of the incident, he had emergency . surgery to relive swelling around his brain from where the ten-foot tree . branch struck him on the head. Tripp’s favorite toy, Lightning McQueen, is in his hand as he remains unconscious. A family friend set up a Facebook for Tripp's parents, Bill and Stacy Halstead, who have been posting updates on their son's condition and asking people to pray and donate money for his recovery. The family said they have been overwhelmed with support from thousands of well-wishers, many of them strangers who have heard about Tripp’s plight from media reports, according to Atlanta Journal Constitution. After the accident, the toddler's father, Bill Halstead, wrote that doctors gave them sobering news. 'His injury is across both sides of the brain which makes it much worse. It is still far too early to know what the outcome will be or what the lasting damage could be,' the message read. Bubbly: Tripp was playing outside with his classmates when the branch came from nowhere and struck him down . Gratitude: Tripp's parents Bill and Stacy Halstead (pictured) have thanked people for their continued support in their time of need . Scene: The accident happened while Tripp was enjoying his recess with friends at the Cribs To Crayons daycare center . 'His swelling is back up and his drainage tube is having problems,' the post said in part. 'Sounds like today is going to be the most trying day so far so please continue to pray. We still need a miracle.' The boy's mother, Stacy Halstead, called her son a miracle baby, explaining to WSB-TV that she and her husband had struggled for a long time to conceive until finally she became pregnant with Tripp, their one and only child. On October 29, tragedy struck when the toddler was playing at around 11.30am outside the Cribs and Crayons day care center, CBS Atlanta reported. A strong gust of wind caused a tree limb to fall, hitting the little boy in the neck and head. Employees from the center rushed to his aid and administered CPR, but the damage had been done. Miracle baby: Tripp's parents Bill and Stacy said they struggled for some time before having their only son . Heavy: A strong gust of wind ripped the branch from the tree before it landed on Tripp . Since the accident, the boy's family have been on a roller coaster ride, going from positive signs, like a brief reduction in pressure around his brain, to serious complications, among them problems with his breathing. On Friday afternoon, a desperate plea appeared on the family's Faceboook page asking for prayers. 'Please Please Please Pray for a miracle and if we can't have a miracle pray for the family to have the strength to cope with what lays ahead.' The wind alone was enough to bring a tear to the eye. The harsh, acidic smoke blanketed the entirety of the Earth now, the results of pumping sulphur and carbon into our atmosphere were plain for all to see. The human population was plummeting, crops were failing and animals were dying. Action from the government was vitally too late, the thick yellow clouds were choking the life out of the Earth. Billions of years of evolution and life had been forced out of existence in the space of what? Two hundred years? Humans were now living for their own greed because we no longer served a useful purpose on this Earth, but of course some may argue we never served any purpose on this planet. There are barely enough people left to argue we did. The lucky ones have escaped this world to one of the many 'goldilocks' planets that scatter our galaxy, perfectly happy to spend many billions on a space craft but not to turn their lights off when leaving the living room. The remaining humans are either killing themselves or killing each other for what resources remain; I intend to enjoy what we have left of our home. Well done humans, well done.

Upgrading the head unit in a Defender is one of the most transformative interior modifications you can make, instantly bringing this iconic vehicle into the 21st century. The standard audio setup in most Defenders is, to put it mildly, rudimentary. Swapping it out for a modern unit unlocks a world of convenience and enjoyment that significantly enhances the driving experience, whether you're tackling trails or cruising city streets.

The biggest game-changer is undoubtedly the integration of **Apple CarPlay and Android Auto**. These systems provide seamless access to navigation, music streaming, hands-free calls, and messaging directly on the head unit's screen. No more fumbling with phones; everything is intuitive and safe. For off-roading, having reliable, up-to-date mapping displayed clearly is invaluable.

**Sound quality** is another huge leap. Even with stock speakers, a new head unit's improved amplification and digital signal processing provide a richer, clearer audio experience. Pair it with upgraded speakers, and your Defender can finally have an audio system worthy of its legendary status.

Practical considerations for Defender owners include **physical controls**. While large touchscreens are appealing, having tactile buttons or a rotary knob for volume and track changes is incredibly useful when bouncing over rough terrain or wearing gloves. Look for units that offer a good balance. A **reverse camera input** is also a highly recommended feature, making parking and maneuvering in tight spots far safer and easier in a vehicle known for its rear blind spots. Screen visibility in bright sunlight is also important, so look for high-brightness displays.

For installation, many modern single-DIN units offer clever flip-out screens that provide a larger display without extensive dash modification. Double-DIN units, while requiring more cutting and custom work, offer the ultimate screen real estate and integrated look.

Ultimately, investing in a quality head unit for your Defender isn't just about better sound; it's about making your vehicle more functional, safer, and infinitely more enjoyable to drive. It's an upgrade that pays dividends every time you get behind the wheel.

Maddeningly inconsistent: this can either be the best slice in the neighborhood or a frustrating letdown. His terms were simple. No verification to obtain, no message to send. A quick bullet from a distance. Half upfront, half upon completion. The target was weak, and the singular object of the wrath of a man willing to pay any price to get rid of him. Reasons had washed away into the morass of time, so now only the seething, perfect hatred remained. But according to the man's station he could not himself act upon his hatred. Compelled by circumstance, the man sought out an assassin, a consummate professional in his field who he was assured could fulfill the task without hesitation. He presented his proposal to the assassin via payphone. He wired the down payment, and tried to put the whole matter out of his mind. The target sat in a corner pub as he was wont to do on muggy days. In front of him on a cafe table sat a frosty glass slowly coming to room temperature. He sat and watched as his younger compatriots laughed swapping their stories and quaffing their drinks. A twinge of envy brought his eyelids together as he remembered sitting in their seats before a million compromises brought him to where he sat now. At the same moment some hundred yards away the assassin drew his eyes together. His view was unimpaired, windage minimal. The perfect shot. The assassin exhaled and rested his finger against the trigger. A glare on the pub table drew the attention of the target to the middle distance and a reflection from a rooftop. As a boy he had served in the army, he knew what this meant. And he smiled, content in the knowledge he would never have to pay the other half.

**A Fascinating Journey Through Mathematical Infinity**

★★★★☆

Amir D. Aczel's "The Mystery of Aleph" takes readers on an captivating exploration of one of mathematics' most mind-bending concepts: infinity. The book masterfully weaves together the personal story of mathematician Georg Cantor with the profound mathematical discoveries that revolutionized our understanding of the infinite.

Aczel excels at making complex mathematical ideas accessible to general readers without oversimplifying them. His explanation of Cantor's groundbreaking work on set theory and different sizes of infinity is both clear and engaging. The historical context provided throughout the book adds depth, showing how Cantor's radical ideas were initially met with fierce resistance from the mathematical establishment.

The biographical elements are particularly compelling, revealing Cantor as a brilliant but troubled figure whose pursuit of mathematical truth came at great personal cost. Aczel sensitively portrays the mathematician's struggles with mental illness while never diminishing his extraordinary intellectual achievements.

While some sections delve quite deeply into mathematical proofs and may challenge readers without a strong mathematical background, the author generally maintains a good balance between rigor and accessibility. The book succeeds in conveying the beauty and mystery of infinity while honoring the complexity of the mathematics involved.

"The Mystery of Aleph" is an excellent choice for anyone interested in the intersection of mathematics, philosophy, and human drama. It's a thought-provoking read that will leave you contemplating the nature of infinity long after turning the final page.

## Introduction: Aspartame Back in the Spotlight

More than 20 years after its approval, aspartame, the artificial sweetener found in everything from soft drinks to toothpaste, is the center of renewed debate over its potential to cause cancer.

## The Study: Testing Aspartame on Rats

In an attempt to simulate a range of human intakes, researchers at the European Ramazzini Foundation of Oncology and Environmental Sciences fed 1,800 rats varying amounts of aspartame. Upon their deaths, the rats’ organs were examined for microscopic changes.

## Findings: Alarming Results

The results were not good. According to a study published in the March issue of Environmental Health Perspectives (the peer-reviewed journal of the National Institute of Environmental Health Sciences), the rats—especially females—showed a significant increase in leukemia and cancers in the kidney and urethra. The effects appeared in rats with a daily aspartame intake equivalent to 20 milligrams per kilogram body weight (mg/kg). That equates roughly to four or five bottles of diet soda for a 150-pound person. The acceptable daily limit is 50mg/kg in the United States and 40mg/kg in Europe.

## Industry Response and Ongoing Review

Commercially sold as Nutra-Sweet and Equal and the key ingredient in diet soft drinks, aspartame is a half-billion-dollar industry. Accordingly, the results of the study have been hotly disputed. Both the FDA and European Food Safety Authority are reviewing the study, but neither have cautioned against the sweetener.

—Adam Raymond

My name is Diamond Sparkles Kitten, and this is the story of the most amazing day of my life. One day I woke up in Hogwarts, lying in a hallway. My hair is white and green, and my eyes were grey because I was scared—my eyes can change color depending on how I feel! I was wearing a Hogwarts uniform, and I found a button on it that made me able to fly. But then it stopped working and I fell down. Luckily, Draco was walking by and found me. He looked at me and said, "Hey, what's a muggle like you doing here?" I thought he was so cute but kept it to myself. 

"I don't know how I got here. Can you help me?" I asked. Draco said, "Well, I don't like muggles much, but you're pretty cute. I'm Draco Malfoy." I was freaking out but just told him my name and didn't say that I knew who he was. Then he took me to Dumbledore, who looked at me and asked, "Why is that button there?" I pressed it and flew around. "Wow! You're a wizard! We should put you in a house!"

Later, Draco and I were in the Slytherin room together and started kissing. He wanted to go further, but I said, "What about me getting pregnant?" He replied, "It's fine, I know a special birth control spell." But he did it wrong, and suddenly I gave birth to NYAN CAT! And that's how Nyan Cat happened.

**Federal Court Upholds California's Landmark Shark Fin Ban, Bolstering Conservation Efforts**

A significant victory for marine conservationists was secured recently as a federal appeals court affirmed California's pioneering ban on the sale and possession of shark fins. The decision puts an end to a protracted legal challenge, solidifying the state's authority to regulate a trade widely condemned for its devastating impact on global shark populations.

California's ban, enacted in 2011 and fully implemented in 2013, prohibits the possession, sale, offer for sale, trade, or distribution of shark fins within the state. The legislation was driven by growing concerns over the practice of "finning"—where sharks are caught, their fins removed, and their often still-living bodies discarded back into the ocean—a brutal method contributing to the decline of numerous shark species. Conservation groups have long argued that the demand for shark fin soup, a traditional delicacy, fuels this unsustainable and cruel practice.

The ban faced immediate legal opposition from various industry groups, including Asian American business associations and seafood companies. These challengers argued that the state law was preempted by federal fishing regulations, specifically the Magnuson-Stevens Fishery Conservation and Management Act, and that it interfered with interstate and foreign commerce. They contended that federal law already regulates shark fishing and finning, making the state ban unnecessary and overreaching.

In its ruling, the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals rejected these arguments. The court found that California's ban does not conflict with or frustrate the purpose of federal fisheries law, which primarily focuses on regulating the *harvest* of sharks in federal waters. The court emphasized that federal law does not explicitly allow or protect the *sale* of fins from sharks legally caught, nor does it preempt state laws that aim to reduce the demand for fins. The judges concluded that California's law serves a legitimate state interest in protecting marine ecosystems and preventing cruelty, and that its impact on interstate commerce was not unduly burdensome given the state's strong conservation goals.

This federal court decision is a crucial win for shark conservation and sets a powerful precedent for other states considering similar bans. It affirms the right of states to enact their own regulations to address environmental and animal welfare concerns, even when federal laws exist on related topics. For conservation organizations, the ruling is a testament to the effectiveness of state-level initiatives in protecting endangered species and curbing harmful practices.

By upholding California's shark fin ban, the federal court has reinforced the state's commitment to marine protection and delivered a significant blow to the global shark fin trade. The ruling underscores the critical role that individual states can play in addressing worldwide environmental challenges, offering hope for the future of shark populations vital to ocean health.

While many cities developed haphazardly and organically with little structured planning, other cities were developed following strict organization–organization that reveals itself beautifully when seen from space.
Wired magazine shares a roundup of ten well-planned cities viewed with a satellite’s eye. Among the roundup our favorite is the oldest, seen in the photo above:
This nine-pointed fortress is perhaps the best example of a planned city from the Renaissance. Palmanova was built in 1593 and is located in the northeastern corner of Italy near the border with Slovenia.
It was intended to be home to a completely self-reliant utopian community that could also defend itself against the Ottomans. It had three guarded entrances, ramparts between each of the star points and eventually a moat. Sadly, nobody was willing to move there. Eventually it was used as free housing for pardoned criminals. Today it is a national monument, a tourist destination and home to around 5,000 people.
Hit up the link below to check out the other nine well-planned entries in the roundup.

The dominance of materialism in science in science, for centuries, has profoundly shaped not only our methodologies but also our very understanding of reality. This philosophical stance, which posits that only matter and energy exist and that all phenomena, including consciousness and thought, are ultimately reducible to physical interactions, has been undeniably instrumental in the spectacular advancements of modern empirical research. It forms the bedrock of disciplines like physics, chemistry, and biology, fostering a rigorous, testable, and replicable approach to exploring the universe. The emphasis on observable, measurable, and quantifiable data has led to groundbreaking discoveries and technological innovations that have transformed human civilization.

However, this pervasive influence, while undeniably powerful, is not without its critics or its inherent limitations. The insistence on purely material explanations, while immensely effective for understanding the physical world, often struggles to adequately address or even acknowledge phenomena that do not easily fit within its framework. Complex emergent properties, the subjective nature of consciousness, and the very quest for meaning can be reduced to mere epiphenomena or dismissed as outside the purview of scientific inquiry altogether. This reductionist tendency, a natural corollary of materialism, can sometimes overlook holistic interactions or the qualitative aspects of reality that defy simple quantitative measurement. The "in science in science" repetition itself might subtly underscore how deeply ingrained and self-referential this paradigm has become within the scientific community, perhaps even defining what counts as 'science.'

While fostering a robust framework for understanding the physical universe, an uncritical adherence to materialism can inadvertently create blind spots, limiting the scope of scientific inquiry and potentially excluding alternative explanatory models. It compels scientists to search exclusively for physical causes, even when other frameworks might offer valuable insights, thereby influencing the types of questions asked and the nature of the answers deemed acceptable. The ongoing debate about the mind-body problem or the origins of consciousness illustrates this tension perfectly, where purely material explanations often feel incomplete to many. Recognizing the powerful utility of methodological materialism, while remaining open to the potential limitations of an ontological materialism, may be a crucial step for science to continue its quest for a more complete understanding of the cosmos and our place within it.

This was the start of a new era for Dr Who - new music, new direction. This adventure's 'beginning of a new era' aspect is, to be honest, about all it has going for it. There is so much importance placed on style, nice-looking sets and clever camera shots that the whole thing never actually gets round to telling any kind of a story. The Doctor and his companions arrive in a leisure complex, the Doctor ages 500 years by mistake, the Doctor helps save everybody from a race of decidedly uninteresting lizard-people.True, this adventure has a freshness and energy about it, but that can't help disguise the frankly dull storyline.

I believe in a way of life based on the free exchange of ideas, in which books have and will continue to play a central role. Devices like this are trying to determine how people will interact with books, but Amazon's use of DRM to control and monitor users and their books constitutes a clear threat to the free exchange of ideas.I demand that Amazon remove all DRM, including any ability to control or access the user's library, from the Kindle.Amazon's assurances that it will refrain from the worst abuses of this power do not address the problem. Amazon should not have this power in the first place. Until they give it up they will be tempted to use it, or they could be forced to by governments or narrow private interests. Whatever Amazon's reasons for imposing this control may be, they are not as important as the public's freedom to use books without interference or supervision.

The tremor started subtly, a deep hum through the office floor, then the world buckled. Not a sway, but a violent, tearing lurch. A colossal roar ripped through the concrete canyon, drowning out the sharp shriek of shattering glass. Mark, mid-sentence, was flung from his chair, hitting the polished floor as the sky outside the panoramic window turned from pale morning blue to a maelstrom of black smoke and orange flame.

He scrambled, half-crawling, half-stumbling, towards the emergency stairwell, the building groaning like a dying beast around him. Debris rained from the ceiling, light fixtures sparking and dying. The air, once cool and conditioned, was now thick with dust, acrid and metallic. People screamed, a raw, primal sound that echoed the very structure’s agony. He pushed past a terrified woman, her face streaked with soot, her eyes wide with uncomprehending horror.

Down, down, the stairwell was a swirling vortex of humanity, a panicked flood. Each landing was a choked bottleneck. The building shook again, harder, and a section of the wall above them cracked, sending a shower of plaster and rebar onto the fleeing crowd. Mark felt a sharp pain in his shoulder as he was knocked against the railing, but kept moving, propelled by the sheer force of the bodies behind him.

Outside, the city was unrecognizable. Skyscrapers, once proud monuments, now leaned precariously, shedding their skins of glass and steel. A fiery mushroom cloud bloomed where the financial district had been. The street was a mangled tapestry of overturned cars, fallen lampposts, and gaping fissures that bled steam. He ran, lungs burning, taste of ash and adrenaline on his tongue. No destination, just *away* from the towering inferno, away from the collapsing concrete and the horrifying symphony of destruction. Every step was a gamble, every breath a fight against the choking air. He didn’t look back, only forward, towards the promise of any open space, a desperate, frantic sprint against the death of a city.

He sat and stared across the round kitchen table at the refrigerator. *Click*, the circular hum of its fan sputtered to life. His neck was growing tired, he hunched lower and lower into his chair, but his eyes were locked on the stainless steel box in front of him. "Paul?" His wife had wandered in from the living room. A look of puzzlement suddenly spread across her visage. "Honey, what are you doing?" she asked. Hesitantly she walked towards where her husband sat, she gently put her hand on the shoulder of his blue, satin pajamas. "It's in there,"he whimpered in a raspy, frightened voice,"I know the damned thing is in there." "What on earth are you talking about?" asked the wife. Paul's eyes barely moved. He was seemingly transfixed by the endless revolutions of the ceiling fan, reflected by the burnished metal door of the refrigerator. His nostrils flared slightly as he inhaled. "The...milk. The damned carton it...it..."he stammered."I touched it and I knew, I just knew..."he paused,"I knew that it would be the last milk I'd ever buy". The color drained from his wife's face, and it turned a pallid white. Her lips quivered, her eyes narrowed. She hissed her words in hoarse, vicious whisper: "You bought soy, didn't you?"

Ronan O'Hora's excellent recordings for the Royal Philharmonic's own label are uniformly fine, which makes it puzzling that none of the major record companies have recorded O'Hora since then. This recording of Tchaikovsky is typical of the excellence of these recordings. O'Hora plays with fine technique and beautiful sound. His interpretation of the Concerto is very much a middle of the road one, sort of in the Arthur Rubinstein mode. Nothing is over stressed, and tempos neither drag nor speed along. Special mention must be made of James Judd and the orchestra. Rarely has this Concerto received such a lush, propulsive accompaniment, and the sound engineering in the Concerto is absolutely fabulous. O'Hora's performances of 8 pieces from The Seasons are full bodied yet piquant. This is a treasurable disc.

"...is important to us, please stay on the line until an operator is..." 

"Hello, this is John at FutureWeather, how can I help you?" The voice coming over the phone was thickly accented, but understandable. 

"Yeah, my FutureWeather 9000 is on the fritz. It claims that the max temperature tomorrow is going to be 10,000 degrees."

"Are you sure the device is in Fahrenheit mode? Many people accidentally switch it to Celsius."

"...10,000 Celsius is also a bit off."

There was a brief discussion on the other side of the line. I only caught the word *targeting*. 

"What did you say your address was again?" I told him. He once again pulled away from the phone and I heard a string of numbers in the background. "What does it say now?"

"It's...lower. Still 400 though. Should I just send it in?"

On the other end of the line, the rocket scientist altered the trajectory slightly. By their projections, they were rapidly closing in on their targeted zone. Another phone rang.

"Hello, this is John at FutureWeather, how can I help you?"

"My first death, I regret the most. You know how they say that everyone who survives the fall wishes they hadn't made the jump? That it wasn't that bad after all. Well they were right. A tower block in Central London, a teenager who felt he was depressed and the world was against him, and a concrete floor below. As soon as I jumped, I regretted doing it. My problems with my family were solvable, my life wasn't that bad. That fall was clarity to me. And then I smashed into the ground. There is nothing after life. No heaven, no hell. No redemption. No 72 virgins. No blackness, no darkness. Just nothing. An absence of everything. The best way I've found to describe it is if you shut your eyes hard, and then roll them back as far into your head as you can. It's not black, its just...hard to explain. Waking up in that hospital was surreal. Surrounded by bright lights and figures in white, I did for a moment think that the Christian's were right, God, angels, all that, did exist. But my sight focused, it was just a hospital. I had been dead for 37 minutes, I found out later. Massive trauma to the head, ribs collapsed, internal organs ruptured. No way to survive falling thirty floors onto concrete. I can't remember how much blood I lost, but I am pretty sure it was most of it. Nobody could explain it, they just stared at me as I opened my eyes, as my bones began to knit back together. After a few days of tests, all inconclusive, they let me go. I had done the impossible, supposedly. But dying brought me back to the world. Gave me a renewed drive and focus. I joined the Royal Engineers, learnt and trained for a few years. Afghanistan, June 2012. Part of a convoy moving some heavy artillery, I was in the front Humvee, when we ran over an IED. People should be able to survive a massive metal spike through there chest. Somehow I did, after dying for 37 minutes again. I got a reputation then as being a lucky fucker. Got the nickname Dice, was never allowed to gamble with the guys again. At the time I didn't blame them. Nobody knew about my fall, and I wasn't going to share it; nobody needed to know. But I knew, and I suspected. I wanted proof. Cold hard evidence. I had no dependents, my parents had died a couple of years back. Drunk driver, underage, stolen car; threw myself into my work due to it. So I bit the bullet. And shot myself through the head. 9mm Parabellums are not friendly when you fire them through your mouth and brain. But I did it in private, while off duty, off base, away from everyone and everything. And I woke up. Again, 37 minutes of nothingness. My head was in one piece, though it was covered in blood and brain tissue. There was a vaguely circular bald patch on the top of my head, but that was all. I didn't die. Maybe I couldn't die. I feared that, more than anything. And I was right to. I didn't age, didn't grow fat. Diseases came and went, illnesses would hit and then go, and never return. I had no fear, because there was nothing to be afraid of. It grows lonely. I can't remember the last time I talked to another person. Years and years ago. Everyone is dead now. Nobody else was immune like me. Some went across the stars, before our sun went Supernova. The earth withered and died, and I was left behind. Alone, on this rock, floating through space. Unable to die. One day the planet will fall into the sun. I don't know what will happen then. Maybe I will float through space, immune to everything. I don't know. I just want to die."

The higher temperature of a surface is due to increased molecular velocity—molecules move more rapidly, and this greater speed constitutes higher temperature. Now, consider the effect on a surface: the normal pressure from the atmosphere is not just from the impact of molecules but also from the recoil as they rebound. When molecules hit a surface and bounce back, the inherent elasticity of both the surface and molecules drives them away, creating a recoil effect—similar to the "kick" of a gun when firing a cannonball. If the collision does not alter velocity, then half the molecular bombardment pressure comes from this recoil. From a heated surface, molecules rebound even faster, so the recoil (and thus the pressure) increases proportionally with temperature.

Therefore, this theory predicts that air pressure on surfaces at different temperatures should be unequal—a hotter surface should experience greater pressure. However, this contradicts everyday experience, which shows that air pressure is uniform against all surfaces of the same area, regardless of temperature or condition. At first glance, this appears to challenge the theory. Yet, further analysis clarifies that, in a dense atmosphere like ours, this outcome is exactly what we should expect.

To explain further, consider molecular collisions: remarkably, we can measure the average number of times a gas molecule collides with its neighbors each second, for any given gas and conditions. Given both molecular velocity and collision frequency, we can calculate the mean free path—the average distance a molecule travels between collisions. For individual molecules, this distance varies constantly, but for a large group under constant conditions, the average is well-defined. This concept helps illustrate how we can estimate these molecular magnitudes.

When I first saw this package, I thought the outside of these bones would be able to be broken down once my dogs began chewing them and their saliva helped to break them down.
