Proposed new European regulations to make agriculture "greener" will reduce food production, increase bureaucracy and could even harm the environment, a parliamentary report has warned.
The House of Commons Environment Committee warned that the European Commission plans - due for implementation in 2014 - are too inflexible and would impose a "one-size-fits-all" regime on farmers from Finland to Sicily.
Farmers could lose 30% of their direct payments under the Common Agricultural Policy (CAP) if they fail to comply with three new "greening" requirements. The report also cited speculation that this penalty could be doubled by unspecified additional punishments being considered by the Commission.
The cross-party committee backed the Commission's ambition of making the CAP greener, but said that local differences in climate, landscape and farming practices must be taken into account.
Committee chair Anne McIntosh said: "As they currently stand, the Commission's proposals to green the CAP would hurt UK farmers, consumers and our countryside. They will reduce food security by taking land out of production and are likely to impact badly on our environment.
"It's a nonsense to think that farmers from Finland to Sicily should be tied to the same narrow prescriptive rules. One-size-fits all regulation cannot work across the range of environments found in Europe.
"To enhance biodiversity and protect the environment farmers across the EU must be able manage their landscapes in ways tailored to local farming methods and ecological concerns. The Commission's approach will damage the natural environment and farming."
Under the proposals, farmers would be required to meet new EU-wide standards for crop diversity and retention of permanent pastureland as well as setting aside 7% of their land as uncultivated ecological focus areas (EFA)
The report warned that the diversity rule - requiring farmers to grow at least three different crops on their arable land - would be less beneficial to the environment than the crop rotation already routinely practised by most UK farmers.
A spokesman for the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs said: "We are working hard to negotiate a more environmentally-friendly CAP which works for the UK's farmers and uses taxpayers' money more effectively - allowing more production at less cost to the environment. We're glad the Commission is starting to change its position on greening, but we want a more flexible approach that would allow member states to green the CAP in a way that works best for them."

# Instrument of Undoing

The violin had belonged to her grandmother, then her mother, then sat silent in the attic for twenty-seven years until Clara found it again. The wood gleamed like honey in the dusty light, unmarked by time.

She drew the bow across the strings experimentally. The note that emerged was perfect, hauntingly clear—impossible for an instrument so long neglected.

That evening, as Clara played her first halting melody, she noticed something odd. The wilted roses on her kitchen table began to straighten, their brown edges fading to pink, then deep red. By the song's end, they were fresh as the day she'd bought them.

The next morning, the roses were dead again.

Clara experimented. She played for her neighbor's arthritic cat, and watched twenty years melt from its joints as it leaped onto her windowsill with kitten-like grace. She played for her own reflection, watching the lines around her eyes smooth away.

But everything returned by dawn. The magic was borrowed, not given.

She began to understand why her grandmother had put the violin away. Why her mother had never spoken of it. Each note was a promise that couldn't be kept, a gift that turned to ash.

Clara played one last song—for her grandmother's photograph, watching the sepia image bloom into color, the face growing young and vital and heartbreakingly alive. For a moment, she could swear those eyes looked back at her with infinite sadness.

When morning came, Clara wrapped the violin in its faded velvet cloth and carried it back to the attic. Some instruments weren't meant to heal.

They were meant to teach you the weight of letting go.

Manchester United sent scouts to watch Brazilian attacking midfielder Roberto Firmino at Hoffenheim this weekend. The 23-year-old, who has been lighting up the German league, is in high demand, with United joining a growing list of Premier League clubs chasing his signature. Everton, Liverpool and Arsenal have all considered bids for the talented Brazilian who reportedly has a bargain release clause of just £14.5million. Sportsmail tells you everything you need to know about the Hoffenheim star.

Roberto Firmino celebrates scoring a late winner for his Hoffenheim side against Eintracht Frankfurt. Firmino scored the winning goal for Brazil in just his second international game against Austria last year.

Think of the most skillful player you've ever watched weaving through defenders - that's Firmino. In the past two seasons, only superstars like Lionel Messi and Eden Hazard have completed more successful dribbles in Europe's top leagues. With all the flair and fancy footwork you'd expect from a Brazilian international, Firmino is an attacking midfielder who knows where the goal is.

He's not your typical goal machine - his 6 goals in 25 games this season proves that - but when chances come his way, he rarely wastes them. Last season's impressive 16 goals earned him his first Brazil call-up, and he made an instant impact on the international stage with a spectacular 25-yard thunderbolt into the top corner against Austria in just his second game.

The Brazilian's shot from 25 yards flew into the top corner to earn him a first international goal. The Brazilian shows off his excellent first touch as he keeps the ball away from two Stuttgart defenders.

But it's not his goalscoring that would excite Louis van Gaal most - it's his ability to create magic for teammates. Firmino loves threading the perfect pass, slipping balls through to strikers from his position just behind them. His numbers speak for themselves: 18 league assists since last season started. 

To put that in perspective, that's more than Manchester United's entire midfield from their match against Tottenham - Juan Mata, Ander Herrera, Marouane Fellaini, Daley Blind and Michael Carrick - have managed combined in the Premier League over the past two seasons.

Usually playing as the link between midfield and attack, Firmino can also torment defenders out wide on the left, where his pace and quick feet make him a nightmare to mark. 

With superstars like Neymar, Coutinho, Oscar and Willian competing for his position, Firmino has done brilliantly to break into the Brazil squad. The playmaker holds off a tackle as he embarks on one of his trademark runs in a recent game.

What makes him special is his ability to hurt teams in multiple ways. Whether he's gliding past defenders with the ball glued to his feet or making clever runs into dangerous areas without it, he's always a threat. Playing wide left, he can cut inside and unleash shots with his preferred right foot, or use his bag of tricks to leave full-backs dizzy.

The big question is whether he's quick enough to thrive as a creative force in the Premier League's intense atmosphere, but his performances in Germany have caught the eye of some serious judges, including Bayern Munich's sporting director Mathias Sammer.

Firmino's dribbling, goals and assists have made him one of the hottest properties in the Bundesliga.

'Firmino is a great player,' said Sammer this week, though he ruled out Bayern making a move. But with the player, his agent, and Hoffenheim all admitting he could leave this season, Premier League clubs have been put on red alert for one of German football's most exciting talents.

Looking for some alternatives to the standard work group format? Need to take a different approach to getting a problem resolved? Want to hold a meeting and no one's in the office? If any of these situations sound familiar, this course will provide you with some choices to meet your needs. In this course, you will explore alternatives to the standard one-facilitator group meeting or work session. You will examine the advantages and disadvantages of co-facilitating meetings and work groups. With that knowledge, learn to divide and coordinate who's going to do what. Gain the ability to establish boundaries for each of the facilitators and determine the most effective way to communicate with your client during this process. Another alternative you will explore is breakout groups. Breakout groups that are planned well and facilitated properly can be an effective, fast means of handling a large project or quickly gathering the information needed to make a decision. You will learn to use multitasking formats as well as how to structure groups that are easy to debrief when the full group reconvenes. Other options abound. What about experiential learning techniques, structured go-arounds, or computer-aided meetings? Finally, you will explore the benefits and pitfalls of chart writing. Communicating information in meetings is an essential factor to their success. Charts can help you clarify information, track ideas and progress, and be a message-conveyor of their own. As an added bonus, you will discover some surprising dos and don'ts and learn about using chart art.

**Man Who Missed Viral $1 Million Halfcourt Shot Reveals He Was Battling Rare Disease That Caused Five Organs to Fail**

By Jenna Martinez

PHILADELPHIA – The man whose missed halfcourt shot at a Sixers game went viral last winter—drawing both sympathy and a fair share of internet memes—has revealed that, at the time, he was privately enduring a devastating health battle that nearly claimed his life.

Derek Porter, 29, of Cherry Hill, New Jersey, stunned fans when he lobbed an errant basketball during the halftime contest on February 16, a shot that would have awarded him $1 million if it went in. The video quickly racked up millions of views, with sports commentators and late-night hosts seizing on his startled reaction.

Now, Porter says there was much more behind that moment than nerves or lack of practice. In a candid interview with The Philadelphia Dispatch published Friday, Porter disclosed he had been secretly battling a rare autoimmune disease, and just weeks before the game, five of his organs went into simultaneous failure.

“I was in the fight of my life, literally,” Porter told the outlet. “I spent all of January in the hospital. My kidneys, my liver, my lungs, my heart, and even my pancreas—all were shutting down. Doctors weren’t sure I was going to survive.”

Porter, a high school history teacher and lifelong Sixers fan, said the contest was arranged by friends to lift his spirits after his discharge from Jefferson Hospital. He accepted, hoping to “feel normal again,” but admits, “I could barely move. My muscles had atrophied. I was still full of toxins from the disease and wasn’t even supposed to be in a big crowd.”

He decided to go ahead despite his frailty, drawing on advice from his medical team. “No matter what happened, being on that court meant I was alive,” he said.

Porter’s story has taken a dramatic turn since February. After months of rehabilitation, his organs are gradually regaining function, and his prognosis has vastly improved. He says the support he received—from friends, doctors, and even sympathetic strangers—has helped him through monstrous challenges.

“The money would have been nice, but right now, just having my health back is the greatest prize,” Porter said. “I’m grateful just to be able to give life another shot.”

Throughout the history of calculating we’ve devised ways to add speed and accuracy while subtracting the drudgery.
Many solutions used body parts, notably fingers. A 19th century Chinese technique can count to 10 billion using just two hands! Other solutions were mechanical — both general-purpose tools for everyday calculations and specialized instruments for engineering, navigational, or other scientific and technical problems.
Renaissance Italian merchants used Pacioli’s finger-counting system to barter. His Summa also included the first published description of double-entry bookkeeping.View Artifact Detail
A Living Abacus
In the 1940s, Korean mathematician Sung Jin Pai applied the abacus principle to human hands. His chisanbop method replaced the abacus’s moveable beads with moving fingers.
Kids and grownups learned to raise and lower their fingers and thumbs off a tabletop, in various combinations, to represent any number from one to 99.

Read the following self introductions from two Icelanders who describe their unique perspectives on life in the land of fire and ice.

**Sigrid, 34, Marine Biologist from Reykjavik:**

"My name is Sigrid Eriksdottir, and I spend my days studying the rich marine ecosystems that surround our island nation. Growing up in Reykjavik, I was always drawn to the ocean that seemed to stretch endlessly beyond our colorful rooftops. The contrast between our volcanic landscape and the deep blue waters fascinated me from childhood. Now, as a researcher at the University of Iceland, I investigate how Arctic species adapt to our changing climate. What many visitors don't realize is that Iceland sits at the intersection of two major ocean currents, creating incredibly diverse underwater habitats. During the long winter months, when darkness dominates our days, I find solace in my laboratory work, analyzing samples and data. But when summer arrives with its endless daylight, I'm out on research vessels, diving in waters that remain surprisingly rich with life. Living here means embracing extremes – not just in weather, but in how we experience time itself. The midnight sun teaches you that productivity doesn't follow conventional schedules, and the polar nights remind you to find light within yourself."

**Björn, 41, Sheep Farmer from Akureyri:**

"I'm Björn Magnusson, a third-generation sheep farmer in the highlands near Akureyri, in northern Iceland. My great-grandfather established our farm in the 1920s, and we've been working the same land ever since, adapting to whatever nature throws at us. People often ask how we manage farming in such harsh conditions, but I tell them that Iceland has been supporting livestock for over a thousand years – you just have to understand the rhythm of the seasons. Our Icelandic sheep are incredibly hardy, descended from animals brought by the first Norse settlers. They roam freely across our mountainous pastures during summer, developing thick wool coats and foraging on native grasses and herbs. The annual sheep roundup in September is still a community effort, with farmers from across the region helping each other gather the flocks before winter. What I love most about this life is the deep connection to our ancestors' traditions while using modern sustainable practices. We face challenges – volcanic ash, sudden weather changes, economic pressures – but there's something profoundly satisfying about continuing this ancient way of life in one of the world's most dramatic landscapes."

The Riddler adjusted his glasses as the dice clattered across the table. "A twenty! I've solved your riddle, Dungeon Master Joker. The ancient runes reveal the hidden door."

Across the battered table, Poison Ivy leaned back, vines twining lazily through her hair. "Just get us inside, Eddie. My druid doesn't like caves."

"All in good time," drawled Two-Face, flipping his coin. "Heads, I banish the goblins. Tails, we negotiate—like civilized criminals."

Harley Quinn bounced in her seat, crayons poised over her character sheet. "My bard seduces the ogre with interpretive dance!"

Joker beamed behind his Dungeon Master's screen, giggling as he drew a wild new map. "Oho! The ogre is charmed, but—plot twist!—the orc king demands a joke-off or the party perishes!"

The dice tumbled, the laughter echoed, and, for a few rare hours in Gotham, villainy was all in good fun.

American Airlines has refunded a family's ticket for their daughter who died after a public outcry following their initial refusal to do so. Madison 'Scout' Cantrell, 9, died tragically from an asthma attack that led to cardiac arrest five months ago, sending her family into deep and sudden grief. The Cantrells,from St Charles, a suburb of Chicago, had been planning a trip to Bald Head, North Carolina this month, but decided to call it off. Scout's sister Katie, 13, said going to their traditional family vacation spot would be too emotionally painful. American Airlines refused to offer a refund for Scout's seat, transfer the ticket to someone else or issue a voucher. Scroll down for video . American Airlines originally refused to refund the ticket of Madison 'Scout' Cantrell, 9, who died in October after an asthma attack led to cardiac arrest . Felicity Cantrell, Scout's mother, applied for a refund and sent in a death certificate but was told that the documents did not 'meet exception requirements' The airline's website says that a travel vouches are given to family members of passengers who die before their flight. Felicity Cantrell, Scout's mother, said on Facebook that she had sent in the required documentation and death certificate, but posted a letter  from the company denying her request for a refund. The callous letter said 'the request does not meet our exception requirements' and that the fee for changing the flight would be waived 'as a one-time courtesy.' 'I know that I would never write this to someone. I don't know how someone could send this to another family,' Katie Cantrell told NBC Chicago. Katie Cantrell (right) said that going to her families normal vacation spot in North Carolina would be too emotionally painful without her sister Scout (left) Senior management from American Airlines got in contact with Felicity Cantrell on Wednesday night, and the family finally got their refund after a 'genuine' conversation . Social media users shared the mother's tragedy and American Airlines' corporate team got in touch with her, saying that she had gotten a refund when they actually hadn't. The customer service representatives that first contacted the family 'further exacerbated how American has treated us,' Mrs Cantrell said. On Wednesday night senior management from the company called and gave the Cantrells their refund. 'Were they doing corporate damage control? Yes, absolutely' Mrs Cantrell said. However, she added that her conversation with the manager was genuine and that he was 'thoroughly embarrassed and horrified by this whole situation'. The American Airlines representative also said that changes would be made in corporate training so  that employees will 'reflect empathy'. Mrs Cantrell posted on Facebook that the family would be vacationing in South Carolina this year. Social media users became outraged after hearing about the airline's callous response to the Cantrells. Pictured, Tony Cantrell and his daughters Scout (left) and Katie (right)

**Temporal Violations Case Files**

Agent Mara Kell adjusted the chrono-lock cuffs on her belt before stepping into the dimly pulsing light of Station X-9’s Violation Room. The temporal rifts hummed rhythmically behind reinforced glass, absently flickering with memories not quite her own.

Case 484. Unauthorized jump to 1912, Southampton. Passenger. Titanic. Mara thumbed through the holopad, scrolling past interviews, ship manifests, cross-temporary contamination charts. At the far end of the room sat their subject, Novi Tran: hair slicked back in Roaring Twenties fashion, though the rest of their outfit—a solar-weave jumpsuit—was stubbornly anachronistic.

“You know why you’re here,” Mara began.

Novi’s smirk was as relentless as ever. “I saved lives. Thirty-seven people, alive in my wake. Is that so wrong?”

Mara set the pad down, its pale blue projections casting shadows against the scars on the table’s surface. “You left anachronistic tech in a lifeboat. Transistorized emergency beacon. They found it in 1952, reversed-engineered half a century early. Cold War never happened.”

Novi stared at their hands as if still seeing the cold steel of Titanic’s rails. “If your job is to let people drown, I want no part of it.”

“Our job,” Mara said wearily, “is to keep time coherent. You don’t see the ripples. I do. At night. Every time.”

Novi raised their eyes, defiance flickering there. “So erase me. Unmake my choice.”

Mara hesitated. In the glass, she saw her own reflection waver—herself on decks, herself rowing. Could one moment undo it all?

She pressed the button. Chrono-locks snapped shut, humming with suppressed probability. Novi faded from existence, their impact smoothed, the ripple stilled. Mara’s memories adjusted—pain, regret, a sharper longing. But she held fast, flipping open the next file.

Case 485. Unauthorized jump to…

have you heard about what's happening with tomatoes lately? there's a major shortage hitting grocery stores across the Midwest as extreme weather conditions have devastated crops in key growing regions. Farmers in Ohio, Indiana, and Illinois report losing up to 60% of their tomato harvests due to an unprecedented combination of late spring frosts followed by record-breaking heat waves.

The shortage has caused prices to spike by nearly 40% in some areas, with a pound of fresh tomatoes now costing as much as $4.99 in certain supermarkets. Restaurant owners are struggling to adapt their menus, with some pizza chains temporarily switching to alternative sauce bases made from red peppers or reducing portion sizes.

Agricultural experts warn the situation could persist through the fall harvest season. Dr. Maria Rodriguez, a crop specialist at the University of Illinois, explains that the damaged plants are unlikely to recover sufficiently to produce normal yields this year.

Local food banks are particularly concerned about the impact on families who rely on tomatoes as an affordable source of vitamins and nutrients. The Midwest Food Alliance has launched an emergency campaign to source tomatoes from western states, though transportation costs are adding to the overall expense.

Weather forecasters predict more stable conditions ahead, offering hope that next year's growing season will return to normal production levels.

In 2014, President Barack Obama challenged America's mayors to help end a shameful symbol of the nation's broken promises to those who fought for the homeland but don't have a home to call their own after returning to civilian life. To that end, the Mayors Challenge to End Veteran Homelessness had a goal as lofty as its title. There was already some progress on that front beginning in 2010, thanks to the initiative of individual mayors who grew weary of waiting for the federal government to make it happen.
With a strategic plan generated by the White House and undergirded by a partnership between the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development and the Department of Veteran Affairs in place, ending veteran homelessness no longer looks like a utopian fantasy. That's why the Obama administration called upon the nation's mayors to be as committed to this effort as the White House and the two federal agencies tasked with making it a reality.
According to the latest report about this effort, the rate of homeless vets has dropped 47 percent in America since 2010. The shrinking population of homeless veterans accelerated downward once the White House, HUD and the VA made it a priority in 2014. Since then, more than 360,000 veterans, including their families, have directly benefited from HUD and VA programs that didn't exist in 2010. Across the country, there are less than 40,000 homeless veterans with 13,000 still on the street. While that is still an unacceptably high number, it is down considerably from six years ago. Two states and 27 communities have ended veteran homelessness. More states are committed to joining the effort.
Currently, New Orleans and Houston have bragging rights for being among the first major cities in the nation to have ended veteran homelessness. In these cities, homeless vets are placed in stable, sanitary and safe housing within 30 days. If other social services are needed from addiction counseling to mental health, then resources are provided by HUD, the VA and organizations at the local level. The goal is to get a roof over the head of every veteran so that whatever other help is needed, there is solid foundation for success. This is both cost-effective and enhances the dignity of the soldier early on.
Even while veterans are being spared the cruel indignities of the street, attention to the issue of general homelessness shouldn't be swept aside. What we're learning from programs like the Mayors Challenge to End Veteran Homelessness should be applied to other homeless populations as well. Other kinds of federal-local partnerships should be forged to bring those numbers down across the board.
— Pittsburgh Post-Gazette

# Simply Energy Proposes Higher Grid Fees for Solar Households Despite Clean Energy Benefits

Simply Energy has sparked controversy with a recent proposal to increase grid connection charges for Victorian households with solar panels, raising questions about the fairness of penalizing consumers who contribute to clean energy production.

## The Proposal Details

The energy retailer has submitted a proposal to Victorian energy regulators seeking to implement higher network charges for customers with rooftop solar systems. Under the proposed structure, solar households would face increased fees for maintaining their connection to the electricity grid, despite already investing in renewable energy infrastructure that benefits the broader power system.

The proposal comes at a time when Victoria has seen rapid adoption of residential solar installations, with over 600,000 homes now generating their own clean electricity. These systems not only reduce household energy bills but also feed excess power back into the grid, supporting the state's renewable energy transition.

## Industry Reaction

Clean energy advocates have criticized the proposal as counterproductive to Victoria's renewable energy goals. They argue that imposing additional charges on solar adopters sends the wrong signal to consumers considering clean energy investments and could slow the uptake of distributed solar generation.

"This proposal penalizes the very households that are helping to decarbonize our electricity system," said one industry spokesperson. "Solar households are already contributing to grid stability and reducing peak demand pressures."

## Simply Energy's Rationale

Simply Energy has defended its proposal by arguing that solar households still rely on grid infrastructure for backup power and to export excess generation. The company contends that all grid users should contribute fairly to network maintenance costs, regardless of whether they generate their own electricity.

The retailer suggests that current charging structures may not adequately reflect the costs of maintaining grid services for customers who both consume from and contribute to the network at different times.

## Broader Implications

The proposal reflects ongoing debates across Australia's energy sector about how to fairly allocate network costs as more households adopt distributed energy resources. Similar discussions have emerged in other states, with regulators grappling with evolving grid dynamics and changing consumption patterns.

Critics worry that increased charges could create barriers to solar adoption, particularly for lower-income households who may struggle with higher upfront costs and ongoing fees. This could potentially slow progress toward renewable energy targets and maintain reliance on fossil fuel generation.

## Regulatory Review Process

The proposal is currently under review by Victorian energy authorities, who will assess its impact on consumers, market competition, and the state's clean energy objectives. The review process includes opportunities for public consultation and stakeholder feedback before any final decisions are made.

Consumer groups and renewable energy organizations are expected to lodge strong objections during the consultation period, arguing that the proposal contradicts policy goals of encouraging distributed renewable energy adoption.

## Looking Forward

As Victoria continues its transition to renewable energy, the outcome of this proposal could set important precedents for how grid costs are allocated in an increasingly decentralized energy system. The decision will likely influence both consumer investment decisions and the pace of solar deployment across the state.

The debate highlights the need for innovative approaches to grid charging that recognize the benefits solar households provide while ensuring fair cost recovery for essential network infrastructure. Finding this balance will be crucial for maintaining public support for renewable energy adoption while funding necessary grid investments.

**KFC Review - 3/5 Stars**

KFC delivers on what it promises: crispy, seasoned fried chicken with that distinctive blend of 11 herbs and spices. The Original Recipe coating has a satisfying crunch and the chicken is generally juicy, though quality can be inconsistent depending on location and timing.

The sides are hit-or-miss. The mashed potatoes with gravy and coleslaw are decent comfort food staples, but the biscuits can be dry and the mac and cheese feels overly processed. The popcorn chicken and hot wings are solid alternatives to the traditional pieces.

Service tends to be fast, making it convenient for a quick meal, but the dining areas often feel dated and could use refreshing. Prices are reasonable for the portion sizes, though healthier options are limited.

KFC succeeds as an occasional indulgence when you're craving that specific flavor profile, but it's not somewhere you'd go for a memorable dining experience. It's reliable fast food that satisfies a particular craving without exceeding expectations.

A large part of my practice deals with kids needing to gain weight. This may include 2 year old sensitive to food textures to 18 year old whose energy for growth spurts and sport exceed their level of energy intake. Over the last few years I have also seen children who are dieting… to the point where they feel they cannot stop. This “disordered eating” can lead to an eating disorder. Children within normal weight parameters should be gaining weight each year through their school years and many even through their high school years depending on stages of development. So when I see weight loss or even weight plateau in children of normal weight I see a red flag.
This month Parenting Magazine (July 2009) wrote an excellent article on “Starving for acceptance, why younger children are dieting themselves sick-warning signs all moms need to know,” by Magaret Renkl. She states that the average age for onset of anorexia used to be ages 13 to 17 and now it is 9-12 years.
I not only recommend this article if you are concerned about a child losing weight but also recommend seeing your pediatrician and a pediatric dietitian to assess signs of trouble and your child’s nutrition status and risk.

By . Martha De Lacey . PUBLISHED: . 10:17 EST, 23 October 2012 . | . UPDATED: . 11:30 EST, 23 October 2012 . He is the secret agent who takes his martinis shaken, not stirred. And he would certainly be toasting the fact that his new fragrance has sold the equivalent of 1,000 full martini glasses in the first days of its launch at The Perfume Shop. James Bond 007 for men - which should perhaps have been called Double Eau Seven - is the official scent of the Bond franchise, and has been launched to mark Bond's 50th anniversary and the release of Sam Mendes' new Bond epic, Skyfall, starring Daniel Craig. Scroll down for video . The special Limited Gold Edition of the James Bond 007 anniversary perfume will go on sale on 1 November . Sales of the vetiver and lavender-scented cologne - which launched nationwide on 19 September after 500 limited edition bottles went on sale in Harrods in August - have exceeded all expectations in The Perfume Shop. When it launched there last week it outsold all new men's and women's scents, and the gift . box has already completely sold out. The studded, rectangular bottle is equipped with a top secret swivel nozzle (designed by Q, we can only assume), and is available as a 30ml and 50ml eau de toilette natural spray, priced at £19.50 and £24.50 . respectively. The 007 Gold Limited Edition comes in 50ml only and is priced . at £24.50. Daniel Craig, left, appears as 007 in the new James Bond film, Skyfall, directed by Sam Mendes, just in time to coincide with the release of the new James Bond 007 scent, right . **Cats Named Coco and Dogs Named Max Are Most Likely to Be Rehomed**


*By Steve Hopkins*


Battersea Dogs and Cats Home, which cared for around 9,000 animals last year, released a list of the five most common names among unwanted pets they have cared for.


The most common dog names are Max, Charlie, Buster, Bailey and Alfie, while the cat names are Coco, Tigger, Princess, Daisy and Frankie.


Battersea currently has four cats called Coco and two called Tigger, four dogs called Buster and two called Max.


Carly Whyborn, Battersea's head of operations, said: "These names are lovely but seem to be more common in our three centres in London, Brands Hatch and Old Windsor.


"Battersea cares for all these homeless dogs and cats, regardless of their name, and hopes animal lovers will give a second chance to all the unwanted Busters, Charlies and Cocos out there."


The shelter also revealed that the most common dog breed they took in was a Staffordshire Bull Terrier. The average age of animals arriving was one year and one month old for cats and nine months for dogs.

The thing that most people don't think about when it comes to abuse is the nausea. Yes, being punched in the face hurts, but it's the gut-wrenching twist of love and hate that follows, the stomach-churning cocktail of affection and cruelty, that truly gets to you. He swore he loved you. Promised he'd never do it again. For years, you clung to it, bound to your love, your monster—until the insidious whispers of 'normal' eroded everything you knew. A tender touch against your cheek feels no different from a brutal slap. A gentle laugh carries the same chilling weight as a furious yell. The boundaries dissolve, the lines between tenderness and terror blurring into an indistinguishable, sickening haze. You grasp at these twisted fragments, clinging to the fragile delusion that everything is still okay. That beneath the rage, he still loves you. That he still cares.

It was the sudden, raw cold that ripped through me, tearing sleep away. My blankets, carelessly tangled around my thighs, were abruptly yanked off and flung aside, leaving me exposed. I opened my eyes and saw my love staring at me, a beer clutched in his hand. His yelling was already a torrent before my mind could surface, before the brutal weight of reality slammed me into the day. My voice was a tight wire, my replies measured, a desperate stream of questions designed to decipher the latest invented slight. This time it was a Facebook friend. "You're fucking around aren't you?" he asked, now strangely calm. I opened my mouth to speak, but his arm was faster than my tongue. I ended up losing one of my front teeth to the bottle that night. The metallic tang of blood filled my mouth, and in that moment, the raw, searing pain of a missing piece of myself was a far greater, more tangible loss than the relationship I was finally losing.

The calls eventually stopped. The messages were deleted. I am finally, painstakingly rebuilding my life, stepping away from what I can now name as abuse—but still, sometimes, I feel the insidious, stomach-churning nausea.

**Star-studded field set for Birmingham Diamond League as Farah, Rutherford and Asher-Smith compete**

British athletics fans are in for a treat as three of the nation's biggest stars headline a world-class field for the Birmingham Diamond League meeting at Alexander Stadium next month.

Four-time Olympic champion Mo Farah will return to the track where he enjoyed so much success during the 2022 Commonwealth Games, competing in the 5,000m against a stellar international field. The 40-year-old distance running legend will face fierce competition from Kenya's Jacob Krop and Ethiopia's Hagos Gebrhiwet.

Long jump sensation Greg Rutherford, the 2012 Olympic champion, makes his highly anticipated return to Diamond League competition after a brief retirement. The 36-year-old will be looking to recapture his best form in front of a home crowd, with American world record holder Ryan Crouser providing formidable opposition.

Sprint queen Dina Asher-Smith headlines the women's 100m, fresh from her impressive performances at the World Championships. The British record holder will clash with Jamaica's Shericka Jackson and American sprinter Sha'Carri Richardson in what promises to be one of the most explosive races of the evening.

"Having these three British icons competing in Birmingham creates an electric atmosphere," said meeting director Tony Hadley. "This is exactly the kind of star power that makes the Diamond League special."

The Birmingham Diamond League meeting takes place on July 20th, with tickets expected to sell out rapidly following this announcement.

Turkish champions Fenerbahce have been withdrawn from the European Champions League group stage by the Turkish Football Federation (TFF) in relation to bribery and match fixing allegations. In July more than 30 people were arrested and jailed over claims that several football matches, as many as 19, in the Turkish first and second divisions had been manipulated. The highest profile figure arrested in the scandal was Fenerbahce's president Aziz Yildirim. Fenerbahce won last season's title on goal difference from Trabzonspor. But as the arrests mount the TFF decided to withdraw Fenerbahce before UEFA, European football's governing body, was forced to act on its behalf. "Regarding the heavy disciplinary sanctions both Fenerbahce and the Turkish Football Federation (TFF), which means Turkey might encounter, it is decided to ban Fenerbahce Sports Club from the UEFA Champions' League this season," read a statement posted on the TFF's official web site. The move took place 24 hours before Thursday's group stage draw. European football's governing body acted swiftly to end speculation over who will replace Fenerbahce by announcing that Trabzonspor, who finished second in the Turkish league last season, will take the final group stage berth. After a meeting of UEFA's Emergency Committee on Wednesday evening it was also agreed that Spanish club Athletic Bilbao will no longer need to take part in a qualification match against Trabzonspor on Thursday and will go straight in to the Europa League instead. "The panel considered that the Turkish Football Federation took the right decision to protect the game, fully in line with our zero-tolerance policy against match-fixing,"  said UEFA General Secretary Gianni Infantino in a statement posted on UEFA's web site. "The Turkish Football Federation has shown with this decision that it takes its full responsibility in the fight against corruption."

If you feel like you spend more time at the airport each year, that is because you do. British air passengers suffered increased delays on flights last summer and families heading to Orlando in Florida endured the longest hold-ups. The number of charter flights into and out of major UK airports that were on time dipped from 79 per cent in summer 2013 to 71 per cent in summer 2014, according to new statistics from the Civil Aviation Authority (CAA). Families suffered more delays and longer waits last summer than the precious year . Flight punctuality also fell for scheduled flights, dropping from 80 per cent on time in summer 2013 to just 75 per cent last year. The figures covered departures from 10 UK airports - Heathrow, Gatwick, Luton, Stansted, London City, Manchester, Birmingham, Newcastle, Edinburgh and Glasgow - during the summer period from July to September. As well as the frequency, the length of the delays are also getting longer. Both charter and scheduled flights suffered delays according to the Civil Aviation Authority statistics . The average delay to charter flights was 18 minutes in 2014 compared with 16 minutes in summer 2014. Average delays to scheduled flights rose from 12 minutes in summer 2013 to 14 minutes last summer. There was a 2.3 per cent increase in the number of scheduled flights monitored in summer 2014 compared with the previous summer, while the number of scheduled flights monitored rose 0.6 per cent in summer 2014. Flights to Orlando suffered the most delays, with an average wait of 31.9 minutes . Of the busiest 75 scheduled international destinations, Rotterdam in Holland recorded the highest on-time performance, with 87.6 per cent, and the lowest average delay, of 7.5 minutes. Flights to and from Pisa in Italy achieved the lowest on-time performance, with 49.9 per cent. Flights to and from Orlando achieved the highest average delay, of 31.9 minutes.

Furious railway staff called in police after discovering an airline's marketing team at one of their busiest stations - trying to lure their customers away. Managers at First Great Western were angered when they spotted Flybe workers at Exeter St David's train hub in Devon handing out coffees and promoting flights. The low-cost airline has just launched daily services from London to Exeter in direct competition to First Great Western rail services. Staff for the low-cost carrier turned up at 7am at the Devon station to promote the airline's new route (file) Flybe claim their Exeter to London City route is cheaper and quicker than the train, but First Great Western said they don't include the time to get to the airport and check-in and said rail is a 'strong alternative' on the service . As part of the promotional drive for the £60 return flights, they dispatched staff to stand outside the Exeter station to advertise the deal to commuters. Angry railway workers complained they did not have permission to stage the guerilla marketing drive and were causing an obstruction. British Transport Police confirmed they were called at 7am on October 23 after a complaint was made by station staff. The incident was eventually defused without any arrests although both sides have refused to back down following the spat which was dubbed 'transport wars' by travellers. First Great Western operates the only major rail route from Devon and Cornwall to London with tickets costing £93.50 for a return from Exeter to London. Flybe said it was 'regrettable' that First Great Western felt in necessary to contact the police as it was a 'free market' but railway managers said the airline's marketing team had 'inadvertently strayed onto our property' The new Flybe flights from Exeter to London City Airport are cheaper and quicker - taking 1hr 20 mins compared to 2hr 25 mins on the train. The company boasts of prices as low as £34.99 for a one-way ticket. Andrew McConnell, Flybe's director of communications, said: 'It is regrettable that in a commercial free market, First Great Western felt it necessary to contact the police to try to protect themselves from Flybe's offer of free coffee in a public space. 'Nothing's going to stop Flybe from being faster, cheaper and more punctual than rail travel.' First Great Western responded: 'We welcome healthy competition but Flybe's promotional team inadvertently strayed onto our property last week. 'Direct flights between Exeter and London are good news for the South West, providing those travelling with greater choices. 'However, with the time taken to check-in at Exeter International Airport and the distance the airport is to Exeter city centre, rail remains a strong, viable alternative - and the quickest way to travel.' British Transport Police said: 'We had received a complaint from station staff regarding representatives of Flybe operating at the station without permission, and a vehicle outside causing an obstruction. 'We spoke to both parties, and the staff representing Flybe agreed to leave the station.'

**Book Review: A Practical Guide for School Leadership**


This book should sit on the desk of every school administrator in America. Being a teacher or administrator is an extremely challenging role in today's educational environment, and this book provides a very practical (and fun) set of ideas for supporting and renewing educational staff. 


The author recognizes that effective school leadership goes beyond administrative tasks—it requires creating an environment where educators feel valued, motivated, and equipped to succeed. These research-based ideas will not only create a positive and safe climate for learning but will make those who use them effective leaders capable of inspiring their teams.


I found the following parts of the book particularly helpful for addressing common leadership challenges: an inventory to determine if "your teachers are hungry" (meaning engaged and motivated rather than burned out), the "P's" associated with being an effective administrator in the 21st Century (likely covering principles like being proactive, positive, and people-focused), and the 150 practical ways described to renew and re-energize a staff! 


In an era where teacher retention and morale are significant concerns in education, this book offers concrete solutions that busy administrators can actually implement. I hope you love this unique book as much as I do!

- "Would you look at all this crap?!" My brother never really understood the concept of grieving... Here he stood, overlooking all the knickknacks, jazz magazines, newspapers, ancient items of clothing, crackled furniture and a container worth pile of various shaped felt-tips while I was sitting in the favorite chair of our late Grandparent. - "Can you please stop whining and help me sort all those things like I asked you to maybe 3 hours ago? The truck Dad rented is going to be here any minute now and we still have these to put in boxes!" I distractedly shouted. "You grab that heavy desk and bring it outside; I'm going to put the pens in that box for our yard-sale tomorrow." - " Remind me why we are doing this again? Sarah, this is a job for a MOVING company or maybe HER grandchildren at least! -Yes well it seems like none of them cared enough to come and take care of her possessions. I mean they thought she was a little bit of a lunatic but come on, they could at least have come to her funeral yesterday... " Eric blatantly sighed and put his arms around said desk. His gray shirt was already very soaked from perspiration on his back and armpits. Eric was the fittest of the family so he always was asked to help during heavy lifting. He carried it through the main door and disappeared behind the purple willow's leaves. My outfit wasn't very dry itself, and as I remained seated, I felt like I could use a minute of fresh breeze on my face. I stood up, poured as many pens as I could fit into this cardboard basket and watched the mesmerizing flow of colors elapse. Suddenly, a sparkle caught my eye. I reached into the layers of pencils and grabbed the unusual object... It was a black varnished full sized wood felt-tip with very detailed gold engravings. Smooth lines were circling the word Develare and loop at the end of the lead. This pen clearly stood out of the others and somehow, this distinctive feature made me choose to not put it back and almost unwillingly conceal it in my trousers ' pocket. - "Sarah, are you finished sorting the books from the attic?" I jumped from surprise. I didn't hear my brother come back in. I realized straightaway that the truck pulled up in the driveway was still running, making a lot of muffled noise. I slowly responded: "Almost, there is a few more that I need to check out..." I walked towards the improvised library and closed the door behind me in order to keep the truck's stifled sounds away. As I was glancing through the rest of the books, one distinguished itself. It had a rich leather cover and radiant gold markings showing: Those who enter this reality beware For it is not for amateurs to bear May the Eye reveal to you What you always wanted to be true Fascinated, I ran my fingers across the blank pages, expecting some sort of science fiction story or designs. Unfortunately, it wasn't the case. The book seemed untouched until I reached the last page. Here was written in a powerful red color what was titled: The rules and warnings of the gift Leto brought to Phoebus: May the blood in this ink lock the stories and may the stories unveil only to the one who swore. I never saw anything like it. My grandma's name was at the very bottom among 5 or 6 others. I sat down on the floor, positioning the mysterious book in front of me. I took the felt-tip I found earlier out of his hideout and put it right aside the book. They clearly matched. I taunted myself, should I dare? I picked up the pen. The lead went from shady black to intense red. "What's the harm anyway, it's just a very stylish stupid book, and Grandma signed it, it really can't be that horrifying for me to use it after she passed away" I took the pen and wrote my name at the very first page in order to start a logbook of this place. My newly written name was swallowed into the fabric. I turned the sheet multiple times as I couldn't believe my eyes... It had disappeared! I flipped the pages... until I realized my name got added right next to my grandmother's...

More than three decades after the arrest of serial killer John Wayne Gacy, authorities in Chicago have launched a new effort to identify eight of his 33 victims. The Cook County Sheriff's Office says new technology has allowed it to obtain DNA profiles for the eight victims, whose remains were recovered from Gacy's house in late 1978 and early 1979 but were never identified. "These were eight young men who had futures, and one of the most evil people to ever be on this planet destroyed their lives," Sheriff Thomas Dart said Wednesday. Gacy was convicted in 1980 of raping and killing 33 boys and young men he had lured into his home over a period of six years, promising them construction jobs, drugs and alcohol, or by posing as a police officer or offering money for sex. Twenty-eight of the bodies were discovered under and around Gacy's home, with most found in the 40-foot crawl space beneath the house and garage. Gacy said there were four others he had thrown into the Des Plaines River. Gacy was put to death in 1994. The latest effort to identify the eight victims began this year when the sheriff's office decided to clear dozens of the department's cold case investigations. The eight unknown Gacy victims make up the department's largest cold case, so Dart approved their exhumations in order to obtain new DNA evidence and, if possible, identify them. Authorities used the DNA samples to compile profiles of each of the eight victims. It shows that they are white males between 14 and 32 years old, and authorities have even listed the approximate dates of their disappearances. The department is now seeking family members who had a male relative go missing in the 1970s and are willing to donate a DNA sample for comparison. "The public coming forward will allow us to do DNA matches that were not available 30 years ago," Dart said Wednesday. "It's not intrusive. It's something that is very straightforward. We have hot lines, websites set up for people to contact us, and in short order, we can conduct any of the matches that need to be done." After Gacy's arrest, authorities learned that some of his identified victims had never been reported missing, the sheriff's office said. One reason was that the victims had been estranged from their families. Another possible reason is that the relevant missing persons reports never made it to Chicago. Gacy was known to have targeted bus stations, the sheriff's office said, seeking victims who were traveling. Those interested in giving a DNA sample can contact the Cook County Sheriff's Office at 1-800-942-1950 or www.cookcountysheriff.com.

for National Geographic News  
Three mummies have been moved from the Valley of the Kings in Luxor to the Egyptian Museum in Cairo so that scientists can study where they came from, Egyptian authorities recently announced.  
Two female mummies, found in a tomb called KV21, and one male mummy, found outside the tomb of Pharaoh Seti II (who ruled Egypt from 1200 to 1194 B.C.), will have CAT scans and DNA tests.  
These tests might help scientists learn who these mummies were and who their families were, but experts say it might take years to be certain.  
Even so, the two female mummies may be important. Archaeologists hope that they could be some of the Egyptian queens they have been trying to find.  
Both women were found in a special pose that only royal women used: their left arms were bent at the elbow with the hand placed across the chest, while the right arms were straight along the body.  
"We'll try to study the two females in KV21, because we're looking for Tutankhamun's family through the Egyptian Mummy Project," said Zahi Hawass, head of Egypt's Supreme Council of Antiquities.  
This project is a five-year effort started by Hawass to check and record the DNA of every mummy in Egypt.  
"Maybe one of them is Nefertiti or Tiye or Kiya, we do not know," said Hawass, who also works with National Geographic. (The National Geographic Society owns National Geographic News.)  
An Egyptian team will study the mummies’ genes in what Hawass calls the only lab in the world built just for mummy DNA tests.  
The best way to study old genes is by looking at mitochondrial DNA, said Angelique Corthals, a teacher in biomedical and forensic studies at the University of Manchester, UK, who helped train the Egyptian team.  
This DNA is passed down from mothers to children and has thousands of copies in every cell. (Learn more about human genetics.)

Susan Orlean: A Masterclass in Magazine Writing delivers precisely what its title promises. Orlean, renowned for her ability to transform the mundane into the magnificent, demystifies the art of the long-form feature. The masterclass illuminates her signature blend of meticulous research, narrative elegance, and a seemingly effortless wit, revealing how she crafts stories that are both deeply human and intellectually engaging. For anyone aspiring to write compelling non-fiction, or simply to appreciate the nuanced craft behind her iconic pieces, this is an essential deep-dive into the mind of a true literary journalist. It’s a captivating blueprint for elevating reportage into art.

Pietra Rivoli’s "The Travels of a T-Shirt in the Global Economy" is a compelling exploration of globalization told through the life story of a simple cotton t-shirt. Rivoli follows her t-shirt from Texas cotton fields to Chinese factories, African used-clothing markets, and beyond, weaving together economic theory, historical context, and personal narrative. The book stands out for making complex issues accessible and engaging, showing how trade policies, labor, and markets connect people worldwide. Rivoli’s balanced perspective avoids easy moral judgments, allowing readers to see the nuances and contradictions of global commerce. While some readers might wish for more in-depth analysis of alternatives to the status quo, the book remains a highly readable, insightful introduction to the forces shaping our global economy.

Antarctic Intermediate WaterArticle Free Pass
Antarctic Intermediate Water, ocean water mass found in all the southern oceans at depths of about 1,650 to 4,000 ft (500 to 1,200 m), characterized by temperatures of 37° to 45° F (3° to 7° C) and salinities of 33.8 to about 34.5 parts per thousand. This water mass forms at the Antarctic Convergence in the latitudinal belt between 50° and 60° S, where water with the relatively low salinity of 33.8 parts per thousand is cooled to about 40.7° F. The resulting density, 1.02702, causes the water to sink beneath the surface before it slowly flows laterally northward. It becomes modified by mixing with other waters, but it is still discernible as minima in temperature and salinity profiles obtained at hydrographic stations as far north as latitude 35° N in the Atlantic Ocean and latitude 20° S in the Indian and Pacific oceans.
