By . Daniel Martin, Whitehall Correspondent . PUBLISHED: . 19:59 EST, 12 August 2013 . | . UPDATED: . 19:59 EST, 12 August 2013 . The Duke of York was drawn into the royal lobbying row last night after it emerged he has held 11 private meetings with Cabinet ministers since the  general election. Over the past three years, he has invited a string of senior politicians such as Chancellor George Osborne, Business Secretary Vince Cable and Education Secretary Michael Gove for audiences at Buckingham Palace. He met Foreign Secretary William Hague twice and also held two meetings with Andrew Mitchell when he was International Development Secretary. Duke of York: Buckingham Palace spokesman says he meets a vast range of people all his meetings are recorded in Court Circular . Prince Andrew met Chris Huhne when he was Energy Secretary. He has also met two Northern Ireland Secretaries, Owen Paterson and Theresa Villiers, and met Mr Paterson again when he became Environment Secretary. He even called Cabinet Secretary Sir Jeremy Heywood, Britain’s most senior civil servant, into the Palace for a summit. The archives of the Court Circular reveal that the Prince’s meetings with senior figures continued even after he stepped down from his controversial official role as the UK’s Special Representative for International Trade and Investment. He was forced into a humiliating resignation from the position in mid-2011 following criticism for his perceived closeness to unsavoury regimes such as that in the former Soviet republic of Azerbaijan. During his ten-year tenure he attracted the nickname ‘Airmiles Andy’. Michael Gove, Secretary of State for Education, pictured, has been invited to Buckingham Palace to meet the Duke of York . Details of the Duke’s high-level summits . comes a day after the Daily Mail revealed that Prince Charles has held . 36 private meetings with Cabinet ministers since the election – one a . month. While Clarence House insisted that the meetings were to prepare . him for his future role as head of state, critics accused Charles of an . ‘incontinence of lobbying’ because many of the audiences were with . ministers covering pet issues such as climate change, alternative . medicine and architecture. Last night, Labour MP Paul Flynn asked why Prince Andrew needed to hold private meetings with busy ministers, when he no longer held any official role. ‘What is he talking to William Hague about?’ he asked. ‘The Duke of York has been cosy with some pretty odious regimes in the past. We want lobbyists to declare who they are seeing, and the same should be true of the Royal Family.’ But a Buckingham Palace spokesman said: ‘The Duke meets a vast range of people in connection with his work including, on occasions, government ministers. Like all official engagements, these meetings are recorded in the Court Circular.’

Janurius Davis sighed. For so long, his empire had held out against the withering decay that had split it apart. An agreement had been struck, penned by the great George Caesar himself, to divide the empire into two halves and prevent it from collapsing under its own weight. Sadly, this move proved to be a mistake. Years of Canadian invasion crippled the Northern Roman Empire. Even Jefferson's Wall had done little to stem these attacks.

After the fall of the Northern Empire, life in the Southern Empire mostly continued as normal—until the Aztecs arrived. The clash of empires was devastating for both sides. Waves of Aztec Jaguars and Confederate Droman’s clashed, blood running like rain in the gutters of Texas. Despite heavy losses, the Aztecs did not falter for an instant.

Janurius shuddered remembering the tale of Davius Crockett, whose still-beating heart was torn out upon the altar of Mars at the Alamo when it finally fell. Now, in the waning days of the conflict, only Richmond remained. Fifteen hundred years of Roman rule in the Americas was finally over.

Janurius flinched as the doors of the palace were forced open. The feared Cuauhtemoc entered the Confederate throne room and shouted something in his native tongue. Janurius did not understand, nor did he care—all that mattered was that his life and his empire were now forfeit.

“Well, Canada, they’re your and the Iroquois’ problem now,” he muttered to himself as two burly Aztec Jaguars grabbed him and forced him to the ground. He didn’t want to think about what Cuauhtemoc was about to do with that knife…

A distant relative of the writer of "The Star Spangled Banner," Francis Scott Key Fitzgerald was raised by his father following the death of his mother; the two lived off her small inheritance. Fitzgerald wrote a series of novels taking place in the "roaring twenties." In 1925 Fitzgerald created the arguably biographical, The Great Gatsby. In the novel Fitzgerald uses animals, mainly a puppy, to characterize, and to drive the plot.
In the earlier section of the book, chapter 2, the reader meets the puppy. In meeting the puppy, Fitzgerald conveys education levels of Myrtle Wilson and Tom Buchanan. "I don't suppose you got that kind?" (27) This quote from Myrtle shows us that her english level is below par. However, Tom's knowledge is shown to be slightly more extensive when he realizes that the dog the man tries to sell them isn't the type myrtle had desired.
"That's no police dog." (27) Throughout the entire ordeal, Myrtle has somewhat of a gitty disposition, while Tom is ready to move ahead. His impatience is show when Myrtle asks the sex of the dog. The dog is said to be male, but he replies, "It's a bitch." (27) This attitude remains throughout the novel. Fitzgerald uses this puppy scene as a fantastic opportunity to characterize.
In the later section of the book, chapter 8, the reader is told about the puppy's dog collar. Fitzgerald no longer uses the puppy for characterization, but rather to provide a vehicle for Gatsby's death, and consequently Wilson's. Wilson had been suspicious of Myrtle before this incident, but it was later that Wilson is assured of her affair after discovering the dog collar Tom had bought for her pet. George Wilson is so mad that he takes his ideas so far as to say that the person who gave her this collar, murdered Myrtle. Eventually, Wilson acquires the location of the yellow car that ran his wife over; Gatsby's house. Wilson murders Gatsby and then commits suicide. This little puppy is one of the main vehicles that leads to Gatsby's death.
Fitzgerald used fantastic writing craft in his novels. He himself had a dog; this fact just adding to the mirror of his non fiction life. Fitzgerald uses the Airedale as a way to characterize his many intricate characters, and to drive the plot.

Vembanadu Lake is the largest water body in Kerala’s lowlands, stretching 60 km long and 16 km across at the widest areas. The famous, labyrinthine backwaters display their grandeur here. Along its shore, poets and painters have drawn inspiration from ancient times. Resorts and hotels followed in their wake as tourism increased.
Sunset and dawn are the best times in the backwaters. The twilight moments are ideal for lazing in the lake – facing portico or on the long, wooden jetty projecting into the lagoon. At the break of dawn, the long sleek fishing boats can be seen making their unhurried forays into the lake, punted along by solitary fishermen silhouetted against the gray sky. Flocks of white egret birds and black cormorants may be seen skimming over the placid waters looking for their breakfast. If the air is still, the calm surface of the lake looks like a sheet of glass. Sometimes, vision of wind ruffling the waters may be seen.
At night, lights from small country boats can be seen flashing in the distance like fireflies.
After sunset, in the receding dusk, the lake gets enveloped in a surreal twilight. It is a magical time. Evenings in the backwaters have a mesmerizing effect, with the languorous lagoon aglow in twilight’s last fleeting rays.
A visit to a boat-building yard on the shores of Vembanadu Lake would be interesting. Houseboats, canoes, small sailboats, etc. are turned out regularly from the yards. They are built completely of wood, according to ancient traditions in the area, by skilled craftsmen. Nails and metallic parts are not used as they would corrode gradually due to chemical reactions. Instead, coconut fibre and ropes made from it are used to tie together the wooden planks. This kind of joints is reinforced with cashew resin, which make the craft waterproof. The craft for houseboats (called ‘Kettuvallom’ in local language Malayalam) are made this way.
'Pathiramanal', a little island on the backwaters is a favourite haunt of hundreds of rare birds, some migratory.
This island is between Kumarakom and Thanneermukkom. It is accessible only by boat.
Kumarakom is synonymous with some of the best that Kerala has to offer to tourists – swaying palms, languorous lagoons and boat rides. The famous backwaters of southern Kerala display their true grandeur here. For, most travelers, Kumarakom ranks high on the list of holiday destinations. One visit is all it would take to find out why.
Also worth a visit is the bird sanctuary about 9km away from Kumarakom, on the southern bank of Kavanar River. This sanctuary is home to a variety of migratory birds, including those from Siberia in the polar region, apart from numerous other species like several varieties of herons, darters, marsh harries, teals, kites, bitterns, etc.
Flocks of egrets skim homeward, their whiteness almost touching the waters.

By Hugo Gye. The world’s smallest library has popped up on the streets of New York City. It’s a bright yellow plastic booth that only fits one person at a time and holds about 40 books. The goal is to give busy city residents a quiet place to relax and lose themselves in a good story.

This “Little Free Library” was created by two creative architects using recycled materials to keep the books safe from the weather. The small library is made from an old plastic water tank and a wooden frame, with holes on the outside so curious people can peek in before going inside. Venezuelan architects Marcelo Ertorteguy and Sara Valente designed the space so that people can really focus on browsing and reading.

The library in Manhattan’s Nolita neighborhood has gotten a lot of attention since it opened last month. Mr. Ertorteguy explained that the Little Free Library movement began about three years ago and has quickly spread across the U.S. and to places in Europe, Africa, and India. “It’s surprising that New York City was one of the few major cities without any of these tiny libraries,” he said.

Unlike a typical library building, this one stands out. It’s just one of 5,000 Free Lending Libraries around the U.S. and the world. The designers hope to give New Yorkers a small escape from daily life. The Nolita library is part of a project with the Architectural League of New York and the Pen World Voices Festival, and it will stay up until September.

Mr. Ertorteguy said they enjoy using recycled materials, and in this case, they wanted to reuse something in a completely new way—a water tank was perfect because it could protect the books and be big enough for a person to go inside. “We wanted to make a special spot where people could slow down—a pocket of calm on the busy streets where you can step inside and get lost in a book,” he said.

The unusual-looking library has gotten lots of attention from New Yorkers. It’s a tight fit inside—it can squeeze in two people, but they won’t have much room to move around. The library sits outside a school in the trendy Nolita district.

There are now over 5,000 Little Free Libraries nationwide and around the world. Most are just outdoor shelves where you can take or leave books, but don’t have room to sit or read inside. The goal of the project is to make sure everyone, even in neighborhoods without public libraries, has access to books.

A distant relative of the writer of "The Star Spangled Banner," Francis Scott Key Fitzgerald was raised by his father following the death of his mother; the two lived off her small inheritance, the days often haunted by the quiet ache of loss and financial uncertainty. The rooms of their home seemed to cling to shadows, their modest surroundings shaped by faded carpets and the faint scent of old books—an atmosphere that seeped into Fitzgerald’s prose. Fitzgerald wrote a series of novels set in the tumultuous and dazzling “roaring twenties,” an era suffused with jazz music that spilled out into the night air from glowing, crowded speakeasies, where the air was thick with cigarette smoke and the undercurrent of reckless ambition.

In 1925, Fitzgerald created the arguably biographical, The Great Gatsby. The novel drips with the sultry heat of long New York nights and the electric flush of city life, but beneath the glamour pulses a strain of unease. Within these pages, Fitzgerald uses animals—most notably a small, trembling puppy—to characterize and to drive the plot, introducing the animal in an apartment shrouded in the claustrophobic haze of forbidden rendezvous.

In the earlier section of the book, chapter 2, the reader meets the puppy in a setting that feels both intimate and stifling: sunlight slants through the dusty window, casting pale shafts across worn linoleum, the air tinged with the faint musk of sweat and spirits. The tiny dog, scared and confused, becomes the centerpiece of an awkward exchange layered with tension and pretense. In meeting the puppy, Fitzgerald deftly conveys the education levels and aspirations of Myrtle Wilson and Tom Buchanan. Myrtle’s hopeful, slightly desperate words hang in the air: "I don't suppose you got that kind?" (27). This line, spoken as she clutches the puppy, reveals not only her lack of formal education but also her longing to possess something beyond her reach. Meanwhile, Tom’s blunt recognition that “That's no police dog” (27) slices through Myrtle’s illusions with the casual authority of someone accustomed to commanding both people and situations. Throughout the ordeal, Myrtle’s laughter bubbles up nervously, filling the cramped space with a sense of anticipation and unease, while Tom’s restlessness crackles like static electricity, always threatening to explode into violence or indifference. When Myrtle asks the sex of the dog and Tom curtly replies, "It's a bitch," (27), the air grows sharp, thick with an unspoken tension that lingers long after the words fade. Fitzgerald uses this puppy scene masterfully to paint the dynamic between his characters, the pacing of their dialogue echoing footsteps in an empty hallway.

In a later, gloomier section of the book—chapter 8—the atmosphere darkens further as the reader learns of the puppy’s dog collar, now a cold, metallic artifact in a world tinged with suspicion and grief. The skies outside Gatsby’s mansion hang heavy with the promise of rain, and the rooms echo hollow, filled with the silence of loss. The puppy, once a symbol of naïve hope, is gone, but its collar—glinting dully in the half-light—becomes evidence, a clue that drags George Wilson deeper into his slow-burning madness. Now, the puppy is no longer a vehicle for characterization, but a harbinger of tragedy, a quiet witness to Myrtle’s death and the consequences that spiral out beneath the overcast, oppressive sky. As Wilson fixates on the collar, the emotional air grows taut, charged with the electricity of his suspicions—a storm ready to break. The revelation of the collar becomes the tipping point that sends Wilson, hollow-eyed and trembling, stalking through the night toward Gatsby’s house, his rage barely contained. The puppy’s collar is the final, silent link in a chain of events that leads inexorably to Gatsby’s death and Wilson’s own bleak end.

Fitzgerald’s writing is drenched in atmosphere—the jazz-age world throbs with energy even as it shivers on the edge of despair. In true writer’s fashion, Fitzgerald mirrored his own life in his work; he himself had a dog, weaving this personal detail into his fiction like smoke curling beneath a lamp-lit ceiling. Through the figure of the Airedale, Fitzgerald channels both the exuberance and vulnerability of his era, using the puppy not just as a device for characterization but as a catalyst for the unraveling of dreams, set against the moody, volatile landscape of the American 1920s.

Linguists announced Monday they have identified an endangered language known as Koro that is spoken by about 800 people in northeast India. The language was unknown to science and recently came to light during an expedition by linguists traveling in India on fellowships for National Geographic, the linguists said in telephone interviews. Koro belongs to the Tibeto-Burman language family, which is composed of a group of about 400 languages spoken primarily in east, central, south and southeast Asia and includes Tibetan and Burmese, according to linguist K. David Harrison. Some 150 Tibeto-Burman languages are spoken in India alone, but no other language has been identified as closely related, said Harrison, an associate professor of linguistics at Swarthmore College in Swarthmore, Pennsylvania. Like most languages, Koro is unwritten and transmitted orally. It is neither a dialect nor a sister language close to Hruso-Aka, despite being considered such by both Hruso and Koro people. Koro shares some vocabulary with other languages spoken in the region but shares more features with languages spoken farther east, such as Milang and Tani, the linguists said in a news release issued by National Geographic. Harrison and another National Geographic Fellow, Greg Anderson, led the expedition, called Enduring Voices, which brought Koro to light. Enduring Voices documents vanishing languages and cultures and assists with language revitalization. Harrison and Anderson, director of the Living Tongues Institute for Endangered Languages, in Salem, Oregon, focused on Arunachal Pradesh, a remote area of northeast India that is considered the black hole of the linguistic world. It is a language hotspot where there is room to study rich, diverse languages, many unwritten or undocumented. A permit is required to visit, few linguists have worked there and a reliable list of languages has never been drawn up. "On a scientist's tally sheet, Koro adds just one entry to the list of 6,909 languages worldwide. But Koro's contribution is much greater than that tiny fraction would suggest," Harrison writes in his book, "The Last Speakers." "Koro brings an entirely different perspective, history, mythology, technology and grammar to what was known before." In the news release, the linguists described their discovery as bittersweet: Of the approximately 800 people who speak Koro, few are under the age of 20, meaning the language is endangered. "We were finding something that was making its exit, was on its way out," Anderson said. "And if we had waited 10 years to make the trip, we might not have come across as many speakers as we found." The team set out in 2008 in Arunachal Pradesh to document Aka and Miji, languages spoken in a small district there. The expedition went door to door among homes propped up on stilts to reach potential speakers of those little-known languages. While recording the vocabularies, they detected a third language -- Koro. It was not listed in Indian language surveys, Indian censuses or standard international registries. "We didn't have to get far on our word list to realize it was extremely different in every possible way," Harrison said. The inventory of sounds and the way these sounds were combined to form words were distinct from other languages spoken in the region. An Aka speaker would call a pig "vo" and a Koro speaker would call a pig "lele." "Koro could hardly sound more different from Aka," Harrison writes. "They sound as different as, say, English and Japanese." Anderson and Harrison said Aka is the traditional language of the region's historic slave traders, and they hypothesized that Koro may have sprung from the slaves; though they said more study is needed to determine the origin. The project reports that a language becomes extinct every two weeks. By 2100, it is estimated that more than half of the 6,910 languages spoken on earth will vanish. The team will return to India to continue studying Koro in November.

Shell said the cuts would help it maintain competitiveness and ensure the long-term sustainability of the North Sea business, which faces mounting pressures from volatile oil prices, aging fields, and an increasing global shift toward renewable energy. The North Sea region, once a booming hub for the oil and gas industry, has witnessed a gradual decline in production and rising operational costs, prompting companies to reassess their business strategies. Of the 90 posts going, half are full-time employees with the rest made up of agency staff and contractors, many of whom are based in Shell’s Aberdeen offices, a city known as the energy capital of Europe. The jobs are expected to go by the end of the year, adding to concerns within a local economy long reliant on the fortunes of oil and gas. Shell's 1,700-strong workforce in Aberdeen were told about the redundancies at meetings on Thursday morning, reported to have taken place at their modern Westhill campus on the outskirts of the city, which has served as a hub for North Sea operations. No jobs will be lost offshore, as Shell looks to maintain the workforce required for ongoing platform activities in the harsh marine environment of the North Sea. Shell announced in January it had agreed to sell $3.8bn (£2.46bn) worth of North Sea assets to oil exploration firm Chrysaor, marking a significant shift in ownership within the region as established players streamline their portfolios in response to changing market dynamics and stricter environmental regulations.

The chill seeped into Elias’s bones long before he saw it. It wasn’t the winter draft from the old window, but an invasive, unnatural cold that gnawed at the warmth of the roaring fire. He’d been reading, the quiet hum of his apartment a familiar comfort, until a strange pressure began to build in the air, thick and suffocating. The shadows in the corners deepened, not with the natural fall of evening, but as if something was actively drinking the light.

Then, it coalesced. Not in the doorway, nor from a wall, but simply *there*, in the center of the room, as if the very fabric of existence had been torn open and something had spilled through. It wasn't a creature of flesh and blood, but an absence, a twisting vortex of pure malice. Its form was an impossible geometry of shadow and hunger, constantly shifting, hinting at limbs that didn't belong, eyes that were pits of starlight swallowed by cosmic dread.

No sound escaped Elias’s lips. His throat was a frozen knot. The entity pulsed, and with each beat, a silent scream echoed in his mind, a chorus of forgotten torments. Its gaze, if the obsidian depths could be called eyes, fixed on him. It didn’t move, not physically, but its presence *expanded*, filling every corner of the room, every crevice of his mind. He felt it rummaging through his memories, plucking out his deepest fears, holding them up like gruesome trophies. It showed him the quiet despair of his youth, the agonizing regret of his failures, the creeping certainty of his own mortality—but amplified, distorted, made infinitely more terrible.

A voice, not heard with his ears but felt directly in his skull, whispered. It was a symphony of static and ancient sorrow, promising oblivion, offering a final, agonizing clarity. *You are nothing. You were never safe. And now, you know.*

The air grew so heavy it felt like his lungs would rupture. He watched, helpless, as the entity seemed to *taste* his terror, growing momentarily denser, its impossible form shimmering with malevolent satisfaction. Then, as abruptly as it had arrived, it simply unraveled. The shadows released their grip, the temperature normalized, and the pressure vanished, leaving behind only the scent of ozone and burnt sugar.

Elias spent the rest of the night huddled in a corner, rocking, incoherent. The morning light brought no solace. The entity was gone, but it had left an indelible mark.

The world was no longer solid. Every shadow hinted at a monstrous form, every creak of the floorboards was a whisper of its return. Sleep became a battleground where its voice still echoed, showing him fractured glimpses of the cosmic horror it represented. He saw its impossible eyes in the faces of strangers, felt its suffocating presence in crowded rooms, heard its promises of annihilation in the rustle of leaves.

He couldn't work. Couldn't eat. Couldn't connect with people who still lived in their unblemished reality. They looked at him with pity, saw a man broken by an unseen trauma, but they didn't understand that the trauma wasn't just *in* him; it had subtly reshaped the *entire world* around him. He knew what lay beneath the veneer of normalcy, the thinness of the veil. He knew he was never safe, had never been safe, and that the beautiful, fragile lie of a benign existence was just that: a lie.

Elias was a husk, forever listening, forever watching, forever waiting for the impossible tear to open again, knowing that next time, it wouldn’t just show him the truth. Next time, it would take him.

THE CURSE OF THE DEMON CORE
The real-life story of a small ball of plutonium, the people
it killed, and the researchers who blew it up.
On the evening of Tuesday, August 21, 1945, American physicist Harry Daghlian was working at the U.S. government’s ultra-secret Los Alamos National Laboratory in New Mexico. He was performing a very delicate experiment: Daghlian was placing brick-shaped pieces of metal around a chunk of plutonium, the highly unstable fuel used in most nuclear bombs. And he was making it more unstable with every brick he placed around it. […]
Daghlian was working with a gray, softball-sized sphere of Pu-239. It was basically the core, or pit, of a nuclear bomb—the part that does the exploding. He was performing experiments with the core to determine whether it was the proper size and density to sustain a chain reaction—so it could be used in an actual bomb.
Daghlian began surrounding the core with bricks of tungsten carbide, a very dense metal that reflects neutron radiation. The more enclosed in metal the core became, the more neutrons were reflected back into the core, rather than simply flying off. That meant that the rate of neutron bashing and atom splitting in the core increased as Daghlian added more and more bricks. (A geiger counter indicated whether the experiment was working, by clicking faster and faster.) […]
Using the bricks, Daghlian built walls, about ten inches on a side and ten inches high, around the plutonium. He then took a brick and slowly positioned it—he was simply holding it in his hand—over the opening at the top of the structure, right over the core. The geiger counter clicked wildly. Enough neutrons were now being reflected back into the core that it was headed toward a supercritical state.
Daghlian went to jerk the brick away…and dropped it.
The brick landed right on top of the ball of plutonium. The plutonium was now effectively surrounded by neutron reflecting material, and it went supercritical immediately. There was a blue flash…

The man was nervous. The whispers sounded like a females voice. The guilt built the paranoia from within him. She has screamed curses out to him as he beat her, the last breath out of her punctured lungs cursed his name. He was nearly certain he'd walk down stairs and find her cold corpse glarring at him. As he slinked down the steps, crucifix in hand he saw in the darkness two red dots of equal size. "Fuck off" the man sighed He walked over to the radio and switched it all the way off, and the two dim red lights turned off along with the whispering. As he turned and walked back up stairs, he heard the whispering once more, but this time, it was from the otherside of his bedroom door.

cane toads—those big, bumpy green invaders wreaking havoc on northern Australian ecosystems—are more than just a nuisance; they are an ecological disaster in progress. Since their introduction in 1935 to control cane beetles, a plan that spectacularly backfired, these toxic amphibians have spread relentlessly across Queensland, the Northern Territory, and into Western Australia, leaving a trail of devastation in their wake.

Their success lies in a combination of factors. Cane toads are incredibly prolific breeders, laying tens of thousands of eggs at a time. They are generalist predators, consuming almost anything that fits into their mouths, and they lack natural predators in Australia due to the potent bufotenin toxin secreted from glands on their shoulders. Native wildlife, from quolls and goannas to snakes and even freshwater crocodiles, often perish after attempting to eat a cane toad, having not evolved the necessary resistance or avoidance behaviors. This leads to localized extinctions and significant disruptions in food webs.

The impact extends beyond direct predation and poisoning. Cane toads compete with native frogs and other small animals for food and breeding sites. Their tadpoles are also toxic and can outcompete native tadpoles for resources. Efforts to control their spread have been numerous and varied, ranging from physical removal programs by volunteers to scientific research into biological controls and genetic manipulation. However, the sheer scale of the problem and the toad's adaptability make a definitive solution elusive.

Understanding and mitigating the cane toad's impact remains a critical conservation challenge. While the front lines of their invasion continue to push westward, scientists and land managers are working to develop strategies that can protect vulnerable native species and manage established toad populations, hoping to restore some balance to Australia's unique and fragile northern environments.

I was moved today by a video of young people taking action in support of indigenous rights, cultural rights and self determination:
This example of student driven action goes well beyond adult organized marches, or adult driven activity for social justice. Many of these young people show an enduring understanding of their interdependence and interconnection with a nation and the world. For example, at minute 11:00 in the video a young woman articulates a distinct article of the UN Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples (More on the declaration and UNPFII here!)
For all of us who see open and free learning as a fundamental human right, it’s important to recognize that there is global deliberation and decision making on issues well beyond neoliberalism happening in the UN and in other spaces….How we participate in these movements and with others around the world on these issues will shape the common bond we have as humans in the 21st century. As ecological and economic overshoot continues, understanding how to participate and network for education and global civic culture will increase in importance.
Since [UN Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples] adoption, Australia, New Zealand, Canada and the United States have all reversed their positions and now endorse the Declaration.

Main Points on Acts and Regulations About the Work Environment:

- The Work Environment Act aims to prevent ill health and accidents in the workplace and to promote a good work environment. It places ultimate responsibility on the employer to continuously improve work conditions.

- The Working Hours Act regulates how much you are allowed to work per day, week, and year and determines your rights to pauses, rest breaks, and meal breaks.

- Collective agreements between employers and trade unions can further regulate your working conditions. If no such agreement exists, only the national laws—set by the Swedish Riksdag—apply.

- The Swedish Work Environment Authority, appointed by the government, issues detailed provisions on specific aspects of the work environment, such as noise, hazardous substances, solitary work, and workplace design.

## Timeless Beauty: Expert Stone Care & Maintenance Tips

Did you know that over 45% of our planet is made of rock and stone? For millennia, humans have harnessed this incredible natural resource, shaping it into homes, monuments, and timeless structures that stand strong even today. From ancient pyramids to modern countertops, stone brings unparalleled beauty and durability to our lives.

But even the toughest stone isn't immune to the passage of time. Wear and tear, environmental elements, and everyday use can gradually diminish its luster, leading to fading, damage, and decay. For the stone features in your home or garden, this can be truly disheartening.

The good news? Keeping your stone surfaces pristine and strong for years to come is entirely achievable! There are effective methods and specialized products designed not only to clean but also to reinforce the natural resilience of stone. Let's dive into how you can protect your investment.

### 1. Combat Fading with Polishing Powders

Commonly used stones, like limestone, are beautiful but can suffer from color fading over time. When this happens, specialized polishing powders, such as those by Aguila, come to the rescue. Mixed with water to form a paste, these powders effectively strip away dirt and debris, revealing a smooth surface and revitalized color beneath. It’s like a mini spa treatment for your stone!

### 2. Deep Clean with Specialized Brushes

For a deeper, more thorough clean, an Aguila cleaning brush is an indispensable tool. Unlike traditional brushes with softer bristles, Aguila's brushes feature stronger, specially designed fibers that work *with* the natural grain of the stone. This unique approach allows for exceptional debris removal and a far cleaner surface without causing damage.

### 3. Achieve a Superior Shine with Polishing Blocks

While rubbing one stone against another can indeed polish surfaces, it's often inefficient, especially with irregular shapes. Enter the polishing block! These ingenious tools are precisely ground and shaped stones designed for optimal contact across various proportions. The Akemi grinding block, for example, offers two sides with different grit consistencies – a finer side for delicate polishing and a slightly thicker grit for more intensive work, ensuring a professional-grade finish.

### The Golden Rule: Consistent Maintenance

The ultimate secret to long-lasting stone beauty is consistent care. Regular polishing – for a few hours at a time, depending on the surface size – is crucial. Prolonged exposure to the elements without maintenance can weaken the stone's internal structure over time. This is why many protect exterior stone, much like painting brickwork, to seal and strengthen it against environmental damage. Don't wait until damage is visible; proactive care preserves your stone's integrity and aesthetic appeal.

The officer stares me down while i am turned around. Standing over my victim, my goal finally accomplished. "Why'd you do it? Why did you kill all those innocent people?" *Because they were'nt as innocent as their personas portrayed them to be. * The officer still had his hand on the trigger, ready to fire. I could sense the barrel of his gun pointing me out like *Wheres Waldo*. Sweat slowrly dripping off the the chin nervously...no confused. "What have these people ever done to deserve death? Well other than meeting a deranged psychopath like you!" *Well see this "innocent"guy as you would call him murdered his bestfriend and staged the crime scene to pin it on the bestfriends brother!! This"innocent" man is the reason...The reason a innocent man died before he should!! * *dont worry though, he was the last on my list please just walk away without further bloodshed...* The officer hesitated for a second. He quickly shook off the doubt steadly building up. "You know i cant do that, i took a pledge to justice! Maybe if you come silently we can make a deal or something." All I could do was smile at his innocence. *Officer do you know what happened to the bestfriend that died? * I turn around so he can finally see my face clearly. *That child was claimed dead and burried. He only attempted to escape the coffin after awakeing from a trauma induced coma just in time to escape the closed coffin in the back of the hurse. * I watch a tear fall from his eyes... *Im that child officer and the justice system was to flawed to convict the real murderer! Dont you understand? I had too. * I knew this was my last right. I cant die in jail like my brother. I reach for my gun and the officer fires 3 shots. I feel the world go black. "Im here to hand in my gun and badge chief." The officer dropped his gun and badge on the table and walked away. *It was the last time he could serve under a flawed justice system. The last victim knew he would have to shoot if he reached for the gun, but he did it anyway. He was only doing what he thought was right. Only serving his own justice. I hated not having superpowers, a fact my family was in denial about for years, even putting me in a superhero school. My dad finally called, confessing that he and Mom had expected too much from me, admitting their own denial and disappointment after I quit superhero school and left home. Though their admission initially made me reflect on my flaws and past exploitation, their newfound humility countered my self-doubt.

My siblings, typically chaotic with their psychic and elemental powers, then surprised me. They humbly apologized and took turns talking, their genuine desire to connect, coupled with their humility, moved me to tears. My mom followed, apologizing for her past harshness, affirming her support for a regular human school, and welcoming me home.

As she spoke, I looked up to see "The Super Sidekicks," a group of classmates Skidder's gang mocked. They expressed worry for me, revealed Skidder's gang was suspended (thanks to one of them gathering evidence), and, crucially, apologized for their inaction during the bullying. They admitted their fear but acknowledged their power to act, stating, "Our superpowers don't make us perfect, Joe... You really had showed some very good points back there."

Staring at them, a label I once feared as it implied being left behind, I realized my pursuit of powers had led me to the wrong crowd. These classmates, neither aces nor dunces, embodied a new truth: "Sidekicks are heroes too, 'cause heroes wouldn't be heroes without the sidekicks." Tears of joy returned as I told them, "Thanks, heroes. If you need a sidekick, you can call me."

I now believe being a true friend is a superpower. And though my new friends might find it unsettling, I intend to extend that friendship, not arrogantly but with guidance, even to Skidder and his gang. If others helped me grow, I can help fellow "idiots" do the same.

By Gerri Peev

China may be able to spy on UK citizens and businesses via broadband equipment it supplies to major British telecom companies, Parliament’s intelligence oversight committee has warned. The Intelligence and Security Committee (ISC) expressed grave concerns about BT and O2’s contracts with the Chinese telecom giant Huawei, stating in its report that China could "covertly intercept or disrupt" traffic on networks utilizing Huawei equipment. The committee criticized the “weak” oversight of Huawei's operations in the UK and the government's lack of a cohesive strategy.

The report warned that vulnerabilities in equipment sold to British firms present an opportunity for Chinese hackers to conduct espionage. Huawei currently provides mobile phones, routers, and other critical infrastructure for several British telecommunication companies. The concerns were serious enough that Chancellor George Osborne issued a rare public response, emphasizing the value of Chinese investment and the UK’s openness to trade with China, likely in anticipation of potential tensions between the two countries. He stated, “Inward investment is key to generating UK jobs and growth. Increasing trade ties with China is a personal priority, and I cannot stress enough that the UK welcomes Chinese investment.”

Among its recommendations, the ISC suggested that GCHQ should take over management of Huawei's cyber security evaluation facility in Banbury, Oxfordshire, highlighting failings in government oversight that the committee, chaired by Sir Malcolm Rifkind, called unacceptable. Huawei’s contract with BT was signed eight years ago as part of a £2.5 billion superfast broadband initiative intended to supply two-thirds of UK homes and businesses by 2015, and the company has secured similar deals with O2, TalkTalk, and Everything Everywhere.

The committee's report argued that national security was neglected in favor of cost, as Huawei’s competitive pricing won out over British suppliers. Committee members were “shocked” to discover that ministers were not informed about the BT contract until a year after its completion. Sir Malcolm Rifkind commented, “A decision of such sensitivity and potential consequence should have been made by ministers. Poor clarity over procedures, responsibility, and powers has meant national security concerns have too often been overlooked.”

Ahead of the report’s release, some committee members claimed it would be heavily redacted due to the Treasury and Prime Minister's office’s concerns about deterring Chinese investment—a claim Downing Street denied. Tensions with China have persisted since Prime Minister David Cameron’s meeting with the Dalai Lama, which China saw as an affront to its sovereignty over Tibet.

The ISC’s previous annual report noted that a fifth of all advanced cyber-attacks against the UK appeared to be state-sponsored or come from organized crime, with China frequently named among the main culprits. Doubts have also been raised about whether Huawei’s interests in the UK are entirely commercial or also political.

A Huawei spokesman responded that the company “is willing to work with all governments in an open and transparent way to reduce cyber security risks together.” BT, in its statement, said, “Security is central to BT’s operations and will remain so. Our rigorous testing programs enable strong partnerships with many global suppliers, including Huawei, with whom we have worked constructively since 2005.”

Spink, part of Bruce's backroom team at Birmingham City, Wigan Athletic and Sunderland, has been linked to Villa.
But, although he hopes Bruce gets the job, at 58, Spink says he is too old.
"I'm racing towards 60. If I started kicking 100 balls a day, probably my leg would fall off," he told BBC WM.
"I'm pro Steve Bruce. And I'm happy to speak to him about all departments and would give him 100 per cent backing. But there's nothing in that story.
"I haven't spoken to Steve for some 18 months or so. I texted him when Hull City got to the FA Cup final, wished him all the best and got a nice reply. But I've since been busy with my business.
"And if he was to ask me, the answer would probably be no. That might come as a surprise to Villa fans, but a goalkeeping coach job is for a younger man."
Spink continued: "I'm fit and healthy, my business is running well and, if I took the job and it only lasted two years, I would lose business which it would take a while to get back.
"In any case, I enjoy being a fan. I know we haven't had much to jump and down about over the last two years, but I still enjoy going down there. To be sat watching the supporters and their passion has been a real eye opener."
Spink, who came off the bench against Bayern Munich in Rotterdam to help the club win the European Cup in 1982, was also part of the last Villa team to be promoted to the top flight under Graham Taylor in 1988.
After working as a goalkeeping coach for four clubs, he now runs his own white-van courier business, in Sutton Coldfield.
Pat Murphy, BBC Radio 5 live
"Villa's managerial recruitment team have completed their due diligence on the short list and will recommend a candidate in the next 24 hours to the club's owner, Dr Tony Xia.
"Dr Xia is in China on business and, unlike his sole decision in the summer to appoint Roberto Di Matteo, will be relying on the findings of chief executive Keith Wyness, former manager Brian Little and technical director Steve Round. But the final decision rests with Villa's owner.
"Steve Clarke, acting caretaker manager was interviewed on Monday, while the three discussions Bruce has had with Wyness over the past five days constitute a formal interview in the opinion of the recruitment team. But Bruce and Clarke are not the only candidates.
"Villa are also considering an approach to a manager contracted to another club, but need to be certain he is the one they definitely want before committing themselves.
"If they do and they fail in their overtures after asking for permission to speak to that manager, then Bruce and/or Clarke will know they were not first choice.
"There is no timescale on the appointment. Once the candidate agrees to join Villa, there will be detailed discussions over the contract, backroom staff and budgets. It may be a day or so before the successful applicant is publicly confirmed. But Bruce remains the odds-on favourite."
Subscribe to the BBC Sport newsletter to get our pick of news, features and video sent to your inbox.

“Barry, darling! Just the man,” Eleanor trilled, her voice a high-pitched hum of pre-party nerves. “Our guests will be arriving any moment, and I’ve completely forgotten the most crucial element.”

Barry, a man whose personal library consisted entirely of action movie DVDs, snapped to attention. “Just tell me where, Aunt E. I’m ready for anything.” He’d arrived early, eager to help his somewhat flustered aunt host her annual summer soirée.

Eleanor wrung her hands. “It’s in the pantry, on the very top shelf, behind the vintage port. Be careful, it’s rather weighty. I need the Magnum.”

Barry’s eyes widened. *The Magnum?* His mind instantly conjured images of Clint Eastwood, a glint of polished steel, the smell of cordite. Why his elegant aunt, a woman whose biggest thrill was a new orchid bloom, would possess such an item, let alone keep it in the pantry, was a mystery he was thrilled to unravel. “Right! Top shelf. Weighty. Got it.” He clenched his jaw, already picturing himself discreetly securing the weapon.

He entered the pantry, a dimly lit cavern of preserves, obscure condiments, and suspiciously labeled jars. He scanned the shelves, looking for anything that resembled a weapon. He pulled out a large rolling pin, inspecting it for hidden compartments. He moved a tower of tins, peering behind them. On the top shelf, amidst dusty bottles of forgotten liqueurs, he found nothing but a vast expanse of empty space. He even checked *under* the shelf, convinced it must be cleverly hidden.

Eleanor’s impatient voice echoed from the dining room. “Barry, what’s taking so long? Are you having trouble?”

“Just… securing the perimeter, Aunt E!” Barry yelled back, his voice strained as he pushed aside a precarious stack of antique biscuit tins. “Are you *sure* it’s up here? And is it… loaded?”

Eleanor appeared in the doorway, her brow furrowed. “Loaded? Barry, for heaven’s sake! What on earth are you talking about? I asked for the *wine*! The Chateau Lafite Rothschild, the *Magnum* bottle!” She pointed a perfectly manicured finger past him, towards a gloriously oversized bottle of red wine, clearly visible now that he wasn't treating the pantry like a hostile environment.

Barry slowly turned, his face a mosaic of dawning realization and profound embarrassment. He looked at the enormous bottle, then back at his aunt, then down at the dustpan he’d been subconsciously holding like a shield. “Oh. *That* Magnum. Right. Thought you meant… you know. For… home defense.” He picked up the bottle, cradling it in his arms. “This *is* weighty, though. Could probably knock someone out with it.”

Eleanor rolled her eyes, a fond smile finally gracing her lips. “Just try not to break it, dear. It’s far more valuable than any ‘home defense’ you’re imagining.”

Barry carried the colossal bottle to the dining table, a lingering smirk on his face. He decided not to mention the suspicious-looking broom he’d almost commandeered from the cleaning cupboard. Some secrets, he thought, were best left in the pantry.

I had to turn away from the Stars of Tzor before I could put it down. The name, of course, came from the sparkles that seemed to be swimming in the dark green gem. And then my attention returned. The wails of my son now rang sharply in my ears. I picked him up. One could easily get lost in reminiscing about the glorious past: the crew(now mostly dead or undead), the voyages(seven of them, the longest and easiest of which took a full three years because each time *Reverie* crowed it took 50 days away) and the wealth(seven times I returned full of treasure. The sixth time was to an empty house, the seventh made me realise I didn't feel it anymore). I patted Jake on the back and he calmed a little. Yeah, I just didn't feel it anymore. --- I am world-building, prompt by prompt. Curated stories go[here](http://fivenswrite.wordpress.com)

I rented this film in June, but didn't try to watch it until early July. I was on a plane heading to Michigan, and it wouldn't allow me to watch it without a wifi connection. This didn't make any sense to me since I had already downloaded the movie. Anyway I got home a week later and decided to watch it. I got about 1/4 of the way through and the movie stopped. No reason I could think of but it just stopped. I didn't contact Amazon due to the fact that I had rented it in June and didn't watch it until July. I just figured they would have assumed I had watched it and was now wanting something they felt I didn't deserve. If I am mistaken about how Amazon would have responded, I apologize. But I hope you understand why I felt the way I did. What little of the movie I saw was really good, but could only give it one star because I couldn't watch the rest of it.

Teens who received emotional intelligence training in school had improved scores on several measures of emotional well-being, including less anxiety, depression and social stress, according to a new study in the Journal of Adolescent Health. Improvements from the training lasted up to six months after the program ended.
Emotional intelligence, or EI, refers to the ability to accurately appraise, express and regulate emotion. The ability to handle emotions is essential for ones physical and psychological well-being, said study lead author Desiree Ruiz-Aranda. In addition, adolescents who are healthier mentally are healthier physically and may engage in fewer risky behaviors.
Emotional abilities need to begin as early as possible and preventive interventions should ideally be provided prior to developing significant symptoms, said Ruiz-Aranda.
The study examined about 300 Spanish students who participated in 24 one-hour training sessions during a two-year period. Teens in the study group participated in games, role-playing, art and discussion designed to promote the recognition of emotions in different contexts and to build empathy and emotional problem solving. When compared with a control group, these teens had lower measures of depression, social stress, and other negative feelings, even up to six months later.
Successful programs need to be catered to individual cultures and ages and be applied to everyday settings, Ruiz-Aranda said.
Schools have not done well in preparing students for life with things such as emotional intelligence training, said Gary Low, Ph.D., professor emeritus of education at Texas A&M University in Kingsville. He added that schools often dont help students learn to cope with fears, stresses, relationships and other areas of life.
We just hope that people learn that as they grow older, and I think weve not paid attention to developing a curriculum that would really help young people experience more success in life, he said.
More information: Ruiz-Aranda, D., et al. (2012). Short- and midterm effects of emotional intelligence training on adolescent mental health. Journal of Adolescent Health. doi: 10.1016/j.jadohealth.2012.02.003.

Turkish champions Fenerbahce have been withdrawn from the European Champions League group stage by the Turkish Football Federation (TFF) in relation to bribery and match-fixing allegations. In July more than 30 people were arrested and jailed over claims that several football matches, as many as 19, in the Turkish first and second divisions had been manipulated. The highest-profile figure arrested in the scandal was Fenerbahce's president Aziz Yildirim. Fenerbahce won last season's title on goal difference from Trabzonspor. But as the arrests mount the TFF decided to withdraw Fenerbahce before UEFA, European football's governing body, was forced to act on its behalf. "Regarding the heavy disciplinary sanctions both Fenerbahce and the Turkish Football Federation (TFF), which means Turkey might encounter, it is decided to ban Fenerbahce Sports Club from the UEFA Champions' League this season," read a statement posted on the TFF's official website. The move took place 24 hours before Thursday's group stage draw. European football's governing body acted swiftly to end speculation over who will replace Fenerbahce by announcing that Trabzonspor, who finished second in the Turkish league last season, will take the final group stage berth. After a meeting of UEFA's Emergency Committee on Wednesday evening it was also agreed that Spanish club Athletic Bilbao will no longer need to take part in a qualification match against Trabzonspor on Thursday and will go straight into the Europa League instead. "The panel considered that the Turkish Football Federation took the right decision to protect the game, fully in line with our zero-tolerance policy against match-fixing," said UEFA General Secretary Gianni Infantino in a statement posted on UEFA's website. "The Turkish Football Federation has shown with this decision that it takes its full responsibility in the fight against corruption."

Jack Sheldon does a fun duet with Anita O'Day in "I Told Ya I Love Ya Now Get Out." As of 2010, this track was posted on YouTube in a witty slide show - search for it there by song title.Jack Sheldon is most widely known as the voice of Louie The Lightning Bug in electrical safety announcements on TV, but his genuine legacy is as a jazz musician. Jack Sheldon also toured with Benny Goodman and Anita O'Day in 1959. Since Roy Eldridge wasn't on that tour, Jack Sheldon performed Anita O'Day's hit "Let Me Off Uptown" with her. It is preserved in an air check and available on "YouBetcha"You Betcha.Oh, and I give this four stars so as not to slight 20 other albums by her that deserve five. Anita O'Day never was mediocre. The Avid collectionFour Classic Albumsis a great place to start and a genuine bargain.

**Excellent Portable Solution for Emergency Tire Inflation**

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (5/5 stars)

I purchased this portable tire inflator after getting stranded with a flat tire, and it has been a game-changer for my peace of mind. The rechargeable battery feature is incredibly convenient - no more searching for power outlets or dealing with messy car adapter cords.

**Pros:**
- Compact and lightweight design fits easily in my trunk
- Powerful enough to inflate car tires from flat to full in about 8-10 minutes
- Clear digital display shows precise PSI readings
