Arthur Ransome’s Swallows and Amazons series is a timeless celebration of childhood adventure, imagination, and freedom. Set in the English Lake District and other picturesque locales, the books follow the Walker and Blackett children—better known as the Swallows and Amazons—as they embark on imaginative voyages, rivalries, and daring escapades. Ransome’s writing captures the wonder of the natural world and the ingenuity of young explorers. The stories are charmingly detailed, blending realism with fantasy in a way that respects children’s intelligence and sense of independence. Though rooted in the early twentieth century, the series' themes of friendship, discovery, and respect for nature remain compelling and relevant. Swallows and Amazons stands as a beloved classic that continues to inspire generations of readers and adventurers alike.

Whether you're super slim or ultra curvy, cellulite is the bane of so many women's lives. In today's beauty-obsessed culture, where social media feeds are filled with airbrushed perfection and magazine covers showcase flawless skin, the pressure to achieve smooth, dimple-free legs and thighs has never been greater. From detox diets to firming lotions and potions, there is no shortage of gimmicks out there to help banish the bumps. But the latest intriguing product claiming to have cellulite-busting powers is a pair of caffeine-infused pants that promise to break down cellulite while you wear them.

Sexy slimmers: The new range of underwear from a French lingerie brand is infused with caffeine, which it claims beats cellulite.

In the heart of France's fashion capital, where chic boutiques line cobblestone streets and the latest innovations in beauty and style emerge from centuries-old ateliers, French lingerie brand Simone Pérèle have launched a shapewear range called Top Model, which aims to be sexy yet effective at fighting the orange peel. The lace and Lycra lingerie contains microfibre microencapsulated with slimming agents - the main one being caffeine - as well as vitamin E, retinol and aloé vera.

The brand claim that the shapewear, which lasts for 100 washes, has body-sculpting actions which flatten the stomach and slim the hips as well as lifting the buttocks. The slimming agents are microencapsulated into the fabric which are then released upon contact with skin, creating a subtle warming sensation as the active ingredients penetrate through the dermis during everyday wear.

It sounds too good to be true - but in an independent study conducted in controlled laboratory conditions over 28 days, the firm found that 63 per cent of women wearing the shapewear experienced a reduction of cellulite.

Flattering: The brand claim that the shapewear has body sculpting actions, which flatten the stomach and slim the hips as well as lifting the buttocks.

While caffeine is better known for boosting flagging energy levels in busy morning routines and late-night work sessions, a range of new products like this maintains that it can help boost blood flow and banish cellulite. In professional dermatology clinics across the country, where advanced treatments and cutting-edge technology meet traditional skincare wisdom, experts are weighing in on this trend.

Nataliya Robinson, of Nataliya Robinson Skin Clinic, speaking from her pristine treatment room surrounded by state-of-the-art equipment and shelves lined with medical-grade skincare products, said: 'First of all there is no known treatment for cellulite than totally eliminates the condition.

'My opinion on cellulite is that it is a disorder of connective tissues (the tissue is damaged and cannot support the deepest layers of the skin), sluggish lymphatic circulation, hormonal problem-estrogen activity.

'As poor circulation aggravate cellulite then application of caffeine, gotu kola and fennel may help to increase the sluggish circulation and by wearing on daily basis shape wear (or support hose) will support the connective tissue especially during the exercise to help encourage healing.'

Pretty effective: In an independent study over 28 days they found that 63 per cent of women wearing the shapewear experienced a reduction of cellulite.

A man walked into a nail salon and, after a heated argument with his estranged wife—who manages the salon—poured a flammable liquid over himself and set himself on fire. The 46-year-old, whose name hasn't been released, is currently being treated for severe burns. Thanks to quick-thinking staff, the flames were put out before anyone else was hurt.

One customer, Sherry Skipper-Spurgeon, described how the manager, known only as Lina, sobbed in her arms for an hour after the shocking event at Creative Nails and Spa in Costa Mesa, Orange County. According to Sherry, the man appeared very upset and was shouting at his wife in Vietnamese. Lina urged her employees and customers to leave the salon so she could speak with him alone, but the situation quickly turned tragic.

Before anyone could react, the man poured the liquid over himself and sat in the puddle. "Next thing you know, someone says, 'He's got a lighter!'" Sherry remembers. "As I was speaking to the fire department, I saw a fireball—his head was completely covered in flames, followed by a cloud of smoke."

Lina’s brother, who was there helping with some repairs, used a fire extinguisher to put out the flames, and the rest of the staff helped using towels. The couple, who have three children, are going through a divorce. "This is absolutely devastating for her," Sherry said. "She was at a loss for how to tell her kids about what happened." 

The man is being treated at Western Medical Center in Santa Ana, and no one else was injured.

**Detective Sergeant Reinstated After Conviction Overturned Following Discovery of Key Witness**

A decorated Detective Sergeant has been reinstated to the City Police Department after a years-long criminal conviction was overturned due to the emergence of a key witness whose testimony cast significant doubt on the original verdict.

Detective Sergeant Marcus Ellery, 42, was convicted of evidence tampering and obstruction of justice in 2021 following an internal investigation into the handling of a high-profile robbery case. Ellery has consistently maintained his innocence throughout the proceedings and subsequent appeals.

However, the case took a dramatic turn last month when defense attorneys located a previously unknown witness, James Weldon, who provided testimony contradicting the prosecution's timeline and alleging that a crucial piece of evidence was mishandled before coming into Ellery's possession.

In a hearing last week, the Court of Appeal accepted Weldon's testimony and concluded that the original conviction could not stand, citing "a fundamental miscarriage of justice." The court also noted significant errors in the initial investigation, including the failure to interview all relevant witnesses.

Interim Police Chief Rebecca Tan confirmed on Monday that Ellery would return to duty effective immediately. "Detective Sergeant Ellery has served this department with distinction," Tan said. "We welcome his return and remain committed to identifying and correcting the factors that led to this wrongful conviction."

Ellery, who has spent more than two years fighting to clear his name, expressed relief in a brief statement outside the courthouse. "I'm grateful to have been vindicated. I look forward to returning to my work serving this community," he said.

The City Police Department has launched a review of internal investigative procedures to prevent similar incidents in the future. Meanwhile, the District Attorney’s office is undertaking an inquiry into how Weldon's testimony was previously overlooked.

The source said troops had entered one building and were planning to push cautiously through the rest of the huge compound amid fears of explosives.
IS militants are believed to have fled to the north-east of the city.
The government has been trying to retake Ramadi for weeks.
The mainly Sunni Arab city, about 55 miles (90km) west of Baghdad, fell to IS in May, and was seen as an embarrassing defeat for the army.
In recent days, troops have been picking their way through booby-trapped streets and buildings as they pushed towards the city centre, seizing several districts on the way.
They were reported to be within a few hundred yards of the former provincial administrative headquarters on Saturday.
When sniper fire from the compound stopped, and aerial surveillance detected no human activity, a group of Iraqi soldiers moved in, the source said.
They reportedly entered what used to be the city's department of health, housing a blood bank.
The Iraqi military believes the militants have headed north-east; fighting meanwhile is reported to be under way to the south-west of the compound.
The operation to recapture Ramadi began in early November, but has made slow progress, mainly because the government chose not to use the powerful Shia-dominated paramilitary force that helped it regain the northern city of Tikrit, to avoid increasing sectarian tensions.
Concern remains for the plight of hundreds of families who have been trapped on the frontline, the BBC's Thomas Fessy reports from Baghdad.

By . Daily Mail Reporter . PUBLISHED: . 13:38 EST, 29 January 2014 . | . UPDATED: . 14:03 EST, 29 January 2014 . New chapter: Boston bombing victim Jeff Bauman is releasing a book about his experiences in April . Boston . bombing survivor Jeff Bauman has written a book that will be published a week before the first anniversary . of the deadly blast in April. His publisher revealed the cover of the book, 'Stronger', on Twitter on Tuesday night, ahead of Bauman's appearance at the State of the Union speech. After heading to the Capitol for the event, Bauman tweeted: 'I wore pants for the first time in 9 . months! And I . also met the president.' The cover shows him standing on two prosthetic legs with 'Boston' emblazoned across his t-shirt. The New Hampshire native, 27, lost both of his lower legs in the April . 15 attack. An iconic photo taken moments after the blast showed Bauman gray-faced with . nothing but exposed bone below his knees. But he has since overcome unimaginable adversities to remain positive and Grand Central Publishing will publish the book about his experiences on April 8, CBS Boston reported. Bauman, who was working to pay off his . student loans at the deli counter in Costco before the life-changing . blast, worked with co-writer Bret Witter on the book. A description on Amazon explains: 'Jeff Bauman woke up on Tuesday, April 16th 2013 and he had no legs. Fighter: On Tuesday, Bauman, right, appeared beside Carlos Arredondo, who helped save his life after the bombings, at the State of the Union address at the U.S. Capitol . Back on his feet: Bauman walked on his prosethetics, left, while Arredondo, waved at Michelle Obama, right . 'Just . thirty hours prior, Jeff was surrounded by revelry at the finish line . of the 2013 Boston Marathon. The first bomb went off at his feet as he . awaited his girlfriend’s finish. 'When Jeff awoke days later from hours . of surgery, rather than take stock of his now completely altered life, . Jeff ripped out his breathing tube and tried to speak. He couldn’t. 'Jeff . asked for a pad and paper and he wrote down seven words, "Saw the guy. Looked right at me", setting off one of the biggest manhunts in the . country’s history and beginning his own brave road to recovery.' It adds that the book aims to inspire people by sharing his experiences and his mission to walk again. 'Jeff will show the terrorists that they . accomplished nothing with their act of cowardice and prove to the entire . world what Boston Strong really means,' it reads. Life-changing: Carlos Arredondo pinches Bauman's femoral artery as they rush from the blast site moments after the April 15 bombing in Boston. Bauman lost both lower legs and much of his hearing . Battle: He lost both of his lower legs in the blast and has since been fitted with prosthetic limbs . Bauman appeared at the State of the . Union speech alongside the man who helped save him, Carlos Arredondo. The men have remained close and Bauman was pictured with his arm around the man's shoulder. Bauman went to the marathon waiting to . see his girlfriend cross the finish line when was close to one of the . explosive-filled backpacks allegedly placed by the Tsarnaev brothers. He . was thrown by the force of the blast and opened his eyes to find . himself in a pool of blood. He saw one of his friends look at his legs . in horror and looked down to see his limbs were shredded. He . underwent an emergency amputation to remove his legs at the knees, and . two days later, flesh was amputated until four inches above the knees. Support: He is pictured with his girlfriend, Erin Hurley, who had been running the marathon that day . Authorities have praised Bauman, who . also lost much of his hearing in the blast, as key to stopping Tsarnaev . and his brother Dzhokhar after he helped identify them as the suspects. 'Even in the ambulance ride I was . trying to say something, trying to say, like, "I knew who did it, I knew . what went on",' he said in an earlier interview with NBC News. 'And . then I think they were kind of thrown back by that.' Bauman . added that he is now looking forward and, while he could wonder how . differently he could have acted that day to avoid the blast, there is no . point. Instead, he is focusing on his recovery, which has impressed doctors. 'It's insane,' he said. 'I'm a quick healer. They were calling me 'Wolverine'.'

I am a medium framed person, but I ordered the large just in case they ran small. I thought, then, I'd just have to wash it to shrink it down. That wasn't the case at all This shirt is pretty flimsy and the large is more like a medium/small. That said, it looks decent and if you layer other shirts under it, you won't notice the wind coming through.I have received compliments about how it looks and so forth, but I do not wear the shirts often (I bought the grey and the blue ones). For the price I paid, I suppose I should not complain. I recommend buying these to wear in the early fall or during the mild spring months.

The meeting was happenstance, though in the participant's minds, it was clearly ordained. The men, both bedecked in the raiments of their respective holy order, easily spotted each other from fifty yards away as they walked in opposite directions on the bustling sidewalk. The din and bustle of the Festival happening on the other side of the street merely served to hasten their stride towards one another. When finally they came within earshot, their paired calling of "Hail, Brother!" caused both to elicit a genuine smile, and more than a few alarmed glances from onlookers. They strode towards each other, the tall, lanky one, T, making up most of the distance towards his diminutive counterpart, S. The two transferred their books to their off-hand, then clasped hands with each other in a warm and hearty handshake. "How goes it, Brother S?""It goes well indeed, Brother T. It warms my heart to see that you have once again decided to grace the heathens with your presence!""Indeed, especially since we have been graced with such fine weather with which to proselytize to them! Perhaps, today we shall even gather a few more wayward lambs to our flock." S sneered and swept his gaze across the street to the Festival and its many revelers. Mumbling under his breath, he then turned his gaze back to T's massive tome, nearly at eye level for the petite priest. "Still in as good a condition as it was in the Orary?" "Indeed it is, Brother S, for it has nary left my grasp for this past decade. I've used it to bring peace to many a troubled soul." "Peace is not what this is all about"came out, too muted for T to hear,"But you are looking well and that is all that I care about." "Thank you, Brother S, and I trust that you have been doing well these past few years?" "Indeed, indeed. But, enough about me, my resolve has been redoubled by your presence! Come, Brother, let us wade among the Fallen and bring the fear of Righteousness to their hearts!" T merely flashed a toothy smile, and turned towards the multitude of revelers and colorful tents. With a deep breath, he began one of the ancient chants of his order. Vairyo Ay Oh Ah Hom Va Ya Ray Vairyo Ay Rah Jerhume Brunnen Gee S left his side, and, holding his holy book above his head, crossed the street towards the multitude. Those immediately near to him were quite startled when be began bellowing "You who live in Sin and Squalor, you who live outside of the Light of our Lord, you shall burn forever in the Fiery Pits!" The crowd parted to let the dwarfish man pass through, unmolested. Most ignored his tirade of hatred and derision. More than a few young children could be heard asking "Why is that man so short?"

A fossilized brachiopod discovered in Carboniferous rock formations from Ardclough, Ireland, dating to approximately 330 million years ago. The specimen measures 10 cm in actual width and 5 cm in height. While brachiopods are frequently mistaken for the dual-shelled 'sea shell' creatures known as bivalves that we find on modern beaches, they represent an entirely distinct animal group. More than 100 genera (species groups) of brachiopods inhabit oceans worldwide today, with their fossil history extending back to the Cambrian period, roughly 560 million years ago.

Their protective hard shell shields the soft body parts and consists of two shells termed 'valves' - a dorsal and a ventral valve. This specimen has undergone preparation where the dorsal valve was removed to expose the fossil's interior. During the brachiopod's lifetime, these coiled spirals would have been encased in soft tissues and delicate tentacles, forming a feeding structure called the lophophore.

Backyard composting is the most effective and environmentally-friendly way to manage the organic "waste" your home produces, transforming your "trash" into "treasure". A single household can compost over 500 kg of kitchen scraps, low-quality papers and yard trimmings per year, resulting in over 100 kg of free fertilizer!
Almost two-thirds of North Shore households compost their organics, keeping almost 9,000 tonnes from curbside collection for disposal. But still, 25% of the 24,000 tonnes of garbage picked up from North Shore houses could be backyard composted.
Beyond these impressive statistics, actively managing organics in your yard reduces pollution, our carbon footprint and disposal costs, while improving our environment and keeping organics in the ecological cycle. It also reduces your need to store smelly garbage indoors until the morning of collection and reduces hauling heavy waste to the curb.

So, how does this incredible transformation from "trash" to "treasure" happen? It all comes down to the tireless effort of bacteria within your compost bin. All you need to do is create the right conditions for these bacteria to thrive, and they'll create rich, earthy-smelling compost out of your home and garden scraps.

To ensure these beneficial bacteria thrive and perform their magic, you must provide them with the right "food": Nitrogen and Carbon. Nitrogen provides the protein for bacteria to grow and multiply; Carbon provides the energy bacteria need to do the work of composting. For every bucket of kitchen scraps or lawn clippings, add an equal volume of fallen leaves or paper products. Always cover "green" material with a 3-4" layer of "brown" material. Click here for a list of "green" and "brown" material. For more information on "Brown" material, view our video on "Carbon Sources".
Beyond food, another crucial condition for successful composting is oxygen. Oxygen-loving (aerobic) bacteria transform organic material into compost without bad odours. Add oxygen at least once a week using a Wingdigger™, pitchfork or strong stick. The more you aerate, the faster you will have finished compost. If the contents of your bin get too compacted, anaerobic bacteria will ferment your food scraps, creating rotten-eggs or ammonia odours. Click here for more information on aerating your compost bin.
Finally, just like all living organisms, these hardworking bacteria need water to survive and thrive. The contents of your compost bin should have the moisture level of a damp, wrung-out sponge. The fresh "green" material you add to your bin will usually provide enough water; however, if your bin is in the sun, you are using a lot of dry brown material or it is a particularly hot summer, you may need to water to the contents of the bin to keep the bacteria and other decomposers alive.
These three things—food (Nitrogen & Carbon), oxygen, and water—will ensure a successful compost bin; if any one is missing, conditions will not be optimal for oxygen-loving bacteria and your compost bin may slow down, get slimy or smell bad.

While often associated with composting, it's important to clarify that bacteria, not worms, do most of the transformative work in your compost bin. Other larger, visible decomposers (such as worms, wood bugs, slugs, snails and ants) may be present in your compost ecosystem, but are not necessary for success. Worms do physically break down organic matter, but when your compost pile heats up, worms will head for cooler pastures until the pile matures and cools. In fact, a mass of worms in a backyard compost bin can actually be a sign that you should be providing more energy (“browns”) to speed your compost up; a very active compost bin will be warm and typically won't have an abundance of worms, as they prefer cooler conditions.

For more in-depth guidance and to see these principles in action, view our video "How Composting Works". Whether you're a beginner or a seasoned pro, you're guaranteed to learn something new and how to:
*   minimize common composting problems,
*   improve your finished compost,
*   and take less garbage to the curb.
Click here to learn more and also find information on how to set up and use the Green Can program and create less waste!

when we first connected, it was a fortuitous accident. she had sent her late-night message to the wrong Theo, and I—expecting spam—read on anyway. Her question, “Could you ever love a murderer?”, was so odd I laughed in the way you do when you’re suddenly alone at midnight and startled.

I wrote back, “Depends on who got murdered.”

She replied instantly: “My former self, I suppose. She was quite dreadful.”

We exchanged oddly confessional notes for weeks, skipping the pleasantries, detailing the precise moments our lives had split and curved and stitched, tracing everything back to convenient fictions we’d told ourselves. She claimed her name was Bird, a name I later realized held, for her, no history at all.

In July, we met for ice cream. “I had to see if you had wings,” I joked.

She lifted one shoulder with a secretive smile. “Only one.”

We sat for a long time, talking about second chances and how, sometimes, the luckiest things begin when everything else goes, gloriously, wrong.

Hendrick's superb run teed up Walters to score on 69 minutes as the Irish strengthened their hold on third place in Group D.
"Jeff was absolutely magnificent. He has that ability and it was great to see Walters scoring," said O'Neill.
"We didn't play well in the first half but then there was a great turnaround."
With Scotland losing 3-2 at home to Germany, the Republic are now four points ahead of Gordon Strachan's side with two rounds of fixtures remaining next month.
Media playback is not supported on this device
The Republic host leaders Germany and visit second-placed Poland, while Scotland welcome the Poles before visiting Gibraltar in their final game.
The third-placed team with the best qualifying record from the nine groups qualifies automatically for next summer's tournament in France, with the other eight facing a two-legged play-off.
O'Neill says his side will "have a go" as they aim to at least secure a play-off spot.
"We need to find a win in two matches," he said. "We'll have to play better for the whole game.
"Germany, with respect, are not only the best team in the group, they are world champions, and I'd say at this minute, possibly even the best side in Europe, so we will have to naturally do better for 90 minutes in the game."
O'Neill was delighted by his team's second-half endeavour.
"We found a way to win the second half," he said. "We were very tentative in the first half - I'm not sure why we should be.
"The players came on very, very strongly in the second half and we deserved to get the goal and we deserved to win the game."
O'Neill praised Derby midfielder Hendrick, who jinked past three challenges before squaring for Stoke forward Walters to notch his seventh international goal.
"It was a great, great run and to keep a little bit of composure as well with the final ball coming in that somebody is going to come in on top of that as Jonathan Walters did."

What would eventually become Hurricane Darby developed about 2800 miles east-southeast of Hawaii on July 26, 2004. Moving to the northwest and then west, Darby ultimately reached peak intensity of 105 kt (120 mph) on July 29 (satellite). Shortly thereafter, Darby moved into an area of cooler waters and stronger upper level winds, both are unfavorable for a hurricane to sustain itself. Just two days later, Darby was nothing more than just a low level swirl of clouds and showers with winds of only 20 to 25 knots as it was about 1000 miles east of the state (satellite). This area of moisture was being pushed westward toward Hawaii by the tradewind flow.
An upper level low was located just to the northwest of the state, and this had made the atmosphere slightly unstable over Hawaii. As the remnant swirl of Darby moved closer to the unstable region, thunderstorms began to develop. The first round of thunderstorms occurred just north and east of the Big Island on August 2 (satellite). That night, additional showers and thunderstorms formed across parts of the Big Island, particularly the normally dry Kona side (satellite, radar). Rainfall amounts of 2 to 5 inches over a few hours were reported, and this led to flooding and closures of several roads.
During the day on August 3, the remnants moved across Maui (satellite, radar). Locally heavy rainfall occurred on the southeast flank of Haleakala, otherwise the development of heavy and widespread rainfall was limited. However that changed during the night as the remnants approached Oahu. Once again thunderstorms developed, affecting the entire island of Oahu and dumping several inches of rain in a few hours (satellite, radar). A few streams overflowed their banks and minor landslides occurred, both resulting in some road closures. The main effect was significant ponding of water on the roads, which impacted the morning rush hour. By mid day August 4, Darby's remnants began to affect Kauai (satellite), but the bulk of the heaviest rainfall stayed south of the island, thus sparing the island from any heavy rains, with the exception at the normally wet Mount Waialeale.
Heavy rains during the summer are very unusual, especially on the drier leeward sides of the islands. In fact, the heavy rainfall was a record in Honolulu. The Honolulu Airport recorded a total of 3.06 inches from the storm, 2.92 inches which fell on August 4. This was the heaviest one day rainfall for the entire month of August. The previous record being 2.10 inches that fell on August 6, 1959 as Hurricane Dot was passing by. This heavy rainfall also pushed the monthly rainfall total at Honolulu above the previously wettest August since 1949. The previous record was again in 1959 when 3.08 inches fell for the entire month. Normally the Honolulu Airport gets only one-half inch of rain during August and 3 inches during the entire dry season (May through September).

**A Comprehensive Guide to International Wealth Management**

Terry Neal's "Offshore Investing" delivers a thorough examination of international investment strategies that goes well beyond the typical surface-level treatment of this complex subject. Neal, drawing from his extensive experience in international finance, provides readers with both the theoretical framework and practical tools necessary to navigate offshore markets effectively.

The book's greatest strength lies in its balanced approach to a topic often clouded by misconceptions. Neal methodically addresses both the legitimate benefits and genuine risks of offshore investing, steering clear of the sensationalism that plagues much of the literature in this field. His discussion of tax implications is particularly valuable, offering clear explanations of compliance requirements that many investors overlook to their detriment.

Neal's coverage of different offshore jurisdictions is impressively detailed, providing insights into the regulatory environments, economic stability, and investment opportunities across various countries. The comparative analysis of different financial centers helps readers make informed decisions about where to establish their offshore presence.

However, the book occasionally suffers from dense technical language that may overwhelm newcomers to international investing. Some sections would benefit from more concrete examples to illustrate complex concepts. Additionally, given the rapidly evolving nature of international tax law and regulations, readers should supplement this information with current professional advice.

The chapters on due diligence and risk assessment are particularly strong, offering practical checklists and red flags that can save investors from costly mistakes. Neal's emphasis on working with qualified professionals and maintaining proper documentation reflects real-world wisdom that only comes from experience.

Overall, "Offshore Investing" serves as an excellent foundation for anyone seriously considering international investment strategies. While it requires careful study and should be paired with professional guidance, it provides the knowledge base necessary to approach offshore investing with confidence and appropriate caution.

**Rating: 4/5 stars**

His terms were simple. No verification to obtain, no message to send. A quick bullet from a distance. Half upfront, half upon completion. The target was weak, and the singular object of the wrath of a man willing to pay any price to get rid of him. Reasons had washed away into the morass of time, so now only the seething, perfect hatred remained. But according to the man's station he could not himself act upon his hatred. Compelled by circumstance, the man sought out an assassin, a consummate professional in his field who he was assured could fulfill the task without hesitation. He presented his proposal to the assassin via payphone. He wired the down payment, and tried to put the whole matter out of his mind. The target sat in a corner bar as he was wont to do on muggy days. In front of him on a cafe table sat a frosty glass slowly coming to room temperature. He sat and watched as his younger compatriots laughed swapping their stories and drinking deeply. A twinge of envy brought his eyelids together as he remembered sitting in their seats before a million compromises brought him to where he sat now. At the same moment some hundred yards away the assassin drew his eyes together. His view was unimpaired, windage minimal. The perfect shot. The assassin exhaled and rested his finger against the trigger. A glare on the bar table drew the attention of the target to the middle distance and a reflection from a rooftop. As a boy he had served in the army, he knew what this meant. And he smiled, content in the knowledge he would never have to pay the other half.

More than 20 years after its approval, aspartame, the artificial sweetener found in everything from soft drinks to toothpaste, is the center of renewed debate over its potential to cause cancer. In an attempt to simulate a range of human intakes, researchers at the European Ramazzini Foundation of Oncology and Environmental Sciences fed 1800 rats varying amounts of aspartame. Upon their deaths, the rats’ organs were examined for microscopic changes.
The results were not good. According to a study published in the March issue of Environmental Health Perspectives (the peer-reviewed journal of the National Institute of Environmental Health Sciences), the rats--especially females--showed a significant increase in leukemia and cancers in the kidney and urethra. The effects appeared in rats with a daily aspartame intake equivalent to 20 milligrams per kilogram body weight (mg/kg). That equates roughly to four or five bottles of diet soda for a 150-pound person. The acceptable daily limit is 50mg/kg in the United States and 40mg/kg in Europe.
Commercially sold as Nutra-Sweet and Equal and the key ingredient in diet soft drinks, aspartame is a half-billion-dollar industry. Accordingly, the results of the study have been hotly disputed. Both the FDA and European Food Safety Authority are reviewing the study but neither have cautioned against the sweetener.—Adam Raymond

It has been closed for a number of months after an inspection revealed the building had structural problems.
The centre is located within the showgrounds, next to Derry City Football Club, on the Lone Moor Road. It will now be temporarily relocated.
Demolition costs will be part of the multi-million pound Brandywell redevelopment project.
Work on the redevelopment is due to begin in July with Derry City FC hoping to have a new stadium in place for the 2017 season.
Derry City and Strabane District Council said the health inspection showed the facility would require "regular intervention" and result in "partial and full closure of the facility" on a frequent basis.
A complete refurbishment of the centre was estimated to cost between Â£500,000 and Â£750,000.
A partial refurbishment was estimated to cost roughly half that.
As a result, the council's health and community committee agreed on Thursday that demolition of the premises was the most cost-effective measure.
Karen McFarland, Health and Community Director, said the demolition of the sports centre will have benefits including "improved location for kennelling and weighing room facilities, the relocation of Lone moor Road access to improve parking and enhancements to the dog track".
The location for the new sports centre will be presented to council at a later date.

end-125 hours: i didn't agree to a countdown install. damned. The numbers pulsed a sickly green behind my eyes, overlaid on every surface. Not on a screen, but *in* my vision, a new, uninvited feature of the neural implant I’d had shoved into my temporal lobe last cycle. I’d signed off on the standard corporate package: enhanced cognitive processing, direct data feeds, integrated comms. Nowhere in the eighty-three pages of fine print, read under duress of a looming project deadline, did it mention a forced termination timer. Or a system purge. Or whatever 'end' was supposed to signify. My stomach churned. A quick mental query to the implant’s diagnostic suite only returned ‘ERROR: Unauthorized Access Attempt. System Integrity Compromised.’ My own system, compromised by its own creators. I slammed my fist on the plasti-steel desk, the impact sending a jolt up my arm, but the countdown didn’t even flicker. One hundred twenty-five hours until whatever hellish upgrade or deletion awaited. I didn’t agree. I never agreed.

**Why I switched from the tiny Honda filter to the bigger Denso 150-1013**


I used to use the standard Honda oil filter that Acura recommends, but I've completely switched to the Denso 150-1013 - and here's why.


**Size matters when it comes to filters**

The Honda filter is surprisingly small, while the Denso is noticeably larger. Think of it like this: would you rather have a small coffee filter or a bigger one when making coffee? The bigger filter simply has more room to catch stuff and lets liquid flow through more easily.


**About that little hole in the wrapper**

Some people worry about a tiny vent hole in the plastic wrapper - it's about the size of a hole punch mark. But here's the thing: Denso is a massive company that makes filters for millions of cars. That little hole is there on purpose to let out moisture during manufacturing so the filter doesn't rust in storage. They're not going to risk their reputation by shipping defective filters.


**My bottom line**

I just bought another Denso 150-1013 for my next oil change. More filter material means better cleaning and smoother oil flow - it's really that simple. After trying both, I'm done with those tiny Honda filters for good.


**The takeaway:** Sometimes the aftermarket option actually makes more sense than the original part.

**Keyboard Cruiser Justice**

Ezra tapped the "Enter" key, watching the screen flicker with new complaints. Pixelburg’s digital streets never slept, not since the city handed out Keyboards of Civic Duty to every citizen—tools and weapons rolled into one.

Tonight’s case: troll infestation in the Library Archives. The trolls, code-glitched, spawned spammy haikus and pop-up riddles, jamming up the public files on ancient music theory. Ezra holstered his virtual stylus, lowering his replicated shades.

“Dispatch, patch me in—Archives, aisle three-point-oh.”

A coppery avatar flashed—Librarian Bot, circuits rumpled, eyes whirring. *They’re eating the Dewey Decimal indexes!*

Ezra deployed his macros, launching a volley of anti-spam command lines: /mute_troll42, /quarantine_haiku, /undo_chaos. The trolls fizzled, words devolving to jumbled punctuation.

One last rogue stood: a specter composed entirely of keyboard emojis, grinning wide.

Ezra grinned wider. “Justice, meet QWERTY.”

He unleashed the ultimate combo: Ctrl+Alt+Smite.

The archives cleared, pixel dust drifting across the restored files. Librarian Bot beamed.

“All in a night’s work,” Ezra said, logging off to the low hum of satisfied servers. In Pixelburg, justice was just a few keystrokes away.

Wanting to broaden my musical tastes and generally enjoying jazz, I gave this group and CD a try because Amazon called it an essential recording. I wouldn't agree. I'll be reselling it soon. Even after many listens, it just doesn't impress me. I enjoy music that has clear melodies, well-thought-out arrangements, and interesting harmonies, even if they're a bit unconventional. This album, however, generally falls short. To me, it's a bit like Pat Metheny's "The Way Up" (which I own but rarely listen to). Someone might appreciate it for its experimental nature and for the occasional recognizable melody, but it's definitely not something I'd consider essential listening. I wonder if Martin, Medeski, or Wood own any Frank Zappa albums. If not, they could learn a thing or two about being innovative in jazz from "Jazz From Hell," though that's another album I don't and won't own myself.

micrornas (mirnas) have recently been recognized as key trans-acting factors in a vast array of biological processes, fundamentally reshaping our understanding of gene regulation. These small, endogenous, non-coding RNA molecules, typically 19-25 nucleotides in length, primarily function to regulate gene expression at the post-transcriptional level. Synthesized from longer primary transcripts, processed by Drosha and Dicer enzymes, and then loaded into the RNA-induced silencing complex (RISC), miRNAs exert their influence by binding to complementary sequences, usually within the 3' untranslated regions (3' UTRs) of target messenger RNAs (mRNAs). This binding typically leads to either translational repression or degradation of the target mRNA, thereby finely tuning the protein output of hundreds, if not thousands, of genes.

The ubiquitous nature and potent regulatory capabilities of miRNAs underscore their critical roles in virtually all aspects of cellular life. They are indispensable for proper development, cell differentiation, proliferation, apoptosis, metabolism, and immune responses. For instance, specific miRNA profiles dictate the differentiation fate of stem cells, while others are essential for the healthy functioning of the nervous, cardiovascular, and immune systems. Their ability to simultaneously regulate multiple genes within a particular signaling pathway makes them powerful orchestrators of complex biological networks.

The discovery of miRNAs has profound implications for understanding disease pathogenesis. Aberrant miRNA expression or function has been implicated in a wide spectrum of human diseases, most notably cancer, but also cardiovascular disorders, neurological diseases, autoimmune conditions, and infectious diseases. In oncology, miRNAs can act as oncogenes (oncomiRs) or tumor suppressors, promoting or inhibiting tumor growth, metastasis, and drug resistance, respectively. Their dysregulation contributes to the hallmarks of cancer, from uncontrolled proliferation to evasion of cell death. Similarly, specific miRNAs have been found to regulate processes central to heart failure, neurodegeneration, and inflammatory responses.

This burgeoning understanding has propelled miRNAs to the forefront of diagnostic and therapeutic research. Their stability in biofluids, such as blood, urine, and cerebrospinal fluid, makes them attractive candidates for non-invasive biomarkers for early disease detection, prognosis, and monitoring treatment response. Furthermore, the capacity to modulate miRNA activity—either by inhibiting overexpressed oncogenic miRNAs with anti-miRs (antagomiRs) or by restoring tumor-suppressive miRNAs with miRNA mimics—presents novel therapeutic avenues. While challenges remain in targeted delivery and minimizing off-target effects, ongoing clinical trials are exploring the potential of miRNA-based therapeutics for various diseases. The continued exploration of the miRNA landscape promises to yield further insights into fundamental biology and translate into innovative strategies for disease diagnosis and treatment.

Trevor Gray lost his job with Nottinghamshire Police after he was sentenced to eight years in jail in May 2012 for attacking a woman in her home. However, his conviction was later overturned, and at a new trial in February, he was found not guilty.

Now, a Police Appeals Tribunal has ordered that Mr. Gray, who had worked for the force for 25 years, should get his job back. He will return to his previous position as detective sergeant, and it will be as if he was never dismissed—his years of service will continue from before.

The board explained that they accepted his appeal because new evidence had come to light that was not available at the first hearing, and this new evidence could have changed the outcome.

Mr. Gray had been found guilty in 2012 of rape, attempted rape, and sexual assault, and given an eight-year prison sentence. He appealed, arguing that police did not find an important witness who could have helped his defense. His wife, Alison Gray, managed to find this witness, and their testimony led to his conviction being overturned.

The Independent Police Complaints Commission is now looking into how Nottinghamshire Police handled the investigation and whether they did enough to find and contact important witnesses.

Necronomicon Journel Entry#1 Why is cursing people so hard? Did I waste 2,000 years of my immortal life in the Black Arts Academy, just to make people feel better? That's not what I want to do! My family is praised and known as the most successful witching family in the area, yet i'm the baby of the family, and the failure. Next i'll be told that i'm secretly a mortal. Ughhhhh. There was this teenager, smoking a cigarette outside the grocery store I was shopping at. I wanted to make him develop lung cancer, for my own amusement. But when I tried using the spell, all it did was turn the cigarette into a candy cane! Sure it was a inconvenience for him, but that's not what I wanted! Another time today, there was this couple arguing outside my apartment complex. I wanted to curse them into deciding to kill each other. But again, when I decided to cast the spell, all it did was make them hug and set aside their differences. I'm no good witch! I'm supposed to be the evil one! ME! I need to speak with the High Council, see if my wand is malfunctioning. I won't let myself be the laughingstock of all witches everywhere!

Two Niyi Adeolokun scores and a Robbie Henshaw try seemed to put Connacht in control but Jonathan Wisniewski replied with 13 points before the break.
A  Matt Healy try left Connacht 26-16 up before Grenoble punished Denis Buckley's sin-binning as Lucas Dupont and Mahamadou Diaby scored tries.
John Cooney kicked Connacht ahead again but Wisniewski's late drop goal won it.
Grenoble's victory means they will face Harlequins in the semi-finals while Connacht will have to recover from this disappointment to regroup for the conclusion of the Pro12, in which they are currently placed second in the table.
As Connacht started with their verve that has been the hallmark of their season, centre Bundee Aki's looping pass set up Adeolokun's 13th-minute try.
After Wisniewski's penalty reply, a lightning Healy break and well-directed Shane O'Leary cross-kick allowed Henshaw to touch down before a Kieran Marmion dart helped set up Adeolokun's second try.
However after slotting his second successful conversion, O'Leary's reckless cross-kick on his own line gifted Grenoble a lifeline as fly-half Wisniewski replied.
Two more Wisniewski penalties reduced Connacht's lead to 19-16 at the break but the Irish province moved 13 clear by the 52nd minute after the outstanding Healy burst through the Grenoble defence and O'Leary kicked five further points.
However, Buckley's sin-binning on the hour mark, after he illegally attempted to prevent a Grenoble try, was a key moment.
Grenoble, currently eighth in the Top 14 table, scored two tries during Buckley's absence with Dupont and Diaby touching down as Irishman Chris Farrell provided a brilliant off-load in the build-up to the second score.
Wisniewski's two conversions helped Grenoble lead for the first time with 10 minutes left and while replacement John Cooney's penalty put Connacht ahead again on 73, the home number 10's drop goal two minutes later clinched victory.
TEAMS
Grenoble: G Aplon; N Nemani, C Farrell, N Hunt, L Dupont; J Wisniewski, C McLeod; S Taumalolo, L Jammes, D Edwards; B Hand (capt), P Kimlin; S Setephano, M Diaby, R Grice. Replacements: F Barcella for Taumalolo 53, M Marie for Hand 70, F Alexandre for Grice 26, F Estebanez for Hunt 67.
Not used: L Saseras, R de Klerk, P Mas, F Gengenbacher
Connacht: M Healy; N Adeolokun, R Henshaw, B Aki, D Poolman; S O'Leary, K Marmion; D Buckley, T McCartney, F Bealham; U Dillane, A Browne; S O'Brien, J Connolly, J Muldoon (capt). Replacements: D Heffernan for McCartney 71, R Loughney for Connolly 60, JP Cooney for Bealham 75, Q Roux for Dillane 60, G Naoupu for O'Brien 73, J Cooney for Marmion 63, P Robb for O'Leary 66, F Carr for Poolman 37.
Referee: Matthew Carley (England)

Mr Trump signed an executive order placing a 90-day ban on people from six mainly Muslim countries on Monday.
New York maintains the new directive is a ban on Muslims while Washington contends it is harmful to the state. Massachusetts later joined them.
Minnesota and Oregon are reportedly also filing lawsuits seeking to block the ban, which begins on 16 March.
The White House has said it is "very confident" the ban will win in court.
The renewed legal challenges come after attorneys for Hawaii filed a lawsuit against the revised order on Wednesday night, arguing it would harm its Muslim population, tourism and foreign students.
Hawaii was among other states that had previously sued over the president's initial travel ban, but the legal challenge was halted until courts ruled on similar cases across the country.
The revised ban bars new visas for people from: Somalia, Iran, Syria, Sudan, Libya and Yemen. It also temporarily blocks all refugees.
The previous order, which Mr Trump signed in January, was blocked in federal courts and sparked mass protests as well as confusion at airports.
But critics maintain the revised travel ban discriminates against Muslims.
"President Trump's latest executive order is a Muslim Ban by another name, imposing policies and protocols that once again violate the Equal Protection Clause and Establishment Clause of the United State Constitution," said New York Attorney General Eric T Schneiderman after announcing his legal challenge.
Washington state Attorney General Bob Ferguson, who was the first to sue over the original ban, said he would ask a federal judge to rule that the temporary restraining halting the first travel ban "remains in effect".
"We're asserting that the president cannot unilaterally declare himself free of the court's restraining order and injunction," he said.
Though the White House has faced mounting criticism over its immigration orders, Trump supporters say the president is fulfilling his campaign promises to protect Americans.
Citizens of Iran, Libya, Syria, Somalia, Sudan and Yemen, the other six countries on the original 27 January order, will once more be subject to a 90-day travel ban.
Iraq has been taken off the banned list because its government has boosted visa screening and data sharing, White House officials said.
The new directive says refugees already approved by the State Department can enter the US. It also lifts an indefinite ban on all Syrian refugees.
Green Card holders (legal permanent residents of the US) from the named countries will not be affected.
The new order does not give priority to religious minorities, unlike the previous directive.
Critics of the Trump administration had argued that this was an unlawful policy showing preference to Christian refugees.

At first I had a hard time balancing everything out. Every time I saved a class of kids from a burning school I then had to go unleash a tidal wave on a small coastal town. People quickly forgot about all the good I had done but all of my evil deeds were never forgotten. I became a public enemy and to many I was a villain. In order to preserve what little reputation I had left I begun performing evil deeds that looked not as harsh in the public eye. Instead of destroying school buses, I would blow up retirement homes. Instead of burning down houses, I would send a tornado at a prison. Although the public became less disapproving of my actions, I lost all self-respect for valuing some lives over others. At one point I decided it would be better to do everything on a massive scale. I would save entire cities from tidal waves thus gaining much praise and then create a hurricane over an entire small third world country. Inevitably the world came to despise me because while one group would love me for protecting them, everyone else still though I was a villain. Nonetheless I still tried to use my powers in some way that could be beneficial but I took a more omnipotent approach. I would cease natural disasters before they even occurred and instead of equating the balance in one foul swoop, I would gradually perform minor acts of evil that went more unnoticed. Unfortunately, people don't think about what could have happened but didn't, but the evil acts were still recognized. Resultantly society became a pretty bad place because only bad things happened, or at least that's what they thought. That was all in the past though. Now I think I've got it down. I don't take credit for anything anymore. I just save thousands of lives from catastrophe and then slowly let out the evil to balance. Sure, the standard of living drops a little but humans are resilient, they'll get over it. The way I see it, if I do my job right, they won't suspect anything at all. -A little afterword, I heavily borrowed ideas on how to be a god from the Futurama episode. "When you do things right, people won't be sure you've done anything at all."

We eat too much and move too little, claims one of the co-authors of a new “diet book” Dr. James Hill. Together with his colleges from the University of Colorado Drs. John C. Peters and Bonnie T. Jortberg he wrote a new guide to losing weight "The Step Diet".
The book itself saw the world not so long ago, in 2004. But the weight-loss program the book promotes is rather old. It is based on more than 25 years of scientific research at the Health Science Center of University of Colorado. The authors of the book are also co-founders of the popular national health movement “America on the Move”.
The key principle of the Step diet is to increase the physical activity and to decrease general food intake. Those who associate the word physical activity with a gym full of training equipments and daily exhausting work-outs have no reasons to worry. According to the Step Diet all you need is just stand up and walk your way to the weight loss. Instead of counting calories the dieter should count steps.
The scientific basis behind this plan is the concept of energy balance that is the calories we eat cannot exceed the calories we burn through our activity. So if we eat too many calories and don’t move enough we gain weight. The simple truth of the Step Diet is calories that come in should step out.
The authors of the Step Diet don't promise their followers achieving rapid results and drastic weight loss. Their aim is long-term weight control. At the same time their studies at the university shows that the dieter can lose 1 to 2 pounds weight per week sticking to the main rules of the diet.
The positive side about the Step Diet is its simplicity. The dieter doesn’t have any food restrictions and can eat everything he or she likes. The person doesn’t have to rack brains over meal preparations and calories calculation. The key condition is to decrease the food intake to 75% of usual portion.
On the other hand some experts say that since the book emphasizes the exercises and eventual achieving of 10 000 steps per day some people can become poor food choices as there are no concrete directions as to what should be eaten.
The Step Diet is much more than a simple idea of losing weight. It promotes changing of negative and unhealthy habits and acquiring positive energy from walking and forming new healthy life style. You needn’t do that much about becoming slim and beautiful just step to you health. And DietSpace.com will assist you in achieving your aim.

Do NOT buy this camera. It's the worst I've ever had, and I regret spending $1300 on this piece of junk, selling it less than two months after purchase.

Its main flaw: the camera constantly underexposed lighting in pictures, regardless of settings, ruining most shots. Additionally, it's too big and bulky. The only good thing about this camera is the clarity and sharpness from its high megapixels, but this single positive isn't enough to redeem it.

Unless you're a professional, you can get just as good shots with a cheap point-and-shoot camera.

**When Your Heartstrings Break by Beulah - A Review**

Beulah's "When Your Heartstrings Break" stands as a compelling entry in the indie pop landscape, showcasing the band's knack for crafting melodically rich songs wrapped in emotional vulnerability. The album balances jangly guitars with introspective lyrics, creating a sound that feels both nostalgic and immediate.

The production strikes an effective middle ground between polished and raw, allowing the band's earnest songwriting to shine through without losing the rough edges that give their music its character. Vocalist Miles Kurosky delivers performances that range from tender whispers to soaring choruses, demonstrating impressive range throughout the record.

Standout tracks showcase Beulah's ability to blend pop sensibilities with deeper emotional themes. The instrumentation frequently surprises, incorporating unexpected elements that keep listeners engaged across the album's runtime. However, some songs feel slightly underdeveloped, leaving the impression that a few more could have benefited from additional refinement.

The album works best when the band leans into their strengths - memorable hooks paired with genuine emotional weight. While not every track reaches the same heights, the overall experience is cohesive and rewarding for fans of thoughtful indie pop.

"When Your Heartstrings Break" confirms Beulah as a band capable of creating music that resonates on both intellectual and emotional levels, making it a worthwhile listen for those seeking substance beneath the surface.

**Rating: 7.5/10**

**The Chase**

Marcus rounded the corner, his sneakers slapping against wet pavement as he called out, "Sarah, wait!" His sister's backpack bounced ahead of him, her dark hair streaming behind her as she ran.

She'd stormed out after their argument about their father's funeral arrangements, taking the key to their childhood home—the key they both needed to sort through decades of memories before the estate sale next week.

"Sarah, please! We need to talk about this!"

But she kept running, tears blurring her vision as she darted between parked cars toward the crosswalk. Marcus was gaining on her, his longer strides closing the distance.

An elderly man walking his terrier looked up to see a young woman fleeing in obvious distress, pursued by a larger man shouting. Without hesitation, Harold stepped forward just as Marcus reached the sidewalk.

"Leave her alone!" Harold thrust his walking stick between Marcus's legs.

Marcus pitched forward into the intersection just as the delivery truck rounded the corner, its driver frantically hitting the horn and brakes on the rain-slicked street.

Sarah turned at the sound of screeching tires and her brother's name died on her lips.

The funeral key fell from her trembling fingers, landing next to where Marcus lay still, finally close enough to touch.

Fostering environmental stewardship among schoolchildren is crucial for safeguarding their future. These principles also offer significant financial advantages for educational institutions, as evidenced by some institutions currently incurring annual electricity bills as high as $60,000.

Newcastle City Council has involved local schools in its ClimateCam for Schools initiative since 2006, an initiative specifically engineered for environmental and economic sustainability. The program motivates schools to implement a range of defined sustainability measures and subsequently recognizes their achievements with “eco stars,” designated for display on an accompanying plaque.

Michelle Lindsay, ClimateCam for Schools co-ordinator, explained that the stars emphasize water and energy efficiency upgrades, as well as fostering behavioral change throughout the school community. To date, 225 stars have been awarded to 52 schools for initiatives such as the installation of rainwater tanks, tree planting programs, lighting retrofits, establishment of food gardens, and comprehensive recycling efforts.

Ms. Lindsay stated that a fundamental aspect of the program involves student participation in monitoring energy and water consumption within their respective institutions. She emphasized, “As a guiding principle, it is asserted that ‘if you cannot measure it, you cannot manage it.’ This has proven particularly crucial for identifying specific opportunities for resource optimization and cost reduction within schools. Ausgrid’s online electricity monitoring software has facilitated the identification of wastage areas and enabled prompt implementation of corrective measures.”

Energy audits conducted across seven schools revealed that approximately 40 percent of electricity consumption occurred during school hours. Students at Lambton Public School identified that reducing weekend electricity consumption by 50 percent could be achieved through the straightforward measure of deactivating equipment at the main power source. Providing classrooms with readily available power strips or seven-day timers could mitigate appliance energy wastage and represent a prudent business decision given escalating energy costs.

The digital revolution has also contributed to increased energy expenditures, with computers and interactive whiteboards in classrooms accounting for approximately 25 percent of the electricity consumed during operational hours. These changes have also impacted residential energy costs, as students charge their portable computing devices at home.

Ms. Lindsay noted that substantial savings could frequently be realized through modest technological enhancements. Refrigeration units could account for approximately 10 percent of a school’s energy expenditure, particularly if they are older models or feature glass doors. Ms. Lindsay stated, “An outdated refrigeration unit can contribute up to $300 annually to a school’s energy costs; consequently, investment in a new, efficient model typically yields rapid returns through energy cost reductions.”

Nevertheless, behavioral change is pivotal for sustained cost reductions. Ms. Lindsay observed, “There has been a greater emphasis on climate-controlled classrooms compared to previous educational paradigms. While student comfort is paramount, it is not uncommon to observe students disrobing in classrooms during winter months. Redundant energy consumption can be obviated by setting thermostats in accordance with WorkCover guidelines. Ceiling fans can also provide effective cooling in moderate thermal conditions and consume as little as 2 percent of the energy required by air conditioning units.”

Corpus Christi Primary School has identified that adopting proactive waste management strategies can also yield substantial financial efficiencies. The school implemented a zero-waste lunch policy six months prior and is now realizing approximate savings of $700 every three weeks on waste disposal expenditures. Non-compostable waste must be transported home by students within their lunch containers. Healthy food choices are also advocated among parents. Cling film and aluminum foil are disincentivized, as are excessive food portions.

Sister Jenny Gerathy stated, “Parents frequently express surprise to learn that apples often receive minimal consumption before disposal.” She added, “We advise parents to provide smaller, portioned servings of fresh fruit instead of larger, undivided items.” The initiative aims to heighten parental awareness regarding packaging and food waste, and assists in their re-evaluation of portion sizes for their children’s lunchboxes. Recent financial efficiencies are being allocated for the acquisition of mathematics and science equipment and will be directed towards the school’s food garden project.

Sister Gerathy emphasized that continuous educational outreach was paramount. In addition to educating students via waste audits, workshops, and expert presentations, she noted that resources had been dedicated to fostering awareness among parents and staff through formal meetings, digital newsletters, and kindergarten orientation sessions.

not too bad of a job, and the prices are quite reasonable for the quality received. I've used their service twice now, once for a routine check-up and another time for a minor repair, and both experiences were surprisingly smooth. The staff are friendly and efficient, getting things done without any fuss or unnecessary delays. While it might not be the absolute top-tier luxury experience, it certainly delivers good value. Christian Jacq's Volume Three, consistent with its predecessors, creates a completely unrealistic Egypt ruled by an almost god-like Ramses, who is perpetually reasonable and good. Jacq fails to instill any real emotion in Ramses. The historical inaccuracies are astounding, making it baffling how he can market this as a history book. The sole positive aspect is its easy readability, allowing for a quick finish. However, Jacq frequently deviates from the main storyline to recount Ramses' experiences in excruciating, often irrelevant, detail. These sections are so tedious that I found myself skipping them. I can only recommend this book to those who are just learning to read.

Eddie Jones' side go into the Six Nations match looking to equal New Zealand's world record of 18 straight Test victories.
However, Cotter says Scotland have enough attacking threats in their ranks to take the game to England.
"The players are keen when they get their hands on the ball to express themselves at Twickenham," said Cotter.
"I think that's a good attitude to have and attitude will determine a big part of the game.
"We know when we defend we'll have to defend resolutely."
Scotland sit third in the Six Nations table, having registered wins over Ireland and Wales either side of a narrow defeat by France in Paris.
The Scots, though, have not beaten England since 2008 and have the unenviable record of not winning at Twickenham since 1983.
Hamish Watson replaces the injured John Hardie in Scotland's only change from the win over Wales.
England have named the returning Billy Vunipola on the bench for Saturday's match, and Cotter admits the powerful Saracens number eight is just one of many strong options Jones can call upon late in the game.
"They [England] have a formula that is working well for them," said Cotter, who will be replaced by Gregor Townsend as head coach at the end of the season.
"They have a bench that is coming on and finishing games off in the last 10 minutes. It's something we're aware of.
"I think they'll comfortably move through the gears as the game goes on and finish in top speed and we've got to stay with them.
"We know more or less what's coming and it's just a matter of withstanding that."
Jones suggested in the aftermath of England's win over Italy that Scotland would be under increased pressure going into the Calcutta Cup match, with expectations raised among supporters after the wins over Ireland and Wales.
"Coaches have different characters and styles," added Cotter. "That's his and everybody has their own.
"I think Eddie's good fun, he's good company. I like having a chat with him outside of the game and in the game we both like to compete as best we can.
"Our players know they're going to have to give their best performance. During that 80 minutes we'll have to string together a number of excellent plays in attack and defence and to think clearly to get them to start adapting to our play.
"It hasn't been discussed a lot within the team, the Calcutta Cup, Triple Crown, nothing. All that has been discussed is content of the game and getting the content right. That's all this team is doing. Nothing else is being applied or talked about."
