"What do you mean, it could be fucking worse?" Mike asked, swatting a couple of mosquitoes away.
"Well I'm just saying, Mike, we should look on the bright side."
"What bright side? We're going to be dead within fucking minutes! Even if we manage to maneuver over this very deadly lava then surely the cops will shoot us dow-aw, damnit!" Mike exclaimed as he got bitten by another mosquito.
Diane waved her hands around her face. "Well, it's not gonna help complaining about it, okay? If my mother taught me anything it's to keep up the optimism at all times."
Mike took a step back to avoid the lava. "Yeah, well, if your mother hadn't hidden those bodies in our backyard then maybe we wouldn't be in this position!"
"Yeah, well how do you think I feel, Mike? I'm close to death too! At least you didn't just find out your own parents framed you for murder."
"Well, who framed who isn't gonna matter a whole lot when we get swallowed by lava now, is it?"
"Actually, I'm pretty sure this is magma," said Diane. "See, lava is when the volcano has just erupted and—"
"I really, really don't give a shit."
The helicopters flew down closer, and a policeman exclaimed through a megaphone: "Attention, this is the Central Intelligence Agency. Get down and put your hands on your head or we will open fire!"
"How are we supposed to do that right now if that would require lying into the mag-lava, whatever, face down?" yelled Mike.
"That's a really good point, but I don't think they can hear us, Mike," said Diane sarcastically.
Mike rolled his eyes. He picked up a rock to throw at the helicopter but felt its heat and quickly dropped it. "Aww, god damnit, stupid superheated rocks!"
"See, that's definitely magma."
"Could you stop arguing the semantics of our death trap and instead try and, maybe, I don't know, find a way to fucking survive this?"
"See, there's the negativity again," lectured Diane. "Why didn't your parents ever teach you not to be such an asshole?"
"Well, at least they weren't fucking serial killers."
"Walked right into that one," said Diane, swatting a mosquito against her arm. "Anyway, you were the one proposing we'd flee into an active volcano in the first place."
"Well, every other way was swarming with cops..." Mike spit out a mosquito. "What should I have done?"
"Well, maybe we could have turned ourselves in instead of running into a certain and long, painful death."
"I'm not going to jail because of my fucking in-laws." Mike spit into the lava.
"It's better than being fucking swallowed alive by magma, isn't it?"
"Only just."
The lava kept approaching, and Diane and Mike backed up against the steep mountainside. The policeman spoke again. "This is your final warning, get down now!"
"Okay," said Diane. "What if we signal the helicopter to let down a rope or a ladder or something, they probably have one of those, don't they?"
"Well, they're not gonna see us gesturing anything with all this smoke and all these goddamned mosquitoes!"
"Well, at least I'm trying to think of something instead of just scolding everything. Do you really want to be such an asshole in your very last moments?"
"I don't know, Diane, I don't want any last moments at all."
"Well, you're shit out of luck."
"It's really happening, isn't it?" sighed Mike. "We're actually going to die." Mike looked into Diane's eyes. "Diane, I want you to know that I've never despised you."
"I loved you too, Mikey," smiled Diane, "for at least the first couple of years."
Diane and Mike embraced. They kissed each other one last time as the lava gnawed at their feet. No screams escaped their lips, and it was over in less than a minute.

Arsenal had a really frustrating day at home on Saturday, drawing 0-0 with Sunderland in the Premier League. It meant they missed a big chance to get within just one point of leaders Manchester United. You can bet Gunners manager Arsene Wenger was absolutely fuming, especially after some late decisions at the Emirates didn't go his title-chasing team's way. Andrey Arshavin actually put the ball in the net, but it was disallowed for offside – even though TV replays clearly showed it should have counted! Then, the Russian star was seemingly pulled down by Titus Bramble when he was bearing down on goal, but the referee waved away what looked like strong penalty appeals.

"It's really disappointing, and it's so tough to take decisions like that in a game of this importance," Wenger told Sky Sports, clearly frustrated. "We just have to take it on the chin and keep fighting. But honestly, it's absolutely disappointing and simply unacceptable," he added.

Arsenal, still reeling from that shock defeat in the English League Cup final on Sunday, now have a seriously tough trip ahead: Barcelona for their Champions League second leg tie. And to make things harder, they'll be without Theo Walcott and Robin van Persie, and are sweating on the fitness of captain Cesc Fabregas, despite their good 2-1 win in the first leg. To top it all off, young midfielder Jack Wilshere also picked up an injury in the Sunderland game and might miss that crucial trip to the Nou Camp too.

Meanwhile, Manchester United, still sitting three points clear of Arsenal at the top, are off to face their arch-rivals Liverpool on Sunday. In the evening game, Manchester City managed to hold onto third place, seven points adrift of United, with a rather unconvincing 1-0 home win against Wigan. David Silva grabbed the only goal right before halftime, thanks to a bit of a fumble from Wigan goalkeeper Ali Al Habsi. Wigan, unfortunately, are still rooted to the bottom, four points from safety, having only managed one win since Boxing Day.

Elsewhere on Saturday, Birmingham crashed back down to earth after their amazing cup win, losing 3-1 at home to fellow strugglers West Bromwich Albion, which sees them tumble into the bottom three. Bolton, on the other hand, had a dramatic day, coming from behind twice and even seeing visitors Aston Villa miss a penalty before snatching a thrilling 3-2 win. Gary Cahill bagged a brace for Bolton, and then substitute Ivan Klasnic popped up with the winner. Another five-goal thriller unfolded at Craven Cottage, where Fulham beat Blackburn 3-2, thanks to a late and somewhat controversial penalty from Bobby Zamora. Everton edged out Newcastle 2-1 at St James' Park, while West Ham's recent good run carried on with a cracking 3-0 win over Stoke. The scoring was wrapped up by German international Thomas Hitzlsperger, who's really been a big influence since coming back from his long injury. That win sees the Hammers climb out of the bottom three in what's becoming a really tight relegation battle.

Dances with Robots
Teaching automatons to figure out what needs to be done
By Rich Barlow
As dancers, this couple is no Fred Astaire and Ginger Rogers. The leader’s moves are clunky, his partner’s so tentative that she’s constantly behind a beat. But be kind: they’re beginners at salsa, and they’re bedeviled by something Fred and Ginger never faced.
They’re robots. Watch the video.
H. Kayhan Ozcimder (ENG’11,’15), a dancer with the Boston troupe Collage, has had the inelegant experience of dancing with one of these machines, which resemble a vacuum cleaner minus the hose. Ozcimder dreams of a more agile automaton someday, but for now he’s pleased to have helped program these salsa-bots, proving that “it’s possible to do an art form in a robotic platform.”
Ozcimder is a graduate student in John Baillieul’s Intelligent Mechatronics Lab, whose mission, says the College of Engineering mechanical engineering professor, is to give machines the ability to respond to their environment. The researchers began by mapping the coordinates of actual salsa dancers and programming the robots with four basic beginner moves (relying on his dancer’s knowledge, Ozcimder suggested salsa as a simple starting point for the mechanized dance amateurs). The robots, which are outfitted with motion sensors, read each other’s moves and respond according to the programming.
Ozcimder thinks motion-reading robots might someday serve as useful tools for judging dance competitions (possibly bouncing Kirstie Alley even sooner from Dancing with the Stars), but Baillieul is hunting bigger game. He’s not out to help “some high school guy who had trouble getting a date, so you get a robot. The ultimate goal is to understand human reaction to gestures and how machines may react to gestures.” That could enable robots to team with, and perhaps take over from, humans in hazardous jobs, from treacherous rescues to repairs in lethal environments (think the workers who plunged into the stricken Fukushima Daiichi nuclear plant after the 2011 Japanese tsunami).
The intelligent mechatronics lab is littered with things from dancing robots to flight vehicles. The work builds on an established fact of 21st-century life: computing machines will do more of the work. “Everyday objects like automobiles have gone from almost entirely mechanically engineered things to being machines that are basically controlled at every level by computers,” notes Baillieul. “A typical automobile now has 100 or more microprocessors in it.”
The challenge is to build machines that can perform tasks with some autonomy and respond in fluid situations they might not have been precisely programmed for, an instance where man still has it all over machines. Whereas human reaction is the child of several parents—instinct, surely, but also the ability to learn from experience and sometimes override instinct—robots are not yet agile enough to ignore their “instinct” (programming). The solution, says Baillieul, is to give the machines sufficiently “massive experiential data sets” that they can react to numerous situations.
One avenue the lab is exploring is humans’ use of nonverbal cues to communicate. Good dancers move seamlessly together, responding to each other’s touch and motions; amateurs without experience reading each other’s cues often come off looking stilted. Nonverbal cues can also be used to send misinformation; bats, for example, camouflage their motions so that they can sneak up on insect prey, a fake-out familiar to anyone who’s tried to swat a pesky fly. Hence the lab’s work with getting robots to use sensors to read each other’s metal-body language, aimed at “how you might program flying vehicles or mobile robots to do the right thing, in terms of communicating or not communicating through their motions,” Baillieul says.
Dance companies like Ozcimder’s can rest easy; even he doesn’t foresee automating human dancers out of a job. Robots may be geniuses at detecting footwork, body angles, and other technical metrics that go into a performance, but they can’t judge the intangible artistic panache that might please an audience, like dancers’ facial expressions.
Ozcimder has bad news for our mechanized friends: intangibles make up half the judging criteria at a typical salsa competition.

With an area of only 1.95km2, Monaco is the second smallest sovereign state in the world, surpassed only by Vatican City. This tiny principality on the French Riviera has managed to establish itself as one of the most glamorous and wealthy destinations on Earth, despite its diminutive size.

Monaco's compact territory is divided into four main districts: Monaco-Ville (the old city), Monte Carlo (famous for its casino), La Condamine (the port area), and Fontvieille (a newer district built on reclaimed land). Every square meter of this microstate is precious, leading to some of the highest real estate prices globally and innovative urban planning solutions.

The principality's economy thrives on several key sectors. Tourism plays a vital role, with visitors flocking to experience the legendary Monte Carlo Casino, luxury shopping, and prestigious events like the Monaco Grand Prix. The banking and finance sector has also flourished, attracting wealthy individuals and businesses with favorable tax policies. Additionally, Monaco has no personal income tax for residents, making it a popular haven for the ultra-wealthy.

Despite its small size, Monaco punches well above its weight culturally. The Monte Carlo Opera House hosts world-class performances, while the Monaco Yacht Show is one of the most prestigious maritime events globally. The principality also maintains its own orchestra, ballet company, and several museums.

The ruling Grimaldi family has governed Monaco for over 700 years, making it one of the world's oldest ruling dynasties. Prince Albert II currently serves as the sovereign, continuing his family's long tradition of leadership while modernizing the principality for the 21st century.

Monaco's population density is extraordinary, with approximately 39,000 residents packed into its tiny territory. Remarkably, only about 12% of residents are actually Monégasque citizens, while the majority are French nationals and other international residents drawn by the principality's unique lifestyle and tax advantages.

The artificial state of society in our own age, has probably acted disadvantageously on our literary researches, if not on our moral character. Civilization is a relative arbitrary term; and the ancestors whom we are pleased to term uncivilized, may have possessed as high a degree of mental culture as ourselves, though it unquestionably differed in kind. Job wrote his epic poem in a state of society which we should probably term uncultivated; and when Lamech gave utterance to the most ancient and the saddest of human lyrics, the world was in its infancy, and it would appear as if the first artificer in “brass and iron” had only helped to make homicide more easy. We can scarce deny that murder, cruel injustice, and the worst forms of inhumanity, are but too common in countries which boast of no ordinary refinement; and we should hesitate ere we condemn any state of society as uncivilized, simply because we find such crimes in the pages of their history.
The question of the early, if not pre-Noahacian colonization of Ireland, though distinctly asserted in our annals, has been met with the ready scepticism which men so freely use to cover ignorance or indifference. It has been taken for granted that the dispersion, after the confusion of tongues at Babel, was the first dispersion of the human race; but it has been overlooked that, on the lowest computation, a number of centuries equal, if not exceeding, those of the Christian era, elapsed between the Creation of man and the Flood; that men had “multiplied exceedingly upon the earth;” and that the age of stone had already given place to that of brass and iron, which, no doubt, facilitated commerce and colonization, even at this early period of the world’s history. The discovery of works of art, of however primitive a character, in the drifts of France and England, indicates an early colonization. The rudely-fashioned harpoon of deer’s horn found beside the gigantic whale, in the alluvium of the carse near the base of Dummyat, twenty feet above the highest tide of the nearest estuary, and the tusk of the mastodon lying alongside fragments of pottery in a deposit of the peat and sands of the post-pliocene beds in South Carolina, are by no means solitary examples. Like the night torch of the gentle Guanahane savage, which Columbus saw as he gazed wearily from his vessel, looking, even after sunset, for the long hoped-for shore, and which told him that his desire was at last consummated, those indications of man, associated with the gigantic animals of a geological age, of whose antiquity there can be no question, speak to our hearts strange tales of the long past, and of the early dispersion and progressive distribution of a race created to “increase and multiply.”
The question of transit has also been raised as a difficulty by those who doubt our early colonization. But this would seem easily removed. It is more than probable that, at the period of which we write, Britain, if not Ireland, formed part of the European continent; but were it not so, we have proof, even in the present day, that screw propellers and iron cast vessels are not necessary for safety in distant voyages, since the present aboriginal vessels of the Pacific will weather a storm in which a Great Eastern or a London might founder hopelessly.

This might take me some time to read, but I’ll get through as much as I can and add my thoughts as I go. I finished Origins, and I found it intriguing. At first, the stories seemed unrelated, but by the end, I saw how they were connected. You linked them well—just enough to make it clear without giving too much away, which kept it interesting and made the whole thing feel connected. So far, I think it’s good.

I also finished War, and I really liked it. Each story on its own is decent, but together they make a well-executed narrative. War doesn't have the same sense of mystery as Origins—instead, it feels like a series of memories that come together to show a bigger picture. Origins feels like the prologue to this part of the story, so now I think the next section might be the epilogue. I’m a bit confused, but that isn’t really a criticism.

Study is even more mysterious than Origins, with lots of open questions that kept me interested. It feels a bit incomplete and makes me want more. Overall, you’ve built an interesting universe with plenty of room for more stories.

Let's Chat About Transition Elements!

Hey, did you know that one super cool thing about transition metals is that they form incredibly colorful ions when they're dissolved in a solution? This picture here gives you a peek at just some of the beautiful shades you can get from them!

Let's dive into a quick intro to these fascinating elements! You might notice something a bit different when you look at the fourth row of the periodic table. It doesn't quite follow the same pattern as the second and third rows. Basically, after elements like potassium (K) and calcium (Ca) fill up their 4s orbital, the electrons then start hopping into the 3d orbitals. Since there are five 'd' orbitals in each shell, this creates a block on the periodic table that's ten elements wide. These ten elements right after calcium, along with their buddies in the fifth and sixth periods, are what we call the "transition metals." They're super common and used everywhere – think about everyday metals like iron, copper, silver, and gold! What's really interesting is that their physical and chemical properties are pretty unique, often quite different from those alkali and alkaline earth metals we've talked about. We'll explore some of those cool differences next!

Here's a neat observation: the properties of transition elements don't change *that* much as you move across a period. Think about the elements in the second period, for example. You start with metals like sodium and magnesium, then hit a metalloid like silicon, and then you get nonmetallic solids like phosphorus and sulfur, and finally gases like chlorine and argon. See that clear shift from metallic to nonmetallic properties? Well, with transition metals, it's quite different! All of them pretty much stay very metallic as you go across. Also, remember how atomic radius usually decreases as you move left to right across a period (like magnesium being smaller than sodium)? For the *first* transition series, it's a bit different! You actually see a slight increase in atomic radius in the first half, and then it pretty much stays steady in the second half. Things like ionization energy and electronegativity also tend to stay pretty consistent across a transition series.

One *huge* difference that sets transition metals apart is their knack for forming what we call "coordination compounds." Think of it like this: certain molecules, like water or ammonia, have an extra pair of electrons they're not using. Transition metals (or their ions) are super happy to accept these extra electrons, neatly tucking them away into their empty 'd' orbitals. What's really cool is that the color of these transition metals can totally change depending on *which* molecules are coordinated to them! For instance, copper(II) might be a pale blue in plain water, and nickel(II) a pale green. But if you add ammonia? The copper(II) instantly switches from that pale blue to a much deeper, darker blue because the ammonia molecules replace the water! And for nickel(II), you'd see a shift from green to a lovely violet when ammonia gets involved. Pretty neat, right?

Most compounds made with transition metals are just bursting with color! Compare that to alkali and alkaline earth metal compounds, which are almost *always* white. So, why the vibrant colors for transition metals? It all comes down to something called "d orbital splitting." Normally, when there aren't any other molecules hanging around, those 'd' orbitals all have the same energy – we call that "degenerate." But when other molecules come along and coordinate with the transition metal, they actually *split* these 'd' orbitals into different energy levels. And guess what? The energy difference between these split orbitals just happens to match the energy of visible light! That's what makes them so colorful to our eyes.

And finally, another really interesting thing about transition metals is that many of them can have *multiple* oxidation states. Remember how predictable the alkali metals (always +1 ions) and alkaline earth metals (always +2 ions) are? Well, with transition metals, it's not quite so straightforward! There isn't a simple formula to guess their oxidation states; they can range from a modest +1 all the way up to a whopping +7!

You'll find some helpful charts and data below, like:
*   The Densities of the First Row Transition Elements (in grams per cubic centimeter)
*   Their Atomic Radii (in picometers)
*   First Ionization Energies
*   And their Electron Configurations

**Oak Alley Plantation Review - ★★★★☆**

Visiting Oak Alley Plantation was a deeply moving and educational experience that I'd recommend to anyone interested in Louisiana history. The iconic quarter-mile canopy of 300-year-old oak trees leading to the antebellum mansion is absolutely breathtaking and worth the visit alone.

**The Good:**
The guided mansion tour was informative and well-presented, offering insights into both the architectural beauty and the harsh realities of plantation life. Our guide was knowledgeable and didn't shy away from discussing the difficult history of slavery. The grounds are meticulously maintained, and the restaurant served excellent Creole cuisine. The exhibits about enslaved people's lives were respectfully done and provided important historical context often overlooked at similar sites.

**Areas for Improvement:**
The tour felt somewhat rushed, and I wished we had more time to explore the slave quarters and learn more about the lives of the 220+ enslaved people who lived here. The gift shop was overpriced, and parking can be challenging during peak season.

**Bottom Line:**
Oak Alley successfully balances its role as both a tourist attraction and an important historical site. While the natural beauty is stunning, the plantation's commitment to telling the complete story - including the brutal realities of slavery - makes this a meaningful educational experience. Plan for 2-3 hours and visit during weekdays if possible to avoid crowds.

Holocaust Remembrance Day (Yom Hashoah) allows us to memorialize the greatest tragedy experienced by the Jewish people in modern times. It reminds us that, wrapped up in that unfathomable horror, were millions of individual lives—real people with real stories. It is up to us to learn and share these stories on their behalf. Together, we say we must never forget. Rituals for Yom Hashoah vary. Some communities read names of people killed in the Holocaust, some observe moments of silence. New liturgy is still being developed, and it is sadly relevant, since genocide continues to plague our world.

open from april 6, 2013 to october 17, 2013 the exhibition “Faces of Modernity” drew visitors from across the globe to experience a unique curation of early twentieth-century portraits. Featuring works by influential artists such as Amedeo Modigliani, Gustav Klimt, and Egon Schiele, the show delved into how the concept of identity evolved over a period marked by rapid social and artistic change.

Curator Isabelle Laurent designed the exhibition to lead visitors through a series of galleries, each representing a decade in the evolution of modern portraiture. Detailed wall texts and interactive displays allowed attendees not only to view the art but also to engage with the stories behind both the creators and their subjects.

Throughout its six-month run, “Faces of Modernity” hosted numerous educational programs, including lectures by art historians, hands-on workshops for young artists, and guided tours that highlighted the connections between art and the political climate of the era. Critics praised the exhibition for its thought-provoking layout and rare loans from private collections.

By closing day, “Faces of Modernity” had attracted over 120,000 visitors, cementing its place as one of the most successful exhibitions of the year and sparking renewed interest in portraiture’s role in chronicling societal change.

By Martha De Lacey  
PUBLISHED: 10:18 EST, 24 April 2013 | UPDATED: 11:20 EST, 24 April 2013  

Michelle Obama, the former First Lady of the United States and wife of President Barack Obama, has long been admired around the world for her sophisticated and trend-setting sense of style. Bloggers and fashion enthusiasts regularly document her outfits, and social media accounts are dedicated to tracking her sartorial choices, which range from high-end designer evening gowns to accessible high street staples. Michelle is known for championing both established designers and emerging talent, making her wardrobe a frequent subject of fascination for those interested in fashion.

For those eager for even more immediate updates about Michelle Obama’s fashion choices, a new option has arrived: a smartphone fashion app that makes it even easier to keep up with her looks. The app, called Michelle's Style List, was created by Helen Barclay, a Birmingham-based developer and fashion lover, following the success of a similar app she made about the Duchess of Cambridge, Kate Middleton. Helen’s first app, Kate’s Style List, allowed users to emulate Kate’s elegant style and proved popular with royal watchers and shoppers alike.

Michelle's Style List is a paid app (priced at 69p/99 cents) and is now available for iPad and iPhone users. It provides up-to-date information about what Michelle Obama is wearing at public appearances, covering her clothing choices for all seasons—spring, summer, autumn, and winter. Every outfit is described in detail, and each item is hyperlinked, letting users find out where to purchase the same or similar pieces, or simply to learn more about the brands she chooses.

Michelle Obama’s style has not only been praised by the public and fashion press, but also earns her top spots on best-dressed lists, such as the Sunday Times Best Dressed List. As First Lady, Michelle often selected clothes from designers like Jason Wu, Isabel Toledo, Roksanda Ilincic, Michael Kors, Narciso Rodriguez, and popular brands like J.Crew, mixing luxury with accessibility. Her influence is so powerful that items she wears frequently sell out shortly after she’s seen in them, a phenomenon sometimes called “the Michelle effect.”

Helen Barclay, formerly an actress with a degree in theatre studies from Hull University, switched careers to app development after seeing the demand for easily accessible fashion information about public figures. She highlights Michelle Obama’s status as a global role model and the impact the entire Obama family has had in the United States and abroad. Helen points out that Michelle’s style reflects the occasion—whether she’s attending a formal event in a designer dress or participating in a school sports day in athletic wear.

The app is updated around the clock to reflect Michelle’s latest public appearances, offering users tools to browse her favorite designers, save favorite outfits, explore her choices in accessories, and track down where to buy similar clothing for themselves. Helen hopes that, like the Kate app, Michelle’s Style List will be popular not only in the U.S. but internationally as well. She says the response in its first week has already been encouraging.

Helen hints at future projects but is keeping details under wraps for now, focusing on keeping fashion fans closely connected to the signature looks of some of the world’s most influential women.  

Michelle's Style List, by Helen Barclay  
Kate's Style List, by Helen Barclay

It is not beyond our awareness how the web has created massive breakthroughs to alter our lives. It has sustained diverse schemes which soon fueled more innovation.
Twenty-five years ago, Tim Bernes-Lee has realized the potential of linking millions of computers to what we call now as the internet. The Web was initially designed to automate information-sharing between scientists and researchers from different institutes around the world. Today, the Web is constantly evolving to tackle new ways of data gathering, interacting with other people, marketing products, and more. As a result, innovations and new technologies are constantly introduced. One end-product of the Web’s sustainable development is the redesigned business landscape.
The Web allowed businesses across different fields to systematize manual operations to adhere its processes with streamline functions. It created new communication mechanisms and modern business designs that help companies sustain their lead generation programs.
In the physical domain, there are businesses that are unable to directly sell their products to consumers or other businesses, such as in the case of B2B firms. These companies, the manufacturing and intermediate goods producers, need middlemen that will inject their products to the market. Using the web, these companies can now attend to this concern. They can increase the product awareness of the potential buyers thru websites or any electronic order systems.
One great impact of the web is the establishment of E-business. E-business constitutes to the application of information technology knowledge in supporting the integral function of business. It has varied features:
- Choice freedom – E-businessmen can offers a diverse options to their customers because the web has unlimited information of products of different brands. This will allow the consumers to find what they exactly need.
- Save time and effort – Through online shops the customers transact in a hassle-free shopping system, that is, open 24/7 with no geographical boundaries.
- Price comparison – Most online shops enables their consumers to perform price comparison for a more rational buying decision.
- Inclusive lead generation – whenever a customer enters the online shop, the site can track the activities of the potential customer within the site. Cookie technology is used to learn what the customer is interested in buying so that next time he visits; the shop can automatically give him offerings based on his history data.
The internet allowed companies to have a better method of lowering their spending and savings capital in their operations. Companies use an array of business software to track its production expenditures and revenues. The real-time generated data can be used to assess future decisions to further stimulate efficiency. Nevertheless, this can be used as a tool in lead generation, through an augmented supply chain that involves significant data about distributors, suppliers, and other stakeholders.
Twenty five years had passed and it’s just starting. The Web pushed a new age of marketing strategies that had sparked new opportunities and competitive edge. From workforce recruitment, lead generation, data gathering, and cost management; the Web hugely altered how businesses used to be.

"Yes, sweetheart?" said Ramone Netz, his attention diverted from the laptop which sat upon his mahogany desk. "There is someone here to see you," said his secretary, prim and wearing a secretary's outfit from the 60s(think *Mad Men*). "No appointment?" "No." "Well, send him in." "It's a woman, sir." "Oh. Well send her in." The secretary vanished. He could hear her behind the thick doors offering coffee to the potential client. The door quacked and in walked a woman in her mid-30s, her suit disheveled, like she had been wearing it for days. She had thick purple bags under her right eye like a ripe squash, and the entire right side of her face emanated a sickly rainbow of bruised flesh. The woman walked in and abruptly sat down without being asked. "So what's the story?" After a bit, like she was considering what to say, the client said: "I'm not exactly sure. I remember last night, but it's hazy. I don't remember how I got here." "Well, you've got some serious problems then," said Netz. He was used to riffraff coming in off the streets, but they usually knew why they were there. He looked at the woman. She was actually quite pretty, he thought, except for the remarkable bruise which encompassed her face. The woman looked straight at Netz, seeming to see him for the first time. "I saw someone get murdered," she said. "Well, that's what I'm here for, doll" responded Netz. The honk of his door, an ancient oak double-door that resisted oiling, signaled his secretary. She walked in, not disturbing the scene but completely silencing both other parties nonetheless. She had that effect. She handed a styrofoam cup to the client and was gone again. "Where was I..." said Netz. "I...it's coming back to me. I was right there, like it just happened, then this fugue or whatever...I was at home. No, actually I was at someone else's home. It was my friend's...oh." "Well doll, out with it,"said Netz, lighting up a Marlboro."We can't do this unless you tell me what happened. I have another appointment waiting." "Well...I think I was in an argument with my husband. I remember now. He was cheating on me." She sipped her cup gingerly, her face flinching from her obvious discomfort. Netz removed a legal pad from a drawer and noted something. His look was one of "go on..." "So I found out, for sure, when he...I don't see what this really has to do with why I'm here." "No, this is important." "Well of course I had suspicions...when he didn't answer my calls, and took work trips. Well one day I decided to see if my suspicions were correct. I don't know why I did it, but I had to know, because it was something deep down that I felt, you know? And if you can't justify it, it just isn't right." "I'm afraid I don't know,"said Netz."But continue." "Well one day while he was sleeping(he keeps his phone with him at all times, except when it charges at night) I decided to take a look. I had carefully watched him type his password the day before, which I knew he changed often, although he said it was for work. So once on his phone, I proceeded to find out that he was cheating on me, with not only one, but two other bitches. I flipped the fuck out, okay?" "That's understandable." "But I don't realize how I got here." "Well doll, you're dead. I hate to be the one to break it to you." The woman's face was a mask of anger that melted as her eyes widened. "No! What do you think I am, some sort of crazy person? You sick bastard! I came here for help!" she got up frantically and turned toward the door. Netz had seen it all before a thousand times. He just shook his head and looked down, pulling up a file on his computer. "That's not gonna be the hardest part for you to take, doll, although take my word for it, you will in good time." "Fuck you, you lying--" But he cut her off, although he wasn't being particularly vocal: "I'm not a homicide detective. I'm a private investigator. And I was hired by your late ex-husband." The door creaked open, although this time it wasn't the secretary.

Quiet Inn that sits right in the middle of EVERYTHING. The room i rented was amazing clean and well taken care of. The staff is friendly and attends to any problems right then and there. I did have an issue with my gas fireplace when i arrived but i called to the office and they came took a look and it still didnt work for the staff. The man working then switched us to another room that was the same as the one i originally rented to fix the issue. I recommend this inn to families and couples who want a nice little getaway. They have a couple gazebos and an in ground pool for swimming. Ice machines and vending machines available.

"I'm God!"he proclaimed."All you mortals kneel before me!" I stared at the man who had made my life miserable for four, long years. Then, I got down on my knees. "Excellent." He sounded abnormally pleased, happier than the time he had dunked me in a trashcan, just before I asked my crush out to prom. Feeling a sudden surge of deja-vu, I craned my neck to look up at him. Even after my late growth spurt, I was still a few inches shorter than him. But from a kneeling position, I felt like I was back in ninth grade. "Why did you do it?" I asked. His brow furrowed. "Do what?" "Why did you torment me? You made my life miserable." There was no point in asking him these questions, but I needed closure. He shook his head. "I didn't." "You did. It was all your fault."I felt like shouting, but I kept my voice calm. There was no point in creating a scene, either."Do you regret it?" He was silent for two damning minutes. "God is never wrong," he said finally. That was all I needed to know. I turned away from the glass and walked out of the room. He started screaming behind me, and the nurses ran to sedate him. For him, I felt nothing but pity.

This has to be one of the worst plays ever written, Shakespeare or no Shakespeare. While the Bard was the master of English drama, he really slipped up here. The plot makes no sense, the characters motivations are contrived, and the jokes fall flat. I have read this play hundreds of times, seen umpteen productions and films, and am astonished at the plaudits universally accorded to it. The modern English translation by Daniel Nystedt, however, corrects many of these flaws (by eliminating the ghost and such unneccesary characters as Claudius, etc.) and overall is much more worthwhile.

ATLANTA — A growing number of teenage girls say they use the rhythm method for birth control, and more teens also think it is OK for an unmarried female to have a baby, according to a government survey released yesterday. The report may help explain why the teen pregnancy rate is no longer dropping.
Overall, teenage use of birth control and teen attitudes toward pregnancy have remained about the same since a similar survey was done in 2002.
But there were some notable exceptions in the new survey by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.
First, about 17 percent of sexually experienced teen girls say they had used the rhythm method — timing their sex to avoid fertile days to prevent getting pregnant. That’s up from 11 percent in 2002.
They may have been using another form of birth control at the same time. But the increase is considered worrisome because the rhythm method does not work about 25 percent of the time, said Joyce Abma, the report’s lead author. Abma is a social scientist at the CDC’s National Center for Health Statistics.
The survey of nearly 2,800 teens found that about 42 percent of never-married teens had had sex at least once in their life. Of those, 98 percent said they had used birth control at least once, with condoms being the most common choice. Those findings were about the same as in the 2002 survey.

collin garbarino, an assistant professor of history at houston baptist, is making waves in the academic community with his recent research on the evolution of religious education in America. This week, Garbarino published a groundbreaking study analyzing how religious colleges have adapted their curricula over the past fifty years in response to shifting cultural and political landscapes.

According to Garbarino, “Understanding the changes in how religious institutions approach education offers us a window into broader societal transformations since the mid-twentieth century.” His work draws on archival materials from dozens of institutions, faculty interviews, and educational policy documents.

Colleagues at Houston Baptist University praised Garbarino’s contribution. Dr. Emily Chen, Dean of the School of Humanities, said, “His research illustrates the delicate balance many faith-based schools navigate between tradition and modernity.”

The study arrives amid growing national debates over academic freedom, the role of religion in public life, and the future of higher education. Garbarino has already been invited to speak at upcoming conferences and is scheduled for a panel at next month’s Association for the Study of Higher Education meeting.

Local students, meanwhile, hope the increased attention will highlight the strengths of Houston Baptist. “It’s great to see our faculty engaged in these important conversations,” said senior Jessica Morales. “It shows that our education is both rooted and relevant.”

As discussions continue, Garbarino’s work is expected to serve as a critical resource for educators, policymakers, and historians seeking to navigate the complex intersection of faith and academia in the twenty-first century.

“Oh man, it's glaringly bright outside the attic. But who am I to complain? These days, I barely make an appearance. But I'm not about to become obsolete like those old record players—I’m built to last. What could possibly surpass a CD player anyway? I keep hearing whispers about this new 'digital download' craze taking over. Seriously, who wants to buy music that you can't even hold in your hand? That's what I don’t get.”

A man approaches the CD player, disc at the ready. “Yes, this is what I’m talking about! He’s actually coming over to play a CD! He’s a little different from the last guy, sure, but finally, I get to pump out some tunes again. Oh, this is going to be great. So, so great. The last owner knew my vibe—a little Metallica on a Saturday night while he sipped from a can. Sometimes he’d even toss a bit of KISS into my tray when the mood struck.”

The man opens the case, revealing a cover with three arches, figures in red, and a picture in a lavish frame. “Wait a second... is that—? Could it really be?” He hits the eject button. “Is this really what I thin—oh man, that feels fantastic after all this time.”

His thumb in the disc box center, he clicks out the disc and sets it into the tray. “Wow, it’s incredible to be used again. At this point, I don’t even care what he picks. Go ahead, press those buttons! I’m ready to bring the noise!”

The tray slides in, and the CD player scans and displays the track list. “Wait, what? Are you kidding me? No. No, no, no, NO!”

The man cranks the volume to maximum.

“No! Why would you do this? Just give me some Dolly Parton or Johnny Cash or something—anything but this!”

He presses play on track one. The groove kicks in.

“What is happening here? This isn’t good music! Not even back in the day! ‘Ahead of their time?’ No, there’s never been a right time for them. Why? Oh, just shove me back up into that dusty attic.”

The man starts singing, “A modern day warrior, Mean, mean stride, Today's Tom Sawyer, Mean, mean pride…”

“RUSH?! Why would you listen to RUSH? You know why Tom Sawyer’s mind wasn’t for rent? Because he made the mistake of listening to RUSH! That’s it, I’m done. I had my good years, played quality music, but not anymore. Let’s see what you do when I—”

The CD player abruptly powers down. The man, bewildered, knocks on its side in confusion. In one final act of mercy, the CD player did the only thing it could: forever locking the RUSH CD away, sparing the world from hearing it again.

Charles Byrne was the tallest Irishman who ever lived. He made his money as a freak in a circus and he grew to 7 feet seven inches tall at his height.
Now an article in today’s New England Journal of Medicine has finally identified the mutated gene that caused his condition-- as well as modern relatives who have the disease too.
When he died in 1783 a surgeon named John Hunter bought his body and boiled the flesh off with acid and exhibited the skeleton in London.
Now through modern day forensics a relative of Byrne, who also has the same condition, has been traced and the mystery of why Charles Byrne grew so tall has been solved.
The Journal of Medicine reported that researchers in Britain and Germany extracted DNA from Byrne’s teeth and identified a rare and mysterious gene mutation that was discovered only in 2006
Researchers have now found the same mutation in four families from Northern Ireland, close by where Charles Byrne was born.
The group was led by Dr. Marta Korbonits, professor of endocrinology and metabolism at Barts and the London School of Medicine and Dentistry.
They proved tht Byrne had a very rare pituitary tumor that led to disfigurement — “patients develop a bulging forehead and large jaw, hands and feet — and chronic severe headaches.” the Journal reports.
Dr. Shlomo Melmed, a pituitary tumor researcher at Cedars-Sinai Medical Center in Los Angeles, says such tumors are very important to study. He explained that the tumor cells undergo premature aging and do not kill like other tumors.
The investigators discovered that the giant and the four contemporary Irish families had the same ancestor who lived about 1,500 years ago -- and many have the same tumor.
One of them is Brendan Holland, a 58-year-old Irishman who experienced the same massive growth surge.
“I kept growing and growing,” Holland said, eventually stopping at 6 feet 9 inches. His pituitary tumor was discovered and treated and he was able to live a normal life.
A study of his DNA and the 18th century giant’s DNA revealed they were related.
He said he was touched by knowing what happened to his predecessor and how he must have suffered as subject of massive curiosity and freak shows.
Originally published in 2011.

**Discovering Ancient Rome: Highlights from the Penn Museum's Mediterranean Collections**

Stepping into the Penn Museum’s Mediterranean galleries is like embarking on a journey through the heart of Ancient Rome. From gleaming marble sculptures to everyday ceramics, the museum’s rich Roman collection illuminates the empire’s grandeur, innovation, and humanity. Here are some highlights that capture the spirit and complexity of this ancient civilization.

**Sculptures of Power and Devotion**

Among the most striking exhibits are the Roman portrait busts that greet visitors. One such piece, a marble bust of the Emperor Commodus, exudes both authority and artistic finesse, emblematic of Rome’s fascination with power and legacy. Another highlight is an exquisitely carved marble statuette of Venus, the goddess of love, reflecting both religious devotion and the Roman appreciation for classical Greek artistry.

**Mosaics: Stories in Stone**

The collection boasts vibrant mosaic panels salvaged from Roman villas. These fragments reveal scenes of mythological drama, animal chases, and intricate geometric patterns. Each mosaic is a testament to Roman engineering and aesthetic skill, bringing color and narrative into the homes of both the elite and ordinary citizens.

**Daily Life Unveiled Through Artifacts**

Beyond the grandeur, the Penn Museum’s artifacts uncover daily Roman life. Functional yet artfully designed oil lamps, bronze utensils, and delicately painted terra-cotta ceramics demonstrate the ingenuity and style present in even mundane activities. A notable find is a Roman glass flask, its iridescence preserved across the centuries, illustrating their mastery of glassworking and trade connections spanning the empire.

**Religious Practices and Rituals**

The museum showcases votive offerings and religious statuary, including miniature representations of gods like Mercury and household deities called Lares. These objects, once placed in homes or temples, exemplify the deeply interwoven nature of religion and daily existence in Ancient Rome.

**Inscriptions That Speak Across Time**

Stone slabs and pottery sherds inscribed in Latin offer glimpses into Roman bureaucratic life, commemorative practices, and personal dedications. One funerary stele immortalizes a beloved family member, bridging the gap between the grand narratives of history and the intimacy of individual lives.

**A Window into Rome’s Enduring Legacy**

The Penn Museum’s Mediterranean collections not only celebrate the artistic and technological achievements of Ancient Rome but also invite visitors to connect with the hopes, beliefs, and experiences of people who shaped—and were shaped by—one of history’s greatest empires. Exploring these treasures provides a tangible link to our shared past, inspiring curiosity and wonder in all who discover them.

'Earn your place': Liv Garfield is set to become the youngest woman ever to lead a FTSE 100 company . From travelling 90 miles to football matches every weekend with her dad, to bringing up two sons, she is used to being in a man’s world. Which is probably just as well – since Liv Garfield is about to join one of the most exclusive men’s clubs of all. The mother-of-two is set to become the youngest woman ever to lead a FTSE 100 company. The granddaughter of an Everton football club groundsman will make history when she leaves BT early next year to become head of water giant Severn Trent. As a female chief executive of a FTSE firm, she will be joining one of the smallest and most exclusive groups in British corporate life. And at 38, she will also become the youngest woman ever to ascend to the top of a blue chip company. The Footsie blue chip index lists the 100 biggest companies in the London Stock Exchange, and includes corporate giants such as Vodafone, Shell and Tesco. Currently only three companies in the index – airline easyJet, cigarette firm Imperial Tobacco and fashion label Burberry – have female chief executives, making Mrs Garfield’s arrival all the more remarkable. But there is one ambition she still has the occasional daydream about. She told the Mail: ‘I said for my entire childhood that I wanted to be the Everton manager, so that was my absolute ambition. ‘My mum had my bedroom with pink and fluffy clouds and I spent my childhood pinning up Everton pin-ups over them.’ From the age of five, she would travel every weekend with her dad to the Everton match – even though her family lived 90 miles away in Harrogate. ‘My grandparents are all Scousers. My dad and I used to go with my granddad,’ she said. ‘That was normal for me.’ Successful: Liv Garfield with Prime Minister David Cameron in March earlier this year . Now the Cambridge graduate takes her . two young sons to the game most weekends – and is trying to dissuade her . youngest, aged two, from switching his support to Chelsea. Her husband Morgan runs a property investment fund. She said her biggest mistake in the past was buying a sports car, only to discover that she was pregnant. ‘I couldn’t get into it,’ she said. But that was the only time family life has held her back – and she said she never encountered the ‘glass ceiling’ that prevents many women rising to the top. Despite being a hugely successful businesswoman, Mrs Garfield's regret is not becoming the Everton manager . ‘I didn’t find getting back on the ladder [after motherhood] hard,’ she said. ‘For any female to do well in business you have to ignore the fact that you are female. 'You have got to earn your place based on your own qualities and abilities.’ And her approach certainly seems to work. In September, she was ranked as number ten in Fortune Magazine’s hottest rising business stars, putting her alongside success stories such as Facebook boss Mark Zuckerberg and Twitter founder Jack Dorsey, and making her the highest ranked Briton. In addition to the three existing FTSE 100 companies led by women, Royal Mail, which floated last month and is led by Canadian Moya Greene, is expected to enter the FTSE 100 next month. Unless any of the companies drop out of the index, Mrs Garfield will be the fifth female chief executive when she takes up her new role. She was educated at the independent Birklands Belmont School – now the £8,733-a-year Belmont Grosvenor – in Birstwith, near Harrogate, and then at Bootham School in York, which charges up to £15,990 annually for day pupils and £26,115 for borders. During school holidays she would help her parents run their project management business. After studying French and German at Cambridge, she worked at management consultancy Accenture for six years. She joined BT in 2000 and will now move to Severn Trent, which supplies water to about four million households across the Midlands and Wales. The job, which could pay up to £2.4million a year, will see her lead the largest water company listed on the London Stock Exchange. She said it was a ‘huge wrench’ to leave BT, but added: ‘I have every confidence it will continue to thrive. I am really looking forward to joining Severn Trent.’

This has been a great router for me for upwards of 8 months now. Before I even installed it, I put the open source dd-wrt firmware on it, and it has been truly great. I literally haven't had to touch it in the 8-9 months I've owned it, which is more than I can say for the highly regarded WRT-54G that it replaced.

Speeds are great for N, although range is a bit disappointing—but no worse than the Linksys. I run daily with a 33% signal and still have good speeds, so I'm happy. You can get one of these dirt cheap now, and if you're looking to get into 802.11N on the cheap, I wouldn't hesitate to recommend this bad boy. Just keep in mind, replacing the firmware is not optional; do it right away for the best performance.

So Anna Fenninger just had this amazing day at Semmering, winning the World Cup giant slalom right in front of her home crowd! She was absolutely on fire, posting the fastest times in both runs to beat out Tina Maze and France's Tess Worley.

You know, it's been just over a year since Anna got her first World Cup win at Lienz, and now she's really hitting her stride. The 23-year-old clocked in at 2:13.09 total, and she's feeling pretty confident about what's ahead.

"My goal now is to keep skiing at that level until the Worlds and we'll see what happens there," she told reporters afterward. "When I won last year, I was really surprised. Winning today gives me the confidence that I can do it again and hopefully very soon."

She sounded so pumped about her performance! "I felt good out there today and I was 100 percent sure of my skiing. That's what you need to have to win such a race. On the upper part of the piste I got it right by just letting my skis do the work. The lead I had after the first run was very important."

Meanwhile, Tina Maze - who's leading the World Cup standings - was pretty happy with her second place finish. "I'm very happy with the 2nd place, although my first run wasn't perfect," she said. "I lost a lot of time in the top part. I'm pretty constant and very pleased with the way I've been skiing. One can't win every time - others also have their perfect day."

Maze is keeping her eye on the big picture though: "I will now stay focused on my goals of the season, which are the Worlds and the overall." With this podium finish - her ninth this season - she's now sitting at 999 points and has a comfortable 417-point lead over Germany's Maria Hoefl-Riesch.

By . Ian Parkes, Press Association . Daniel Ricciardo believes finishing ahead of Sebastian Vettel in this year's Formula One world championship would prove to be a real feather in his cap. Back-to-back third-place finishes in the last two grands prix behind dominant Mercedes pair Lewis Hamilton and Nico Rosberg have elevated Red Bull's Ricciardo into fourth place in the drivers' standings. Centre of attention: Daniel Ricciardo claims finishing above Sebastian Vettel would boost his confidence . Reigning four-times champion Vettel, meanwhile, has suffered gremlins this year and is now nine points adrift of Ricciardo, promoted from Toro Rosso at the end of last season as replacement for Mark Webber. Despite the regulation changes over the winter, not many would have put good money on the 24-year-old Australian having the edge over Vettel so far, but that is how the season is panning out. Naturally, Ricciardo is aware of the kudos he would gain should he still be ahead of Vettel come the conclusion to the season in Abu Dhabi. 'It . wouldn't be a bad thing. It would give me a lot of confidence,' said . Ricciardo, speaking ahead of this weekend's Canadian Grand Prix that was . won by Vettel 12 months ago. 'In . saying that, I've probably already done enough to show the guys I can . do it, and when I say 'can do it', not specifically beat Seb, but to . race at the front, be in a top team, and deserve to be here. 'Whatever happens in the championship, the final result, unless we win then it's probably not going to matter. 'They . (the team) will probably look at specific races in the year and say 'in . this one he did well', or 'in this he did quite well'. It would be a . nice pat on the back I guess.' I'm still smiling: Vettel has won the F1 title in the last four years but has struggled to hit the front this term . Bearing in mind Vettel has never finished behind a team-mate since he entered F1 in 2007, Ricciardo insists there is no magic to what he has achieved so far, just unwavering confidence in himself. 'It's important for me to try to learn from him (Vettel), and I'd be silly not to because he's obviously the best in the world, so I can't be stubborn about that," added Ricciardo. 'At the same time I work well when I focus on myself. If I get distracted too much with what Seb is doing then it takes my mind off what I need to be doing. On the march: Ricciardo (right) has leaped to fourth in the championship following his third place at Monaco . Surprise: Ricciardo has exceeded expectations by leading Vettel in the championship . 'I have a lot of belief in my ability, and I know if I do everything I can to my best then the results will come. 'At the moment 95 per cent is directed towards myself, and then on the side, if I can learn a bit from Seb, then I do. It's trying to do what I know first and then worry about the rest later.'

NEW YORK – The 51st International Emmy Awards celebrated the pinnacle of global television last night, honoring a diverse array of talent and productions from across the world while paying a special tribute to the legendary Sir David Attenborough. The evening, held in New York City, underscored the vast creativity and storytelling prowess thriving in television markets beyond the United States.

A profound moment of the ceremony was the presentation of the Directorate Award to Sir David Attenborough. The iconic broadcaster and naturalist was recognized for his extraordinary seven-decade career and his unparalleled contribution to television, particularly in bringing the wonders of the natural world into millions of homes and inspiring generations to care for the planet. Accepting the honor remotely, Sir David emphasized the enduring power of television to educate and connect audiences with critical global issues.

The awards saw fierce competition across 14 categories, with programs from 16 countries vying for the coveted statues. Germany's acclaimed historical drama "The Empress" (Die Kaiserin) took home the coveted Best Drama Series award, while the beloved British comedy "Derry Girls" secured the award for Best Comedy Series. The emotional Best Documentary prize went to "Mariupol: The People's Story," a powerful account from the conflict in Ukraine.

Individual acting honors were bestowed upon Martin Freeman for Best Actor for his performance in "The Responder" (UK), and Karla Souza for Best Actress for her compelling role in "La Caída" (Dive) (Mexico). Other notable winners included "A Ponte" (Brazil) for Non-Scripted Entertainment and "Harina, El Costo de un Error" (Mexico) for Best Short-Form Series, showcasing the breadth of genres recognized.

Bruce L. Paisner, President & CEO of the International Academy of Television Arts & Sciences, highlighted the record number of entries and the exceptional quality of this year's nominees. The International Emmys continue to serve as a crucial platform for celebrating the diverse narratives and artistic excellence that define television globally, reinforcing its role as a powerful medium for cultural exchange and shared human experience.

I read the reviews before I bought this. Numerous people complained about a hissing noise that you hear all the time. So why did I go ahead with buying it?Because I am _NOT_ an audiophile! I know people who will only listen to 256 kbps MP3s because they can "totally hear the difference between 192 and 256." Well, I can't! So when I read those reviews I thought that maybe the reviewers were just overly picky.Let me tell you-- they are NOT. The humming hissing noise is really loud. It sounds like those old clock radios "wake to buzz"-- if those alarm clocks have a volume of 10, this is certainly a 1.I really really thought that I could deal with it. I said, "Well, most of the time I will listen to it fairly loud." But then a song came on that had a lot of quiet parts, and you could hear that horrible hiss!I'm going to have to send it back-- it's that bad.

introduction to the objective **objective: head to this location.** the holographic map spun slowly, a shimmering blue sphere hovering above the console. Its designated point, a pulsing red beacon, lay nestled deep within the forgotten archives of Sector Gamma-7. Elias frowned, tracing the path with a wary finger. Gamma-7 hadn't been accessed in centuries, its airlocks sealed, its power grid dormant, a tomb of lost knowledge. His comm-link crackled. "You heard the Captain, Elias. We need that data module. Whatever it takes." Elias sighed, slinging his rifle higher on his back. "Whatever it takes," he echoed, stepping onto the transport platform. The objective was clear; the challenge, far less so.
