Former Prime Minister Silvio Berlusconi, who is actively campaigning for re-election in next month's general election, set off a chorus of outrage this week.
He suggested, while attending a Holocaust Memorial Day ceremony in Milan, that the Duce had not been as entirely bad as the history books made out. In fact he had done some "good things".
Mr Berlusconi said that the racial laws, which led to 8,000 Italian Jews being herded off to death camps, were Mussolini's "worst mistake".
Mr Berlusconi was even rebuked by Italy's head of state, President Giorgio Napolitano, who said that democratic Italy knew that Fascism was an "aberration".
The canny media magnate was immediately accused of courting extreme right-wing support for his proposed new People of Freedom party coalition in the run-up to the election on 24-25 February.
He later backtracked, but his tactless remarks - and his even more tactless conduct in dozing off during part of the ceremony - recalled another occasion a decade ago when he appeared to praise Mussolini by claiming that the Fascist dictator had "never killed anybody".
In Germany de-Nazification was actively and officially pursued for years after the end of World War II. Chancellor Angel Merkel said this week that Germany's responsibility for the Holocaust was "everlasting". But in Italy, Fascism was never rooted out with the same enthusiasm.
True, the new Italian republic which succeeded two decades of Fascist rule made it a crime to attempt to revive Mussolini's Fascist party.
But for decades after the war, the right-wing MSI (Italian Social Movement) led by Giorgio Almirante was represented in parliament and managed to keep alive many Fascist ideals while avoiding prosecution under this law. "If being a gentleman means that I am a Fascist," he once told the BBC, "then I am a Fascist."
The distinguished Italian historian of Fascism Emilio Gentile has written that Mussolini raised his political ideology to the status of a religious cult.
Mussolini's grand-daughter Alessandra is still active in politics. She founded a right-wing movement of her own and latterly has been supporting Mr Berlusconi's Freedom Party coalition.
Shouting, she stalked out of a TV political debate this week because a leftist journalist had, in her opinion, insulted the memory of her grandfather.
Italy's political ambivalence over its Fascist heritage has been mirrored in the arts.
The 1920s and 1930s were a period of considerable artistic and architectural endeavour and achievement, encouraged by the Mussolini regime, but political taboos have tended to hinder serious attempts to re-examine the art of the Fascist periods - until now.
This winter, an exhibition at the Palazzo Strozzi in Florence of paintings and sculpture from the 1930s entitled The Arts in Italy Beyond Fascism has attracted large numbers of visitors.
Apart from works of important Italian painters of the period who also circulated in Paris and Berlin, the exhibition included a large composite of four monumental nudes by Hitler's favourite artist, Adolf Ziegler. This was one of the most reproduced works of modern art in Nazi Germany.
The curators of the Palazzo Strozzi exhibition - under the direction of a Canadian art historian who heads the museum - invited visitors to "explore the decade without prejudice", breaking political and intellectual taboos which have endured since Mussolini's ignominious end in Milan in 1945.

Cesc Fabregas faces Arsenal for the first time since leaving the Emirates this weekend as Chelsea host the Gunners at Stamford Bridge. We asked Arsenal fan site Highbury House just what the supporters feel about a former captain and hero lining up for their bitter rivals against them, especially since he's been playing so well. If you wanted a symbol of the divide among Arsenal supporters at the moment then you will find no better example than Cesc Fabregas. There is an on-going debate, both within the Emirates and on the numerous websites devoted to the club, about Arsene Wenger and his continued tenure and Barcelona’s sale of Fabregas to Chelsea has rubbed salt into those wounds. For many of us, Cesc epitomised the great things Wenger achieved over so many years. He was the lingering ember, still smouldering in Barcelona, that so many Arsenal fans believed could re-ignite Wenger's reign and breathe quality back into a central midfield that had become dull. Cesc Fabregas (right) will line up for Jose Mourinho's high-flying Chelsea against Arsenal this weekend . Fabregas has been the driving force behind Chelsea's improved creativity and goal scoring this season . He never spoke badly of the club, even after forcing the move that hurt so many of us. I could see why he wanted to go at the time, though. Barcelona had exerted a strong pull under Pep Guardiola and it was Cesc's homeland. All the rumours about him refusing to play for us in pre-season and feigning injury were disturbing for sure but fans’ forgiveness tends to be in proportion to the player's quality and nobody could dispute Cesc had that in abundance. After the FA Cup success the word was that Wenger would push the boat out in the summer and sign the players that just about everybody in football knew we needed. So, when it became clear that the golden boy was available and Arsenal had a clause in his contract of sale that gave the club the right of first refusal, at a discount price, then what was the problem? Sign him, Arsene. The problem was that our manager didn't see it that way at all. Arsene Wenger played a key role in shaping Fabregas at Arsenal into the talented player you see today . Fabregas became club captain at Arsenal and is pictured here celebrating a goal with Robin van Persie . After several attempts Fabregas finally left Arsenal to return his boyhood club Barcelona in summer 2011 . Now, whether this was for purely footballing reasons in that he didn't want to inhibit the growth of Jack Wilshere and Aaron Ramsey or, as Cesc has said, because of the presence of Mesut Ozil. Or was it a bit of rather ill-judged payback for some shoddy behaviour against the man who had made him the great player he undoubtedly is? But the awful fact of the matter is that Arsenal said no and took a percentage of the transfer fee to, of all clubs, Chelsea. Fabregas is showing the midfield creativity at Chelsea that Arsenal would have benefited from this season . Will Wenger's decision not to resign Fabregas come back to haunt Arsenal at Stamford Bridge this weekend? Those of us who have watched Mourinho's team through gritted teeth this season couldn't have failed to have noticed that he is doing rather well for them. In fact, he puts the opposition under pressure just about every time he gets the ball in their half. Unfortunately for us and in my opinion, Cesc was exactly the player we needed and as Wenger seems to see Ozil as a left-winger I really can't see the sense in not signing him. The sudden drop in Ramsey's form and his subsequent two-month injury, plus the inconsistent nature of Jack's game has made the decision look worse and worse with each passing day. But, not to sign him and at the same time strengthening Sunday¹s opponents, Mourinho¹s Chelsea? Crazy and for some of us, painful too. Follow Highbury House on Twitter @Highbury_House .

Cristiano Ronaldo and Zlatan Ibrahimovic will go head-to-head for a place at the 2014 World Cup after Portugal and Sweden were drawn together in the European qualification playoffs. Portugal captain Ronaldo will lead his team out against Ibrahimovic's Sweden, with one of the star duo set to miss out on a ticket to football's biggest tournament following the draw at FIFA headquarters in Zurich. Eight second-place teams from the nine European groups will compete over two legs for four World Cup berths. Paulo Bento's Portugal were pipped to automatic qualification by one point, finishing behind Fabio Capello's Russia. If Portugal fail to qualify it will be the first time they have missed the tournament since 1998. Sweden, coached by Erik Hamren, will be desperate to reach a 12th World Cup after missing out on South Africa 2010. France, World Cup winners in 1998, were drawn against Euro 2012 co-hosts Ukraine, with Euro 2004 champions Greece taking on Romania. Minnows Iceland, who are bidding to become the smallest nation to successfully qualify for the quadrennial competition, will play Croatia, who sacked coach Igor Stimac last week. Stimac was replaced by former player Niko Kovac, who took over on a caretaker basis. Each of the four ties will be contested over two matches, with the first set to be played on November 15 and the second on November 19. European playoff draw in full: . Portugal vs. Sweden . Ukraine vs. France . Greece vs. Romania . Iceland vs. Croatia .

Stone Care and Maintenance for Experts
Over 45% of the earth’s composition is made of rock and stone – and as the crust circles the world’s inner core, more and more compounds are pressed together to form all new stone structures. For millennia, mankind has been harvesting these sources of rock with the intention of building homes and other types of structures – many of which have stood the test of time and can still be seen today, thousands of years after their initial erection.
Although very durable and able to resist all but the most severe damage; stones aren’t entirely safe against wear and tear, the elements and general use. Over time, even the most well-preserved rocks can start to suffer with damage and decay, and as far as stone resources within homes and gardens go; this event can be detrimental.
Fortunately, there are several methods that can help to keep stones looking clean and clear for years – and there are even products on the market that can help to reinforce the natural strength of rocks. But what are they and how do they work exactly?
Limestone and other types of commonly used rocks can suffer with colour fading over time. When these events occur, polishing powders like those manufactured by Aguila can be very beneficial. When added to water, the resulting paste is more than enough to strip away dirt and debris, before revealing a smooth surface and replenished colour.
Another very effective way to clean stone surfaces is by scrubbing them with an Aguila cleaning brush – a brand well-known for the strength and durability of its products. Unlike traditional brushes that rely on softer hairs to clean and buff, these specifically-made alternatives feature stronger fibres that run alongside the grains of the stone; allowing the potential for a much cleaner surface.
One of the most effective ways to polish a stone is by rubbing another one against it. As effective as this technique can be, it does leave a little something to be desired; especially if the rocks have different shapes, making point to point contact difficult. Polishing blocks are rocks that have been ground down and shaped to cater to a variety of proportions – and the Akemi grinding block in particular features two sides in both a finer and slightly thicker grit consistency.
The key to keeping rocky surfaces clean and clear is to polish them often – and in a consistent manner for at least a few hours, depending on the size of the surface. If allowed to be exposed to the elements for any length of time, the internal structure of rocks can begin to weaken and this is one of the main reasons why people paint brickwork and other rocky structures.

**The Mark**

"Chris," I whisper, champagne flute trembling in my grasp, "I love you beyond measure, and tomorrow I become yours forever." The crystal touches my lips as I await the chorus of celebration, the warm applause that should follow such a declaration. Instead, silence blankets the garden party like a shroud.

Every face that moments before glowed with joy now reflects something else entirely—confusion, fear, pity. My eyes drop instinctively to my lavender dress, searching for some embarrassing stain that might explain their stares. But the silk remains pristine, matching perfectly the lavender band that has circled my wrist since my fourteenth birthday. The Mark that binds Chris and me as destined souls.

Except the Mark is gone.

Where vibrant lavender should pulse with life, only bare skin remains—pale, ordinary, wrong. My stomach lurches as I turn toward Chris, desperate for reassurance, for explanation. His face mirrors the crowd's horror, but beneath it lies something worse: betrayal.

The champagne glass slips from my fingers as I flee the makeshift stage, abandoning the frozen musicians and their half-played melody. Behind me, Mother's voice rises in damage control: "A technical malfunction, surely! These devices glitch constantly—nothing more!"

I cling to her words through the sleepless night that follows.

The next morning finds me in Uncle Peter's sterile waiting room, Chris's damp palm pressed against mine. His lavender Mark seems to pulse with accusation while the wall-mounted screen drones about rebel forces advancing toward our border. I focus on the newscaster's warnings rather than the growing dread in my chest.

"Come in, sweetheart." Uncle Peter's voice carries none of its usual warmth.

The scanner feels cold against my neck as he runs it back and forth, searching for signals that should be there. When he finally sets it aside, his expression tells me everything.

"The device is functioning perfectly," he says, settling onto his stool with visible exhaustion.

"Then why—"

"PSMS. Proxy Soulmate Syndrome." The clinical term hangs between us like a death sentence. "You loved him genuinely, completely, but Chris was never your true Match."

My uncle's yellow Mark catches the fluorescent light as he explains—bright and mocking above his bare ring finger. "Some of us never find our real soulmates. Some of us settle for beautiful approximations."

"But we were happy," I whisper.

"And you can be again. I'll report this as a device failure. Marry him tomorrow—live the life you've built together. It's more than many people get."

Chris's relieved smile when I repeat the lie nearly breaks me.

Our wedding day arrives wrapped in whispered speculation. Despite Uncle Peter's falsified report, guests still steal glances at my unmarked wrist throughout the ceremony. Their curiosity feels like insects crawling across my skin, but I endure it for Chris, for the life we're choosing despite destiny's verdict.

The "I do" has barely left my lips when chaos erupts.

Screams shatter the afternoon as black-clad figures storm through our celebration, weapons raised. The rebels have arrived. I spot my father immediately—three rifle barrels aimed at the State President's badge gleaming on his chest.

Chris moves without hesitation, throwing himself between the guns and my father. The shot echoes across the garden like thunder.

Pain lances through my wrist as crimson spreads across my skin like spilled wine. My arm jerks forward involuntarily, and I see my movement mirrored by another. A young rebel soldier, no older than myself, stares at his own wrist where red now blooms like a fresh wound.

The same vivid red. The same violent shade.

His eyes meet mine across the space between us—across the smoking gun in his hands, across my husband's crumpled form, across the chasm between our worlds. In that moment, I understand the cruelest truth of all: destiny doesn't care about our choices, our happiness, or our carefully constructed lives.

It only cares about perfect, terrible timing.

As an Audioslave fan, unable to find a tracklisting for their "Show Me How To Live" CD single anywhere, I purchased it, drawn by my appreciation for their hit singles "Cochise" and "Like a Stone." The "Show Me How To Live" CD single (a release featuring the main track and bonus material) has the following tracklisting:1. The title track2. Super Stupid (Live BBC Radio 1 Session)3. Like a Stone (Live BBC Radio 1 Session)4. Gasoline (Live BBC Radio 1 Session)Look familiar? If you have Audioslave's "Like a Stone" EP (an extended play release, typically with more tracks than a single but fewer than an album), it should, because all three bonus tracks on this "Show Me How To Live" single are also featured there. While the band's cover of Funkadelic's "Super Stupid" (a track by the pioneering funk and soul group) is really cool and makes the "Show Me How To Live" single a decent purchase, the fact remains that you can get the "Like a Stone" EP (which uniquely includes a live Letterman version of "Set it Off" as well) for the exact same price. Given Audioslave's extensive live shows, it's disappointing that we can't get a new live track for each new single release.

I really should get ready for work; I have a project deadline to submit. I look at my notebook. Oh, wait, it says the deadline was last week. But nobody's angry, so everything must be fine. I can relax! I'll just watch my favorite shows on stream. Since there's no deadline, I can totally be late for work. I'm pretty important, after all; nobody's going to get mad at *me* for being a bit tardy.

There's this new post on Reddit. I live alone, and as the only user on my computer, I figured subscribing to r/gonewild was a good idea. Nobody can check what I'm looking at. It's nice to see cute girls posing for us. We truly live in a golden age of humanity. Can't decide if I should watch a stream, check Imgur's hot/new, or just browse the front page. Music's playing in the background. I feel pretty happy. There's a post about Ebola, but luckily I live in a rural area; it'll never come to me.

It's 11:48 AM now. I'm still at home, thinking of watching some 'adult' clips to relax even *more*, but I'm getting hungry. Maybe I should eat something for breakfast, but lunch is so close. My girlfriend calls, asking when I'll be at work. I tell her, "In time for lunch!" Decided to skip breakfast. I'll just get dressed and head to work right in time for lunch break!

Once upon a time long long ago, in a land far far away, there was a King who had no telepathic powers. No one did, in his entire kingdom and in all the other kingdoms surrounding his. Communication was done with organs that created airwaves, and interpreted them, a laborious process fraught with delay and misunderstanding, making it profoundly inefficient. When people brought problems to him, the inherent limitations of their verbal exchange meant there was much confusion; nuances were lost, urgency misjudged, and critical details frequently omitted or misinterpreted. Needs could never be fully met because during deliberation, a constant and arduous process, complex emotions and intricate solutions were difficult to articulate, or were intentionally overlooked by others who leveraged the ambiguity of language to protect their own interests. Every one had the ability to withhold information, leading to pervasive distrust, and they could tell "lies," falsehoods that, lacking any verifiable mental truth, with some cunning could be made to appear as truth through eloquent persuasion or selective evidence. And in this weird land, despite the constant friction of misunderstanding and manipulation, this King somehow kept order, relying on a fragile authority and the sheer force of his his pronouncements. That is, he did until one day, when his people caught him telling a lie, a transgression that shattered the delicate foundation of his rule and made him, in effect, cease to exist as their sovereign leader.

One year ago today. One year since I saw Mr. J take out those corporate tools I worked with at the bank. When he wiped the smug smile off their faces and replaced it with a much...wider one. One year since I joined his organization. Most of the assholes who followed him had stupid ideas of what his big plan was. The skin heads all talked about how he was going to cleanse the nation of "subhumans", the stoned out druggies thought he was going to tear down the government, but one by one I'd seen all of those assholes die, either in one of Mr. J's plans or just for opening their stupid mouths at the wrong time. I knew better, I knew what he was planning all along and I kept my mouth shut and did what I was supposed to do. I relished watching the idiots try and fit his plans into their own narratives, until one by one they all died and here I was, watching from a few steps back as Mr. J surveyed the wreckage of the Wayne Manor burning below us. I had been watching with mounting glee as yet another corporate tool burned in the pyre of his own opulence. Things like this had been so much easier since the Bat had disappeared last month. The rumors were that he'd finally been killed, but I didn't buy it. I figured whatever corporation that had been funding him to keep the status quo had just decided he wasn't worth the money anymore. "You've been here quite a while huh?" Oh shit, he's talking to me! Mr. J doesn't talk to any of us ever and he's actually talking to me! "U-uh, yes sir Mr. J. Been a year about now." "You know who's burning down there?" "That young corporate guy who had that stroke right? Newspaper said he fell over at a dinner someplace fancy."Crap I sound like a dumb thug from a comic book. Everyone knows him, Christ, he's been the subject of every news report for the last few weeks with the media practically blowing a load at the chance to"mourn" another trust fund baby even though he was still alive. "...You know I don't think I've ever had someone last a year with me. I think a performance review might be in order." Turning quickly he threw me to the ground before pouncing on me and presing his scalpel to my cheek. "Now then, just one small question. A simple question for a simple man. What. Am. I. Doing?" Terrified I could only stammer a weak and stupid "What?" "You've been, as it were, here for it all. You've seen all my plans. What am I doing it all for? Review my performance for me here above this viking funeral" The blade of the scalpel seemed to caress my skin as he said this. "You-you're eliminating corporate greed, you're gonna show those fat cats running everything that us little guys can.. AARRGH!"The blade turns from caress to a line of fire from one side of my mouth to my jaw. In a whisper he says."Try again." "But, that's what you've been doing right?"I struggle to talk, my face is on fire and my mouth keeps filling with blood."Why else would you destroy everything, nothing else makes sense!" The other side of my face explodes in pain and through it, I scream. "Closer that time, closer. You'll have plenty of time to think about it as those heal. Now if you'll excuse me.." And with that he threw himself over me and down into the firey blaze below us. I can still hear him laughing as he fell into the waiting flames. It keeps me up at night.

the German four-time world champion finished 0.715 seconds off  the pace set by his compatriot. Vettel's Finnish teammate, Kimi Raikkonen was fourth, 1.1 seconds behind the lead with Williams' Valtteri Bottas and Daniel Kvyat, who was making his debut for Red Bull since his switch from Toro Rosso, finishing fifth and sixth respectively. "It was great to be back in the car at this awesome track," Rosberg said, the official Formula One site reported. "Today we have the evidence that our Silver Arrow is quick again and it was a great start for the team," he added. "It seems again that it's very close between Lewis and me and he is a great driver, so I need to nail the setup every time to come out on top. This year will be a big battle again against him, I'm sure. I'm looking forward to the first weekend of the new season with all the great fans out there." Hamilton, who was forced to retire from last year's race with engine trouble, was satisfied with his pace. "It feels great to be back on track and back into a race weekend. In general it's been a good first day," Hamilton said. "Today seemed to confirm that we have pretty good pace. But there are still other quick cars out there and we can't go into tomorrow's sessions not thinking that they will be close." The opening day's racing was somewhat overshadowed by an ongoing dispute between Sauber and the Swiss team's 2014 reserve driver Giedo van der Garde. The 29-year-old Dutchman recently started legal proceeding against the Swiss team claiming they had reneged on a promise to make him one of the lead drivers for the 2015 season. Van der Garde won the case held in a Swiss court earlier this month, with the decision being upheld by Supreme Court of Victoria in Australia on Tuesday after Sauber had appealed the original ruling. During practice Van der Garde could be seen in the Sauber garage wearing his race overalls, but he did not make an appearance on track as Marcus Ericsson and Felipe Nasr completing both practice sessions. Following the initial court ruling, team principal Monisha Kaltenborn said that changing drivers hours before a Grand Prix could be dangerous. "What we cannot do is jeopardize the safety of our team, or any other driver on the track, by having an unprepared driver in a car that has now been tailored to two other assigned drivers," she said, Formula1.com reported. Brazilian Nasr was the highest placed of the two finishing 11th while Swede Marcus Ericsson was 15th. Elsewhere there was also a uncertain start for McLaren who finished way down the pecking order. Jenson Button was 13th, almost four seconds off the pace for the CNN-sponsored team while teammate Kevin Magnussen, who was deputizing for the absent Fernando Alonso, was 16th following second practice. Local favorite Daniel Ricciardo completed just nine laps of practice after his Red Bull suffered engine failure. Qualifying for Sunday's race gets underway on Saturday.

Before him, she had only understood shades of color. The world was a canvas of muted greys, functional and defined. Then, his eyes, like twin suns rising, illuminated a spectrum she hadn't known existed. It wasn’t a sudden burst, but a slow, unfolding dawn, painting every leaf with emerald, every sky with sapphire, every shadow with a depth that held its own kind of light.

His hand in hers was not just a touch, but the click of a lock finally finding its key, a whisper of recognition that resonated through bone and spirit. It was the feeling of a scattered self gathering into one perfect, whole form. In his presence, the ceaseless hum of existential doubt quieted, replaced by a profound, resonant peace. He saw not just the person she was, but the soul she was meant to be, and in his gaze, she bloomed into that vision, effortlessly, completely.

Their laughter interwove, a melody that felt both ancient and brand new, a score written just for them across the fabric of time. Their silences were richer than any conversation, filled with a shared understanding that transcended words, a comfort so absolute it felt like breathing the purest air for the first time. It was a love that wasn't a choice, but a cosmic necessity, a gravitational pull of two celestial bodies finally aligned. It wasn't perfect *despite* their flaws, but perfect *because* it embraced every jagged edge, every shadow, every vulnerability, weaving them into a tapestry of undeniable beauty. It was a home built not of bricks, but of shared breath, of unspoken promises, of a joy so pure it felt like a secret whispered by the universe, just for them.

**Ciara Mageean Withdraws from European Indoor 1500m Final Due to Lingering Cold**

Ireland’s leading middle-distance athlete Ciara Mageean has withdrawn from the women’s 1500m final at the European Indoor Athletics Championships in Belgrade, with her camp citing a lingering cold as the primary reason.

Mageean, who comfortably advanced through the heats and was considered one of Ireland’s best medal prospects, made the decision shortly before Saturday’s final. The 31-year-old had been contending with illness in the days leading up to the championship and, despite initial hopes of recovery, ultimately opted not to risk her health or the upcoming outdoor season.

Mageean’s coach, Helen Clitheroe, clarified that although the Portaferry runner had experienced some minor Achilles concerns during training, these were not a serious injury and did not contribute to her withdrawal. “Ciara has been suffering with a cold for most of this week, which unfortunately hasn’t cleared up as we hoped,” Clitheroe said. “She did mention slight stiffness in her Achilles, but that’s very mild—certainly nothing you’d call an injury. The main factor today was her health due to illness.”

Known for her resilience and competitive spirit, Mageean has a strong record in European competition, including silver medals at both the European Indoors and Outdoors. She had entered Belgrade in promising form after setting an Irish indoor 800m record earlier this season.

Athletics Ireland expressed support for Mageean’s decision, emphasizing the importance of athlete welfare as qualification for the Paris Olympics approaches. Mageean herself expressed her disappointment at missing the final, which was set to feature several of Europe’s top middle-distance runners, but reaffirmed her commitment to returning to full strength ahead of the outdoor campaign.

The Belgrade European Indoor Championships continue this weekend, with Mageean’s absence marking a blow to Ireland’s medal hopes, but her team remains optimistic about her prospects for the months ahead.

Michael Ondaatje’s "The English Patient" is a hauntingly lyrical novel set against the devastated landscape of World War II Italy. The narrative weaves together the stories of four disparate characters—an enigmatic, badly burned patient; his devoted nurse, Hana; the resourceful thief, Caravaggio; and the gentle sapper, Kip—whose lives intersect in a ruined villa.

Ondaatje’s prose is poetic, layered, and sensual, evoking the complex interplay of memory, love, and loss. The novel travels seamlessly between the present and the past, revealing the secrets and traumas each character carries. The rich descriptions and evocative imagery lend a dreamlike quality to the storytelling, while the themes of identity, nationality, and the scars of war are explored with subtlety and depth.

"The English Patient" demands patience and close attention, rewarding readers with moments of profound beauty and insight. Though its non-linear structure can be challenging, the emotional resonance and artistry are undeniable. A deeply moving meditation on the ravages of war and the redemptive power of human connection, this novel is a modern classic.

The president thanked Mr Xi for his "congratulatory letter" on his inauguration, and said he looked forward to "constructive" relations.
Mr Trump has not spoken to Mr Xi, but has called several other world leaders.
The letter, featuring standard diplomatic pleasantries, comes after a steady stream of belligerent attacks aimed at Chinese trade and policies.
In recent months, Mr Trump has challenged Beijing on sensitive issues such as Taiwan and the South China Sea. He angered China by taking a call from Taiwan's President Tsai Ing-wen, the first involving a US President or President-elect in decades.
China sees Taiwan as a breakaway province to be reunited with the mainland, if force by necessary. The US cut formal ties with Taiwan in 1979.
"President Trump stated that he looks forward to working with President Xi to develop a constructive relationship that benefits both the United States and China," the letter said, according to the White House.
Mr Trump also wished the Chinese people "a happy Lantern Festival and prosperous Year of the Rooster".
The Chinese are currently celebrating the Lunar New Year, and the official celebration period will end on Saturday with the Lantern Festival.
Beijing has yet to respond to his latest letter.
The conciliatory tone is in stark contrast to previous statements by Mr Trump, whose presidential campaign was marked by anti-China rhetoric that continued after winning the election.
In December, before his inauguration, Mr Trump posted  a series of tweets criticising China for its exchange rate policy and its operations in the South China Sea.
He also questioned the One China policy, which is the diplomatic acknowledgement by the US of Beijing's position that there is only one Chinese government, following his call with Taiwan.
Shortly after he took office on 20 January, his administration vowed to prevent China from taking territory in the South China Sea.
Beijing has so far responded cautiously, expressing "serious concern" about Mr Trump's position on the One China policy, and urging the US to maintain close ties with China.
It also lodged a protest over the phone call with Ms Tsai, dismissing it as a "petty trick", and maintained it would "defend its rights" in the South China Sea.
But state media outlets have been less restrained and have issued strongly-worded rebukes, blasting Mr Trump for  "playing with fire" on the Taiwan issue.
They also warned of serious action and a "resolute battle" against Mr Trump.

Lazy Garlic, cultivated in the foothills of the Himalayas, redefines convenience in the kitchen. Unlike traditional garlic, this innovative variety is not divided into separate cloves but grows as a single large bulb, equivalent in size to a standard garlic head. This design eliminates the hassle of extensive peeling—simply slice off the required amount, and store the remainder for future use. Now available exclusively at Lidl, which is the UK's sole supplier of single clove garlic, customers can purchase a 250g basket containing approximately eight single cloves for £1.49.

This remarkable garlic variety was first discovered growing organically in the misty foothills of China's Yunnan province. While regular garlic develops multiple cloves due to exposure to cold weather, the temperate climate of Yunnan encourages bulbs to form as a single, large clove, reaching up to two inches in diameter. Harvested in February and March, the distinctive white and purple bulbs are celebrated for their milder, exceptionally fragrant flavor, setting them apart from traditional garlic.

A Lidl spokesperson explained, “The single clove garlic originates from the mountainous Yunnan province in southwestern China. Grown at altitudes around 2,000 meters, where warm temperatures and abundant sunlight prevail, only about 50 percent of seeds produce the sought-after single clove garlic. This is not a separate species, but the result of unique environmental conditions.”

Single clove garlic is nutritionally rich and highly valued for its mild, aromatic flavor profile, making it a favorite among chefs and home cooks alike. Its straightforward peeling process significantly reduces preparation time compared to multi-clove varieties. According to Lidl, customer feedback has been overwhelmingly positive, although some initial guidance was necessary for those unfamiliar with its use. Overall, the introduction of single clove garlic has been a resounding success, further enhancing convenience and flavor in the kitchen.

Todd Timmons’s latest book, Mathematics in Nineteenth-Century America, directly examines the intellectual, cultural, and institutional developments that shaped mathematical practice in the United States during one of its most formative periods. Drawing on archival research, rich biographical sketches, and an analysis of primary sources such as correspondence, textbooks, and academic journals, Timmons situates American mathematics within broader social and political contexts.

At the heart of the book lies a central question: How did mathematics evolve from a scattered, often amateur pursuit into a burgeoning professional discipline during the nineteenth century? Timmons tracks the influence of European mathematical traditions, particularly the influx of German academic models, and details how these paradigms were adapted (and at times resisted) by American scholars. He pays special attention to pivotal institutions such as Harvard, Yale, and, after the Civil War, Johns Hopkins, highlighting the roles of key figures like Benjamin Peirce and Josiah Willard Gibbs.

Importantly, Timmons does not focus solely on elite mathematicians. The book also examines the experiences of teachers, women, and African Americans who contributed to mathematical culture, often in the face of significant obstacles. He discusses the increasing presence of mathematics in public education, the spread of mathematical societies, and the growing intersection between mathematics and the country’s burgeoning industries.

Mathematics in Nineteenth-Century America offers not only a detailed academic history but also prompts readers to consider the complex relationships between mathematics, nation-building, and identity. Timmons’s nuanced narrative makes clear that the history of American mathematics is as much about social transformation as it is about intellectual achievement, providing an essential resource for historians of science, educators, and anyone interested in the development of mathematics in America.

**Against the pristine backdrop of FIFA's glass-walled headquarters in Zurich, where the crisp November air carried the weight of national dreams, two of football's most magnetic superstars learned their World Cup fate would be decided in direct confrontation.**


Cristiano Ronaldo and Zlatan Ibrahimovic will go head-to-head for a place at the 2014 World Cup after Portugal and Sweden were drawn together in the European qualification playoffs. **The sterile conference room atmosphere couldn't contain the electric tension as officials revealed the matchup that would see one of these legendary figures watching Brazil 2014 from home.**


Portugal captain Ronaldo will lead his team out against Ibrahimovic's Sweden, with one of the star duo set to miss out on a ticket to football's biggest tournament following the draw at FIFA headquarters in Zurich. **The weight of expectation from two proud footballing nations now rests squarely on the shoulders of these iconic performers, who have dominated European football for over a decade.**


Eight second-place teams from the nine European groups will compete over two legs for four World Cup berths. **The unforgiving mathematics of qualification had already claimed several casualties, leaving these nations to battle through the playoff gauntlet with everything on the line.**


Paulo Bento's Portugal were pipped to automatic qualification by one point, finishing behind Fabio Capello's Russia. **The bitter taste of near-misses haunts Portuguese football, where memories of past glories under the scorching sun of Euro 2004 on home soil still burn bright.** If Portugal fail to qualify it will be the first time they have missed the tournament since 1998.


Sweden, coached by Erik Hamren, will be desperate to reach a 12th World Cup after missing out on South Africa 2010. **The disappointment of watching the 2010 tournament from the sidelines still lingers in Swedish football circles, where the northern lights have illuminated many World Cup dreams over the decades.**


**In the grand theater of international football, where packed stadiums echo with national anthems and the roar of passionate supporters,** France, World Cup winners in 1998, were drawn against Euro 2012 co-hosts Ukraine, with Euro 2004 champions Greece taking on Romania.


**Perhaps the most romantic storyline emerged from the icy fjords of the North Atlantic,** where minnows Iceland, who are bidding to become the smallest nation to successfully qualify for the quadrennial competition, will play Croatia, who sacked coach Igor Stimac last week. **The volcanic island nation's fairytale journey contrasts sharply with Croatia's recent turmoil,** as Stimac was replaced by former player Niko Kovac, who took over on a caretaker basis.


**As autumn gives way to winter across Europe, with floodlit stadiums preparing for the most crucial matches of the qualification cycle,** each of the four ties will be contested over two matches, with the first set to be played on November 15 and the second on November 19.


**European playoff draw in full:**

- Portugal vs. Sweden

- Ukraine vs. France  

- Greece vs. Romania

- Iceland vs. Croatia

**French Consular Official Detained on Rape Charges in Bangalore**

French consular agent Pascal Mazurier was taken into custody Tuesday morning on rape charges following allegations of child sexual abuse. The diplomat appeared before a magistrate late Tuesday evening under heavy security presence.

The charges stem from accusations made by Mazurier's Indian wife, who alleges he sexually abused their three-year-old child. Mazurier was initially questioned by authorities on Friday but has remained silent regarding the allegations.

India's External Affairs Minister S.M. Krishna clarified Wednesday that Mazurier "does not enjoy diplomatic immunity and will face trial under Indian law." The French official has been remanded to 14 days of judicial custody at Bangalore's Parapanna Agrahara jail, a facility that currently houses over 4,000 inmates and undertrials.

"This is far from over—it's merely the beginning. Let the legal process commence," Mazurier's wife, Suja Jones Mazurier, declared to The Times of India.

Mrs. Mazurier has formally petitioned both the French ambassador to India and the Indian government to prevent her husband from leaving the country pending the completion of all legal proceedings. Additionally, she has requested financial assistance from the French embassy for herself and her three children, all of whom hold French citizenship.

The case has drawn significant attention to the limits of diplomatic immunity and the jurisdiction of Indian courts over foreign consular staff in cases involving serious criminal allegations.

**Mocking hecklers is part of the job for stand-up comedians** - a skill honed through years of performing in comedy clubs where audience disruption is common. But this comic was somewhat lost for words when an NYPD officer shouted 'shut the f*** up' from the back of the room. The cop was one of four who interrupted Adam Newman's set with flashlights to arrest a homeless man allegedly masturbating in the audience. And it seemed they couldn't resist joining in on the show.


Newman, 31, was telling a joke about shampoo that had his fans in stitches at **the Upright Citizens Brigade Theatre in Chelsea, New York City** - a renowned comedy venue known for its improvisational performances and as a launching pad for comedy careers. But the next joke was cut off when four police officers barged into the room with flashlights.


True to form, the stand-up tried to make the event part of his show - **a classic comedian technique of incorporating unexpected moments into the performance**. 'Well this is the first time this has happened,' he joked, as the audience fell about laughing. He continues: 'Guys, this is part of the act, I do this every show.'


But he was lost for words when an officer retorts: 'What is happening?' Newman looks shocked. He stammers in disbelief: 'I have no idea what is going on. Couldn't you have waited until the end of my set?' One retorts: 'Obviously not. Honestly, shut the f*** up'


Silence. 'Who? Me?' Newman asks. 'Oooh...are you yelling at me?' he tentatively replied. 'Shut your mouth, all right?' the officer snapped.


At this point Newman apologized and waited for the officers to leave - as the audience wooped and cheered. **A video, posted on Newman's YouTube profile**, shows the comic then exclaiming: 'What the f*** just happened!'


'I've dealt with hecklers before and I can put a heckler in their place, I can do that, but if I yelled "you shut the fuck up" to a police officer...? If an audience said 'shut the f*** up' I would've gone crazy, but when a police officer yells 'shut the f*** up" I'm like "yes sorry!'


**The Upright Citizens Brigade Theatre in Chelsea** - founded by comedy legends Amy Poehler, Matt Besser, Ian Roberts, and Matt Walsh - **saw an unusual interruption for comic Adam Newman** during what appeared to be a regular evening performance at the venue that has hosted countless comedy shows since opening in New York in 1999.

sharon mills originated in the 1830s. later, a gristmill was constructed along the rushing waters of the Sharon Creek, becoming the economic heart of this small rural community. The mill's massive wooden waterwheel turned steadily through the seasons, grinding corn and wheat for farmers throughout the region.

By the 1850s, Sharon Mills had grown into a bustling settlement with a general store, blacksmith shop, and several modest homes clustered around the mill complex. The miller's family, the Hendersons, became prominent figures in the community, often hosting social gatherings and serving as unofficial leaders for local initiatives.

The Civil War brought both challenges and opportunities to Sharon Mills. While many young men left to serve in the Union Army, the mill remained vital for supplying flour to nearby military camps. The constant demand kept the waterwheel spinning day and night, and the Henderson family prospered despite the uncertain times.

As the railroad expanded westward in the 1870s, Sharon Mills faced new competition from larger commercial mills in distant cities. The once-thriving settlement began to decline as farmers found it more convenient to ship their grain directly to urban markets. The general store closed first, followed by the blacksmith shop, leaving only the mill and a handful of die-hard residents.

Today, Sharon Mills exists primarily as a historical marker along a quiet country road. The old gristmill has been restored as a museum, its waterwheel once again turning slowly in the creek, though now it serves to educate visitors about rural American life in the 19th century rather than grinding grain for survival.

introduction to the shahnameh** a paper presented by amin banani, provides a foundational insight into one of the most important works of Persian literature. The Shahnameh, or "The Book of Kings," composed by the poet Ferdowsi over a thousand years ago, is not simply a collection of myths and legends, but a monumental epic that preserves the cultural memory and identity of Iran. Banani, in his influential presentation, underscores the Shahnameh’s dual function: as a treasury of ancient Iranian lore and as a literary masterpiece that shaped the course of Persian language and literary style.

The paper begins by situating the Shahnameh within the historical context of its creation. Ferdowsi wrote during a period of profound political and cultural transformation, as Arab conquests and the ensuing Islamic influence profoundly altered the Iranian world. Banani notes how Ferdowsi’s epic poised itself as an act of cultural preservation, safeguarding pre-Islamic histories, ethical values, and national identity through poetic narration.

Banani further explores the structure of the Shahnameh, which unfolds over nearly 50,000 couplets and encompasses the mythical, heroic, and historical ages of Iran. The epic features an array of unforgettable characters—Rostam, Sohrab, Zal, and many others—each embodying the virtues and struggles of their age. Through their stories, Banani observes, the Shahnameh instills ideals of loyalty, justice, and heroism while also reflecting on the complexities of fate and human fallibility.

Crucially, Banani’s paper draws attention to Ferdowsi's poetic language. The Shahnameh’s use of pure Persian, with a minimal incorporation of Arabic vocabulary, is seen as a deliberate artistic choice that contributes to its enduring influence on the Persian language itself. Banani suggests that Ferdowsi’s linguistic choices were not just aesthetic, but also ideological—a means of reinforcing Iranian literary and cultural continuity amidst foreign domination.

In conclusion, Banani’s paper asserts that the Shahnameh is more than an ancient epic; it is a living cultural scripture. Its resonance extends far beyond literary circles, serving as a symbol of national identity and resilience for generations of Iranians and Persian-speaking peoples. In Banani’s analysis, the enduring power of the Shahnameh lies in its capacity to engage, instruct, and inspire—a true testament to the vision of Ferdowsi and the enduring legacy of Persian civilization.

Chris Johnson is a master of representing 3D scientific information related to air currents, heat, and the physics of light. In other words, he creates highly-accurate virtual explosions like the one you see above breaking out of a container. At the AAAS conference today, he gave a presentation about the latest cool images and videos created where he works: the Scientific Computing and Imaging Group at the University of Utah. The group creates fire and explosion simulations for chemists, amazing 3D medical images of the human body, and recently made rather haunting depictions of the ways genetically-engineered mice develop differently from normal ones. Here are a few more of the images he discussed.
What's cool about this image of a flame is that it can be looked at from many different perspectives — not just rotated around CAD-style in 3D, but also transformed to depict which areas of the flame are hottest, for example. Here the flame is in its most realistic mode, showing what a methane fire would look like to the naked eye. Chemists use an image like this to figure out how a chemical fire might behave in a variety of conditions.
Here's a 3D picture of a human skull, which shows off all the different ways that Johnson's lab can render a solid, 3D object, showing features of different densities like bone, soft tissue, and neurons. It's only very recently that images like this could be explored interactively in real time with massive parallel processing power and (of course) technologies borrowed from video games.
And here's an image of the skeleton of a mouse fetus, which researchers can explore in 3D from every angle to see how genetic changes they've induced may (or may not) have changed the way the mouse is developing in the womb.

ayan mohamud, 18, who was visiting family in the vibrant, bustling district of Camden, became an unexpected hero yesterday when she spotted smoke billowing from a ground-floor apartment and alerted residents to evacuate the building.

The University of Leeds student was walking along Regent's Canal at approximately 3:30 PM when she noticed the smoke and immediately called emergency services. Her quick thinking potentially saved dozens of lives as firefighters later confirmed the blaze could have spread rapidly through the Victorian-era building.

"I just did what anyone would do," Mohamud said from outside the cordoned area as fire crews worked to extinguish the flames. "I could see people were still inside and I knew I had to act fast."

London Fire Brigade Station Manager David Phillips praised the teenager's actions, stating that her early alert allowed crews to respond before the fire reached the upper floors where several families with young children resided.

The cause of the fire remains under investigation, though initial reports suggest it may have started in the building's electrical system. All residents were safely evacuated with no reported injuries.

Mohamud, who is studying environmental science, was visiting her aunt's family for the Easter holidays when the incident occurred. Local councillor Sarah Jenkins has nominated her for a community bravery award.

**California Man Arrested for Beating His Cocker Spaniel**

LOS ANGELES — A California man was arrested Wednesday evening after allegedly beating his three-year-old Cocker Spaniel with a stick, according to local authorities.

Officers with the Los Angeles Police Department responded to reports from concerned neighbors in the North Hollywood area who witnessed a man repeatedly striking his dog in the backyard of a residential property. Upon arrival, officers found the Cocker Spaniel, named Daisy, cowering and injured near the back fence.

Police identified the suspect as 42-year-old Michael Ramirez. He was taken into custody without incident and booked on felony animal cruelty charges. The dog was transported to a nearby animal hospital, where veterinarians treated her for bruises and lacerations. Officials indicate that Daisy is expected to recover.

“The safety and well-being of animals in our community is a top priority,” said LAPD spokesperson Officer Maria Torres. “We thank the citizens who called 911 and prevented further harm.”

Animal control officers have taken custody of Daisy while the case is under investigation. Ramirez is being held at the Van Nuys Detention Center on $50,000 bail and is expected to appear in court next week.

Anyone with additional information about the incident is encouraged to contact the Los Angeles Animal Cruelty Task Force.

# Red Flag Warning Issued for Grundy County: Extreme Fire Risk and Safety Precautions Urged

The National Weather Service has issued a Red Flag Warning for Grundy County effective immediately through 8:00 PM tonight, citing extremely dangerous fire weather conditions that pose a significant threat to public safety and property.

## Critical Fire Conditions Present

Weather officials report a dangerous combination of factors creating optimal conditions for rapid wildfire ignition and spread. Current conditions include:

- Sustained winds of 20-30 mph with gusts up to 45 mph
- Relative humidity levels dropping below 15%
- Temperatures reaching the mid-80s
- Dry vegetation from recent drought conditions

"This is an extremely dangerous situation," said Sarah Martinez, meteorologist with the National Weather Service. "Any spark could quickly develop into a fast-moving, uncontrollable wildfire under these conditions."

## Immediate Safety Measures Required

County Emergency Management Director Robert Thompson is urging all residents to take immediate precautions:

**Avoid all outdoor burning activities, including:**
- Campfires and burn barrels
- Grilling with charcoal or gas
- Welding or grinding operations
- Smoking outdoors
- Operating equipment that could create sparks

**Additional safety measures:**
- Keep vehicle windows closed when driving through dry areas
- Avoid parking on dry grass
- Report any signs of fire immediately by calling 911
- Have an evacuation plan ready
- Keep important documents and emergency supplies easily accessible

## Fire Department on High Alert

Local fire departments across Grundy County have positioned additional crews and equipment strategically throughout high-risk areas. Fire Chief Michael Anderson reports all available personnel are on standby.

"We're taking this warning very seriously," Anderson stated. "Our crews are prepared to respond rapidly, but prevention is our best defense right now."

## Agricultural and Business Impact

The warning has prompted several local businesses to suspend outdoor operations. Construction companies have halted welding activities, and agricultural operations have postponed field work involving machinery.

Local farmer Janet Peterson expressed concern about the timing. "We understand the necessity, but this puts us behind on harvest preparations," she said. "Safety has to come first though."

## Weather Outlook

Meteorologists indicate conditions may begin to improve by tomorrow morning as a weak cold front approaches, bringing slightly higher humidity and reduced wind speeds. However, fire danger will remain elevated through the weekend.

Residents are advised to monitor weather conditions closely and remain vigilant even after the Red Flag Warning expires.

## Emergency Resources

For real-time updates and emergency information:
- Grundy County Emergency Management: (555) 123-4567
- Fire emergencies: 911
- Non-emergency fire department: (555) 234-5678
- County alert system: Register at www.grundycountyalerts.gov

The Red Flag Warning remains in effect until 8:00 PM today. Residents should continue monitoring local weather services and emergency management communications for updates and potential extensions of the warning period.

Earlier this month we noted that scientists had begun calling for cool heads in the pursuit of alternative energies and climate change measures — so as to avoid unintended consequences.
Following that trend, a preliminary study released Thursday (PDF) by a consortium led by the Netherlands Environmental Assessment Agency looked at the potential ramifications of a Dutch plan to reduce greenhouse gas emissions — dubbed “Clean and Efficient.”
The study found that saving energy and increasing the use of wind energy could lead to a reduction in the emission of air-polluting compounds as well as greenhouse gases.
But it also found “uncertainties” about the effect of using biofuels. It warned that blending biofuels in diesel or gasoline could create increased emissions of air pollutants and that using blends with high amounts of biofuels required specially adapted vehicles, to avoid increased air pollution. The study also warned that large-scale capture and storage of carbon dioxide would decrease sulphur dioxide emissions, but might lead to increased nitrogen oxide emissions

## Michigan County Fairs: Fostering Youth Development

Beyond the thrilling rides, irresistible fried foods, and grandstand entertainment, Michigan county fairs serve a profound and often understated purpose: they are vibrant incubators for youth development. For generations, these annual gatherings have provided invaluable platforms for young Michiganders to cultivate essential life skills, embrace responsibility, and forge lasting connections, shaping them into capable and engaged members of their communities.

At the heart of this developmental process are organizations like 4-H and Future Farmers of America (FFA), which are intrinsically linked to the fair experience. Through these programs, children and teenagers embark on year-long projects ranging from raising livestock and growing produce to robotics, crafting, baking, and public speaking. The fair then becomes the culminating showcase for their dedication and hard work.

The preparation alone instills a robust work ethic. Young people learn the discipline required to care for an animal daily, regardless of weather or personal preference. They meticulously plan and execute projects, understanding the importance of attention to detail and meeting deadlines. This hands-on learning cultivates practical skills – whether it’s animal husbandry, carpentry, sewing, or coding – that are directly applicable to future careers and everyday life. The sheer responsibility of managing a project from inception to exhibition teaches accountability in a way few other experiences can.

Beyond tangible skills, county fairs are crucibles for character development. Participants learn resilience when a project doesn’t go as planned or when they don’t place as high as they hoped. They experience the joy of success and the lessons of defeat, fostering sportsmanship and perseverance. Public speaking and presentation skills are honed as they explain their projects to judges and the public, building confidence and articulate communication.

Furthermore, fairs are powerful community builders. Young participants interact with peers from diverse backgrounds, fostering teamwork, camaraderie, and a sense of shared purpose. They connect with mentors, judges, and experienced community members who offer guidance and encouragement, broadening their perspectives and networking opportunities. This creates a supportive environment where young people feel valued and belong. For many, the fair becomes a cherished annual tradition, strengthening family bonds and intergenerational connections as older family members pass down knowledge and support younger ones.

In an increasingly digital world, Michigan county fairs offer a vital antidote, providing real-world, hands-on experiences that encourage critical thinking, problem-solving, and direct engagement. They are not merely agricultural showcases or entertainment venues; they are dynamic educational laboratories that quietly but profoundly shape the next generation of leaders, innovators, and responsible citizens. As the gates open each year, they reaffirm their enduring role as cornerstones of youth development across Michigan.

We are a nosy country. Though to be fair, it's not entirely our fault. Between the 24/7 news cycle, social media and reality TV, we have been spoon fed other people's private business for so long we now assume it's a given to know everything. And if there are people who choose not to disclose, they must be hiding something. Being told that something's "none of your business" is slowly being characterized as rude, and if such a statement is coming from the government, it seems incriminating. Times have changed. Yet, not everything is our business. And in the political arena, there are things that should be and need to be kept quiet. News: Issa, White House clash over Fast and Furious executive privilege . I know that's hard to digest in a society where pregnancies and marriages of D-list celebrities make the cover of People magazine, but there comes a point where the public's right to know needs to take a back seat to matters like national security and diplomacy. Heads should roll because of the Fast and Furious debacle. We don't need every detail of that operation to be made public in order for that to happen. If it were an isolated sting, maybe. But it is at least the third incarnation of a gun-running scheme stretching across two administrations, which means we could be pressing to open Pandora's Box. We do not want to open Pandora's Box, not about this and certainly not about a bunch of other potentially scandalous things the federal government has been involved with. Fast and Furious? Please. We still don't have access to all of the messy facts surrounding the Iran-Contra scandal that erupted during the Reagan administration. All we know is that weapons were sold to Iran in exchange for hostages and that the proceeds from those sales were used to illegally fund rebels in Nicaragua who were supposedly fighting Communism. Lt. Col. Oliver North took one for the team back then, and there's a good chance Attorney General Eric Holder will have to take one for the team in the Fast and Furious controversy. And by team, I'm not referring to Republicans or Democrats, but rather Americans. You see, freedom isn't entirely free. It also isn't squeaky clean. And sometimes the federal government deems it necessary to get its hands a little dirty in the hopes of achieving something we generally accept as good for the country. News: By the numbers: Fast and Furious . Such as the death of Osama bin Laden. We danced, we cried, but we did not make a big deal about a secret operation that was executed in Pakistan without the permission of the Pakistani government. The Obama administration did what it thought was in the best interest of America. Much in the same way, Project Wide Receiver and Project Road Runner -- the earlier versions of Fast and Furious under President Bush -- were executed with the hope that they will do more good than harm. Hardly anyone in the public knows the finer points of these programs. Were they legal? Hell no. Were they effective? Who knows? Were they done as a way to keep America safe? Yes. North was a fall guy. Not for President Reagan but for all of us. Just as Holder has become a villain to many who are pointing fingers at him. But to go much beyond the criticism of these men runs the risk of learning that this great nation of ours is heavily involved in doing some things that are not so great. Think about it: We have allowed weapons to cross the Mexican border and into the hands of criminals for years. Many of these weapons were involved in killing innocent Mexicans. There's nothing very admirable about that. But the truth is, it's very American. By allowing guns to infiltrate Mexico's drug cartel, we thought we could trace them up the ladder to the leaders. Take off the head and the body dies. As for the innocent people who lost their lives? Collateral damage. That's the uncomfortable backstory to this scandal. And there are likely other operations like it in our nation's history that we don't even have a clue about. And maybe it's better for us not to be so nosy, not to know everything because, to paraphrase the famous line from the movie "A Few Good Men," many of us won't be able to handle the truth. The opinions expressed in this commentary are solely those of LZ Granderson.

I cannot remember myself rising from my bed; nor the blue skies that hovered above my head.
