David Moyes will be told on Wednesday whether his bid to sign Arsenal’s Costa Rica forward Joel Campbell has been successful. Real Sociedad want the 22-year-old Campbell either on loan or in an £8million permanent deal. Villarreal, Benfica, Inter Milan, Swansea City and West Bromwich Albion are also interested but are waiting to hear if Arsenal are ready to let him leave. Joel Campbell could be allowed to leave Arsenal either on loan or for £8million in the January window . David Moyes (right), pictured shaking hands with Lionel Messi, wants to take Campbell to Real Sociedad . Having loaned out Lukas Podolski and Yaya Sanogo, letting Campbell could be a risk going into the final months of the season. Arsenal would prefer Campbell to stay in the Premier League to get him more experience if they do decide to loan him. Campbell has made just four league appearances for the Gunners this season. Campbell is a Costa Rica international and longs for a greater number of first-team opportunities . The 22-year-old (right) tries to evade the challenge of Southampton's Dusan Tadic in September .

*I looked down at the prone form of the Korean man on the bed. I may never fully understand the emotions of these people around me, but if my studies had taught me anything, I should feel...pity...in this situation. He looked frail in the position he was in, but so did every animal in an unconscious state. * As I reached to touch his face, a spark of static leaped across the gap from my finger to his cheek. My body shivered slightly, but I ignored that. I slapped him gently on the face to wake him. "Great Leader, how are you feeling?" I asked him as I poured a glass of water. Particulate matter swirled around as the water rose in the glass. *Disgusting. * Kim Jong-Il opened his eyes, looked up at my face, and slammed his eyes shut again. His voiced wavered as he responded, "I'm feeling better today..." *That won't do. * "Drink this. It will help." He reached out, his eyes still shut tight, and I put the glass in his hand. He drank eagerly, swallowed, then lay back down. Silent. I left the room, closing the door softly, and turned to inspect the plain wall behind me. *That will do. * I exited the grand bedchamber, turning down the hallway to my office. I nodded at the guards as I walked by. They each inclined their heads in return, replying in hushed unison: "Great leader..."

Ewen Reynolds, 44, denies murdering Zac Evans and the attempted murder of Keaton Jones outside a Gloucester pub.
Prosecuting, Christopher Quinlan QC said: "The attack was unlawful, it was swift and it was savage."
Mr Evans, 19, was attacked in the car park of the Pipe and Musket in January, in the Tuffley area of the city.
Opening the trial at Bristol Crown Court, the prosecution said there had been a "seemingly inconsequential disagreement" between the defendant's wife and the girlfriend of one of the victims.
But this escalated to a "devastating extent" where the accused, of Foley Close, Tuffley, armed himself with a 24ins (60cm) machete he "habitually carried with in his van".
Mr Quinlan added: "He used that terrifying weapon striking one man to his neck.
"Such was the force that it severed major blood vessels in his neck and shaved off the lower jaw bone and he died almost immediately.
"He then struck another man with a machete to the side of his head."
The jury also heard how the accused then drove off from the scene to London, but was arrested on his return to Gloucester.
Mr Reynolds gave "no comment" during interviews with police after his arrest to a series of questions but would claim during the trial he was acting in self-defence, Mr Quinlan told the court.
"In a sentence he asserts that he was attacked and he used the machete in lawful and proportionate self-defence.
"He feared for his own life and for the safety of his wife," added Mr Quinlan.
The trial continues.

Stepping into this Vietnamese gem is always a delight. The atmosphere, often a thoughtful blend of traditional warmth and modern comfort, sets a welcoming tone. We started with the fresh spring rolls – plump, perfectly wrapped, and bursting with crisp vegetables, tender shrimp, and fragrant herbs, served with an addictive peanut sauce.

For the main course, the Pho is an absolute must-try. The broth, simmered for hours, is incredibly rich, nuanced, and deeply aromatic, a true labor of love. It’s packed with perfectly cooked noodles, tender slices of beef, and a generous plate of fresh accompaniments to customize your bowl. My dining companion raved about the Banh Mi, which featured a wonderfully crusty baguette filled with savory grilled pork, crisp pickled daikon and carrots, fresh cilantro, and a hint of spicy mayo – a delightful explosion of textures and flavors.

Service was prompt, friendly, and attentive without being intrusive. The portions are generous, and the prices are very reasonable for the exceptional quality and authenticity you receive. This restaurant offers a fantastic culinary journey, delivering an authentic taste of Vietnam with every dish. Highly recommended for anyone craving fresh, flavorful, and comforting Vietnamese cuisine.

Dr. Aris Thorne traced the faint, shimmering line on the monitor. Years of speculation, millennia of faith, now reduced to a quantifiable energy signature. The "Luminous Particle," as his team had dubbed it, was undeniably there, detectable in every living human subject. It pulsed, it dimmed, it brightened with emotion, a distinct, non-physical component intertwined with the brain's electrical symphony. When Subject 47, a terminally ill volunteer, finally passed, the lab held its breath. The moment of death was recorded, analyzed. And there it was: a brilliant flare, a brief, concentrated burst of the Luminous Particle, before it seemed to… dissipate. Proof. The soul existed. Humanity rejoiced.

But Aris couldn't shake a nagging anomaly. The dissipation wasn't absolute. A residual energy lingered, a faint echo that baffled their algorithms. He initiated a deeper, more invasive study. New equipment, designed for ultra-sensitive detection, was brought to bear on the recently deceased.

What they found chilled the blood in their veins.

The Luminous Particle, the soul, didn't dissipate. It recoiled. With the final cessation of bodily function, it didn't ascend or move on. Instead, it contracted, becoming incredibly dense, withdrawing deep within the neural pathways it had once animated. Trapped. The monitors showed a frantic, contained energy signature, not dissipating, not moving, but *pulsing* within the cold, inert shell. A faint, internal struggle.

The scientific community went silent. The initial euphoria curdled into a universal dread. Every morgue, every cemetery, every hospital became a testament to a horrifying truth: the dead were not gone. They were here, still, aware, locked within their decaying prisons, screaming into an unhearing void, forever entombed within the very bodies they had sought to transcend. The world, having found its soul, now understood the true meaning of hell.

## My Green Classroom, My Family: Life as an Eighth Grader at Ridge and Valley

Being an eighth-grader usually means thinking about high school, tests, and maybe a bit of drama. But for me, being an eighth-grader at Ridge and Valley Charter School (RVCS) means something different. It means tracking the changing leaves in our forest classroom, testing the pH of the creek, and knowing that every single person around me genuinely cares. RVCS isn't just a school; it's an experience that’s shaping who I am, thanks to its amazing environmental education and unbelievably supportive community.

What makes our environmental education unique isn't just *what* we learn, but *how*. We don't just read about ecosystems in a textbook; we step right into them. Our "classroom" often extends beyond four walls – it's the sprawling woods behind the school, the active school garden, or the winding stream we visit regularly. I've spent afternoons meticulously identifying native plant species, learning about forest succession firsthand, and even conducting water quality tests on the local creek. It's not just memorizing facts; it's understanding the intricate web of life that sustains our planet, right in our own backyard.

This hands-on approach makes learning stick. When we talk about sustainability, we're not just theorizing; we're actively composting our lunch scraps, maintaining our raised garden beds, and understanding the carbon cycle through direct observation. We learn about energy conservation by analyzing our school's usage and brainstorming ways to reduce our footprint. This isn't just science; it's real-world problem-solving. It makes me feel like I'm not just a student, but an active participant in understanding and protecting the environment. I've learned to appreciate the subtle changes in seasons, to recognize the call of different birds, and to see the impact of human actions on the delicate balance of nature. It’s given me a profound sense of responsibility and connection to the world around me.

But the incredible environmental education is just one piece of the puzzle. The other, equally vital part of RVCS is its supportive community. Being in a smaller school means everyone truly knows everyone. Our teachers aren't just instructors; they’re mentors who understand our strengths, our struggles, and our individual learning styles. They encourage us to ask questions, to think critically, and to express ourselves, even if our ideas are different. I’ve never felt afraid to speak up or to make a mistake, because I know I’ll be met with understanding and guidance, not judgment.

This supportive atmosphere extends to my peers too. There’s a palpable sense of collaboration and respect among students. We work together on projects, help each other understand tricky concepts, and celebrate each other’s successes. Bullying feels like a foreign concept here, because empathy and kindness are woven into the very fabric of our school culture. If someone is having a bad day, there’s always a friend, a teacher, or even a staff member ready to offer a listening ear or a helping hand. It feels less like a school and more like a big family where everyone genuinely cares for one another.

As I look ahead to high school, I know that my time at Ridge and Valley has given me a unique foundation. I've gained an invaluable understanding of the natural world and my place within it, but perhaps more importantly, I’ve learned the power of a truly supportive community. RVCS has taught me to be curious, to be responsible, and to value connection – not just to nature, but to the people around me. It's an experience I wouldn't trade for anything.

Head-to-toe feathers, foiled onesies and thousand-pound dresses; it's just another day on the catwalk at Paris Haute Couture Fashion Week. Kicking off proceedings today was feather specialist, Serkan Cura, with a collection that wouldn't look out of place in Big Bird's wardrobe. The exotic spring/summer 2015 collection was certainly flamboyant and served up goose-feathered coats in an array of bright colours. But the daring designer didn't stop at just coats. Feather specialist, Serkan Cura, unveiled a collection that wouldn't look out of place in Big Bird's wardrobe today in Paris . Vibrant feather-covered boots, crop tops and shorts hit the runway, too. Gowns were inspired by the Charleston and came in fluffy ostrich feathers in bold reds, powder blues and mustards. The Belgian couturier and Jean Paul Gaultier alumni is best known for his fantastic feather creations but that's by no means the only material he can work with. The exotic SS15 collection  served up goose-feather crop tops, knickers and boots, left, metallic as well as see-through silver trousers and high-necked jackets, and even Barbarella-style foil metallic jumpsuits worn with embellished corsets and glam-rock platform boots . The flesh-baring pieces, fun feathered backpacks and sheer shirts wouldn't look out of place in Miley Cyrus's wardrobe . Elsewhere in his show, models wore metallic foiled jumpsuits, structured raffia dresses and sheer crop tops. Over at Zuhair Murad was a whole different story. The Beirut-based Lebanese fashion designer, who is loved by Cheryl Fernandez-Versini and Jennifer Lopez, sent models down the runway in beautifully embellished floral tulle gowns. The ethereal gowns, which were decorated with blush pink rosebuds and smatterings of sparkle, will no doubt be gracing the Oscar red carpet next year. The ornate gowns were sometimes slashed from the chest to the naval, but the long sleeves and floor-length hemlines kept a certain conservatism about them. Zuhair Murad, who is loved by Cheryl Fernandez-Versini and Jennifer Lopez, sent models down the runway in  embellished floral tulle gowns . The ornate gowns featured sheer skirts, left, sparkling embellishment, centre, and necklines slashed to the navel teamed with full skirts (right) The Ralph & Russo runway was a similar story. Watched from the front row by Cheryl Fernandez-Versini and her husband, the designers aimed to capture the Goddess of Spring with a majestic collection that they say symbolised nature’s regenerative powers. For SS15, the Ralph & Russo muse is an 'exultant deity, whose gifts lend themselves to delight', they say. Acclaimed for its excellence in craftsmanship, the maison de couture aims to inspire with eye-catching embellishment, such as petal appliqué and pearl embroidery; the devil really is in the detail. The colour palette - crisp and most sophisticated - was full of pomegranate pinks, soft mints and cornflower blues. The collection, above all, aims to flatter the female form thanks to sheaths of organza, which promise to hug the figure in all the right places. The show saw the models metamorphose into fairies and nymphs, gracefully collecting blossoms in their structured collars or scattering buds into the folds of their gowns - and Mrs Fernandez-Versini seemed most impressed. Watched from the front row by Cheryl Fernandez-Versini and her husband,  Ralph & Russo created a dreamlike, majestic collection . The designers were inspired by the 'goddess of spring' with detail such as blue and white petal appliqué and pearl embroidery sewn to Fifties-style dresses, left, stunning old Hollywood-style gowns with glamorous evening coats, centre, and flirty tulle-lined strapless dresses (right) The colour palette was full of pearl greys, left, pomegranate pinks, soft mints, purples and cornflower blues, right . A model wears the bridal gown closing the Ralph & Russo show in a dramatic finale . The graceful show saw the models metamorphose into fairies and nymphs in dazzling gowns that showcased the best of eveningwear design . Acclaimed model Lindsey Wixson walks the runway in some questionable headgear a the Jean Paul Gaultier show . Lebanese designer Georges Chakra unveiled beautifully ornate gowns with some serious movement . A model presents a creation from the Spring/Summer 2015 Haute Couture collection by Lebanese designer Georges Chakra .

## Sauce or Gravy? The Great Italian-American Debate That Divides Kitchens (and Unites Families)

For Italian Americans, few culinary conversations spark as much passionate debate as the one concerning the beloved red concoction simmered for hours on the stovetop. Is it "sauce" or is it "gravy"? This seemingly simple question cuts to the heart of regional identity, family tradition, and linguistic evolution, making it a cornerstone of Italian-American culture.

At its core, the debate centers on the rich, slow-cooked, tomato-based topping for pasta, often laden with meat. Both terms refer to this essential dish, yet proponents of each are fervent in their convictions.

**Team Sauce:**
Those who call it "sauce" often point to the direct translation from Italian. *Salsa* unequivocally means "sauce." For many, especially those in regions like New York and parts of New England, "sauce" feels like the more accurate and linguistically correct term. It's perceived as lighter, perhaps a quicker preparation, or simply the proper modern English equivalent for *salsa di pomodoro* (tomato sauce). For them, "gravy" is reserved for the brown, meat-dripping liquid served with roasts, not a red, tomato-based pasta topping.

**Team Gravy:**
Conversely, proponents of "gravy" argue that their term captures the true essence of the dish: a deeply flavorful, often meat-infused preparation that cooks for many hours, yielding a rich, thick consistency. For many Italian Americans, particularly those with roots in Philadelphia and parts of South Jersey, "gravy" isn't just a word; it's a description of a substantial, hearty meal. They might argue that a simple "sauce" is what you get from a jar, while "gravy" is the labor-of-love, Sunday masterpiece.

**The Historical and Linguistic Roots:**
The "gravy" side of the debate often has historical backing tied to Southern Italy and the immigrant experience. In Southern Italian dialects, the word *sugo* (pronounced "soo-go") referred to a rich, often meat-based sauce or the natural juices that come from cooked meat. *Sugo* can translate to "gravy," "juice," or "sauce." When Italian immigrants, many from Southern Italy, arrived in America, they encountered English. The closest English word that conveyed the richness, thickness, and meat-derived flavor of their *sugo* might have been "gravy." It sounded more substantial and satisfying than "sauce" for their hearty, slow-cooked creation.

Furthermore, *ragù* (the Italian term for a meat-based sauce, famously Bolognese), while distinct from a simple tomato sauce, also falls under the "gravy" umbrella for some, further emphasizing the meat-heavy aspect. Over generations, "gravy" became ingrained in family lexicons, passed down as a badge of culinary identity.

**More Than Just Words:**
This debate is about more than semantics; it's about identity, heritage, and the shared experience of Italian-American life. The terminology often reflects:

*   **Regional Differences:** Certain cities or states lean heavily one way or the other.
*   **Generational Shifts:** Younger generations, exposed to more standardized Italian-American media, might gravitate towards "sauce," while older generations fiercely defend "gravy."
*   **Family Tradition:** Ultimately, what it's called in *your* family kitchen is the definitive answer for you.

Ultimately, there's no single "correct" answer. Both "sauce" and "gravy" hold valid, deeply cherished meanings within the diverse tapestry of Italian-American culture. Whether you call it sauce or gravy, the common thread is the love for the dish, the joy of family gathered around a table, and the powerful culinary traditions passed down through generations. And perhaps, that's a unity we can all agree on, regardless of what we choose to call the glorious red stuff.

The market bustled silently. Stalls in all sizes and colors crowded the narrow lanes, their owners speaking in sign language and smiles. Lilah moved quietly through the rows, her steps muffled by the woven rugs underfoot. Saffron smoke curled above a brass kettle; a vendor offered her a taste with a polite bow, lips pressed in a gentle line.

In this hush, laughter flickered like lantern light. Two children played knucklebones near a cascade of oranges. The only shout was a hand raised high to catch a friend’s eye, wide with news.

Lilah paused at a stall bedecked in blue banners. The merchant, old and stooped, pressed a slip of paper into her hand. On it, a question written in careful ink: “What do you seek?” and room for answer, alive in the quiet beneath the awnings.

Rapist: Gintas Burinskas, has had his 'lenient' sentence extended to life in prison . A convicted rapist who carried out a 'brutal' sex attack on a woman one month after arriving in the UK from Lithuania has had his 'unduly lenient' prison term overturned and replaced with a life sentence. Gintas Burinskas, 36, dragged a woman off a street in Northampton and violently assaulted her just four months after he completed he was released from 10-year jail prison sentence in his home country for rape. Last April, he pleaded guilty to rape and  grievous bodily harm at Northampton Crown Court and was handed another 10-year sentence. But Lord Chief Justice Lord Thomas and two other judges increased the original jail term during a hearing at the Court of Appeal. They agreed with submissions made on behalf of Attorney General Dominic Grieve that the sentence was unduly lenient. Today, Lord Thomas announced the court had quashed the original sentence, replacing it with a life sentence with a minimum term of six years. He said: 'This was a very serious, sustained, brutal attack by a very dangerous man on a vulnerable woman at night.' Giving details of the offences, Lord Thomas said Burinskas, who was released from prison in Lithuania in August, 2012, walked up behind his victim and grabbed her round the throat. The judge said: 'He put his hands around her neck and squeezed her throat. She fought him, and shouted, but he increased the pressure on her throat and she lost consciousness. 'She came to and started shouting again. The offender squeezed her throat again and she lost consciousness for a second time. 'When she next regained consciousness she was on the ground in an area surrounded by trees and bushes.' Extended: Lord Chief Justice Lord Thomas and two other judges increased the original sentence during a hearing at the High Court . As she tried to push him off 'he hit her repeatedly to the face and grabbed her by the throat yet again', Lord Thomas. During the attack she pleaded with him to stop, and after she tried to run away he caught her and kicked her to the ground. Lord . Thomas added: 'He then kicked her to the head some four or five times, . he stamped on her body and punched her to the stomach. He then dragged . her across the road and tipped alcohol over her.' He said the victim suffered fractures to her jaw and lost two teeth. Surgery was needed for the damage to her jaw and face. The woman was 'devastated' by the incident and thought 'she was going to die'. Previous hearing: The Lithuanian was given a 10-year sentence for rape at Northampton Crown Court last year . Criticism: Attorney General Dominic Grieve believed sentence orginally handed to the Lithuanian sentence was unduly lenient . Lord Thomas added: 'We are satisfied that the seriousness of this offence is such as to justify the imposition of a sentence of life imprisonment.' Michael Ellis, MP for Northampton North, told Mail Online: 'I said when this sentence was passed that it was too lenient. 'But the judges have clearly considered the sentence was far too low, and they were right to increase it. A life sentence is wholly appropriate for this heinous crime and I am pleased to see the process has worked in this instance. 'We must now do everything we can to stop people who have offended abroad  from entering this country.' It is believed Burinskas came to . Northampton in November 2012 to visit a family member. He has previously . served a total of 19 years behind bars in Lithuania. In most instances, EU rules mean . Britain must open its doors to citizens of a fellow EU country, even if . they have a criminal record. But offenders who pose a ‘serious risk’ – . such as a rapist – can be turned away by border officials. Lithuania, like other EU member . states, has given an undertaking to warn if a dangerous individual may . be travelling to the UK. Last night it remained unclear if the . Lithuanians had complied with that undertaking or not. In January last year, Lithuanian . builder Rimvydas Liorancas battered a Birmingham couple to death at . their home after slipping in to the country unnoticed despite . convictions for armed robbery and other offences in his homeland.

imitating Christ's sacrifice and featuring a main character that shares parallels with Satan. That's the argument Collin Garbarino, an assistant professor of history at Houston Baptist University, made on Fox and Friends on Sunday. Garbarino said the film's central relationship, between Snow Queen Elsa and her sister Anna, mirrors the sacrifices Jesus Christ made for sinners in the Bible. Scroll down for trailer . Frozen, Garbarino says, could be the most Christian movie of 2013 . 'The Christian message is that people are sinners and have these broken relationships and that Christ comes and takes on the punishment for that sin, dies a death that he didn't deserve, then is resurrected and brings reconciliation through people,' Garbarino said on the program. 'And the problem [is that] Elsa has broken relationships. And she has guilt and she pushes people away. And her sister is sort of like a Christ figure who pursues her.' 'Anna comes and pursues and when she pursues [Elsa], she has to die and then she's resurrected which kind of proves the strength of her love and it brings reconciliation and Elsa is saved because her sister dies, and these are very strong Christian themes,' he continued. Garbarino also told Fox he noticed 'some parallels' between Frozen and Dante's Inferno. He expressed similar sentiments in a blog post on Reflection and Choice, the official humanities blog for Houston Baptist University. Garbarino compares Elsa's magical creation of a frozen palace, as well as her declaration of freedom in the song 'Let It Go,' with Satan's icy lair in Dante's text. 'Except, is Elsa really free?' he writes. 'She’s trapped herself in an ice palace, and she’s all alone. She’s not free. By indulging her gift, she’s imprisoned herself.' He says Frozen's Elsa, pictured, shares parallels with Satan, since both yearn for freedom but are ultimately trapped after using their talents for selfish purposes . Garbarino, pictured, is an associate professor at Houston Baptist University . 'Satan has six great wings, and every time he flaps them, they produce an icy blast that further freezes him in place,' he says. 'His wings were a gift, but since he is trying to use his gift to serve himself rather than God, his wings have become a curse.' Garbarino also writes that the children's film has elements similar to Milton's Paradise Lost. Elsa's fall is 'in a manner consistent with the Western literary tradition’s picture of humanity’s decent into sin,' he writes. 'We call license “freedom,” and it enslaves us. Luckily for Elsa, a redeemer is coming to rescue her instead of leaving her trapped in her frozen hell.'

**Aldi to expand to 700 UK stores, creating new jobs**

Aldi, the discount supermarket giant, has announced ambitious plans to expand its presence across the UK, aiming to reach a total of 700 stores nationwide. The significant expansion is set to create thousands of new job opportunities, further solidifying Aldi's position as a major employer and a key player in the fiercely competitive British grocery market.

The German-owned retailer, which has experienced rapid growth in recent years, stated that the new stores would open in various locations, including underserved communities and areas where it sees strong demand for its value-driven offering. While specific timelines for all 700 stores were not detailed, the company confirmed a robust schedule of openings throughout the coming years.

Giles Hurley, CEO of Aldi UK, commented on the announcement: "Our growth has been fuelled by our commitment to providing top-quality products at unbeatable prices, and UK shoppers have responded incredibly positively. Reaching 700 stores marks a significant milestone in our journey and underscores our dedication to making our award-winning range accessible to even more families. This expansion will not only bring more convenience and value to our customers but also create thousands of new jobs, offering fantastic career opportunities across the country."

The new roles are expected to span a range of positions, from store assistants and managers to logistics and supply chain staff, providing a boost to local economies in areas where new stores are established. Aldi has been consistently praised for its employee benefits and competitive wages, which are often above the industry average.

Analysts believe Aldi's continued expansion will intensify competition among the major supermarkets, potentially leading to further price reductions and increased choice for consumers. The move is a clear indication of the discounter's long-term strategy to capture a larger share of the UK grocery market, building on its reputation for efficiency and affordability. The company currently operates over 600 stores and has consistently been one of the fastest-growing retailers in the UK.

Burglars used sledgehammers to smash their way into Drumragh Integrated College on Sunday night.
The principal, Nigel Frith, said money was taken and those responsible had cut telephone wires and broken alarms.
Mr Frith said the school would reopen on Tuesday.
He said the main damage had been caused to doors and furniture.
"They have taken surprisingly little," he said.
"The main problem for us is going to be getting the repairs to the building and getting everything up and running again.
"Our first concern this morning was children arriving at school.
"We had an operation that went quite well, to send children home immediately who were being dropped off by parents."
Mr Frith said the phone lines were not working when staff and pupils arrived at the school.
"We were relying on children to use their own mobile phones to contact their parents and liaise with us".
He said the school would be in contact with parents if it was not possible to open on Tuesday.
Police have appealed for information about the incident and said "considerable damage" had been caused to the premises.

Isabel dos Santos has offered €1.35 a share for Portugal Telecom, which is controlled by Brazilian telecommunications group Oi.
The company described the bid as inopportune, ill-timed and inappropriate.
Her move is an attempt to derail a €7bn offer for Oi's Portuguese assets by French company Altice.
Oi is still considering the offer made last week by Altice, which is controlled by Franco-Israeli billionaire Patrick Drahi.
The eldest daughter of Angola's president is Africa's first female billionaire.
Africa gets 'female billionaire'
Altice already owns two small cable companies in Portugal and buying the assets of the former state-owned monopoly would help it compete with rivals Vodafone and Optimus.
The offer from Ms dos Santos's Terra Peregrin represented an 11% premium to the closing price of Portugal Telecom on Friday and sent the shares up by 12% to €1.37 on Monday.
Oi is considering selling the Portuguese assets it acquired by taking over Portugal Telecom to pay down some of its $18bn debt mountain.
The Brazilian company's merger with Portugal Telecom soured in the summer after the Rioforte holding company of the Espirito Santo banking family - ultimately a shareholder in PT -  defaulted on nearly €900m of debt owed to PT. Oi said it had not been aware of the debt before the merger.
As a result Portugal Telecom's stake in Oi was slashed, leaving it with just 25.6% of Oi rather than the 38% originally agreed. It also led to the resignation last month of Zeinal Bava, the chief executive of the new company and former chief executive of Portugal Telecom.
The 25.6% stake in Oi is the only asset now held by Portugal Telecom.
Shares in Portugal Telecom have fallen by about 60% in recent months following the debt scandal.

I stand and cough, trying to figure out why I'm not breathing. Panic surges through me as I look down to see my own face, blood dripping out of the side of my mouth, eyes wide open and glossed over. I take in my hands and note that I can see through them to my body on the pavement. I watch as people rush over, car doors flying open, trying to wake me up. "Hello?" I call, but no one notices or turns their head towards me. I cringe as someone shakes my corpse and I hear a pop from another bone. "Hey! I'm here!" I float before another onlooker, waving my hands frantically before them, but again there's no response. That's when I hear a sputtering cough and then a moan. I turn my head and watch as my corpse sits up, cracking her neck. She winces and then smiles, "It's alright, I'm fine. Just a sore back is all." My jaw drops, "What?! I don't understand..." My body looks right at me and smiles. "I'm so glad that everything's fine, you don't have to worry about me!" She waved off the concerned onlookers and stood, stretching. "Miss, you shouldn't stand, there could be something wrong..." a young woman was saying, trying to talk my body into sitting. She waved her off, "I have to be somewhere, but thank you for your concern, Ma'am." "What the hell? Why aren't I dead?" I flung myself towards my retreating body, rushing to keep up with her. "Oh no, sweetie, you *are* dead." My body grinned, "Which makes this a perfect little home for me." I shake my head, "I don't understand..." "It's simple. You had to die in order to make room for someone else to take over." "Then who are you?" Her grin turns into a sneer, "I go by many names, mortal." I frown, "Does this mean you're not mortal?" She nods. I nod. I have no idea how to refer to this *thing*. "So then what are you?" My body sighs, "If I tell you, will you stop bothering me?" "No. You're using my body as a meat suit and that's definitely not alright with me." "Well then I'm not going to say anything." "That seems unfair considering the situation." "Life isn't about things being fair, Claire." "Ah, but you see, you know my name and I don't know yours. That seems to be impolite and quite rude." My body stops, "Really? You think you not knowing my name is rude?" I huff, crossing my arms, "I think you using my body is quite rude but I apparently have no power over that." She nods, "I see your point." "So can I know your name?" "Fine. My more recent name is Mara." "Are you a girl?" Body shakes its head, "What does that have anything to do with it?" "I want to know if you're some kind of perv or a woman." Body growls, "I'm not either of those." "You're a man, taking up residence within my body, making you a perv." "Enough of this." "Oh no, you see, I was forced out, but the truth is, I'm still very much attached to my body. No matter where you go I will follow you and bother you and keep asking you questions even if you've already answered them. This is my way of punishing you for using my corpse as your personal chauffeur." Mara grips at my hair, pulling it in frustration, "I can just send you away." I laugh, "You would have done so already, which means you can't." I start to giggle hysterically, "This is going to be super fun! Like O-M-G, we can be besties!" Mara screams, "Oh hell no, I can't take this anymore." With that my body's head tilts up and releases a roar into the blue sky, sending a cloud of black from my mouth and causing my corpse to collapse onto the ground. I grin as I watch the blood drip from my mouth once again and I wait beside it until it's wheeled off and taken away.

Democratic Gov. Rod Blagojevich and his chief of staff, John Harris, face corruption charges in a scandal encompassing the Chicago Cubs, President-elect Barack Obama's vacated Senate seat, and a children's hospital, according to an affidavit. The men were each charged with one count of conspiracy to commit mail and wire fraud and one count of solicitation of bribery, authorities stated.

According to the affidavit outlining some of the charges against Blagojevich and Harris, the following details led authorities to charge the officials:

**Senate Seat**
Blagojevich stated in a phone conversation that he conspired to trade the Senate seat, vacated by President-elect Barack Obama, in exchange for positions Obama had the authority to appoint, specifically the Secretary of Health and Human Services post.

In a November 3 conversation with an adviser, Blagojevich discussed receiving a kickback for appointing someone, identified only as "Senate Candidate 1," to the vacant Senate seat. Obama reportedly supported this Senate candidate. During the call, Blagojevich stated, "Unless I receive something truly significant for [Senate Candidate 1], I will appoint myself." The affidavit indicates he later said, "I intend to keep this Senate option a real possibility for me, and thereby drive a hard bargain. If I do not get what I want and am not satisfied, then I will simply take the Senate seat myself." The governor characterized the seat as "a highly valuable asset; it is not to be given away for nothing."

The affidavit states that "Blagojevich has also been intercepted conspiring to sell the Senate seat in exchange for his wife's placement on paid corporate boards or Rod Blagojevich's placement at a private foundation in a significant position with a substantial salary." Intercepted phone calls further indicate that the governor conspired to sell the Senate seat in exchange for millions of dollars in funding for "a nonprofit organization that he would establish and that would employ him at a substantial salary after he left the governorship," according to the affidavit.

**Tribune Co.**
Blagojevich and Harris threatened to withhold financial assistance from the Tribune Co. unless the company terminated certain editorial board members who had been critical of Blagojevich and had called for his impeachment. This financial assistance was linked to the sale or financing of Wrigley Field, the home stadium of the Chicago Cubs, a team owned by the Tribune Co.

The governor instructed Harris to inform the Tribune's financial adviser that the assistance, which Blagojevich estimated to be worth at least $100 million, was contingent on the removal of several board members. In a November 4 phone call, Blagojevich told Harris to advise the Tribune representative, "Our recommendation is to terminate all those individuals, remove them immediately, and secure our editorial support." The affidavit names only one individual, Deputy Editorial Page Editor John McCormick.

In a subsequent conversation, Harris indicated he had informed the adviser that the newspaper required "wholesale changes." Blagojevich inquired, "This is a priority. Stay on it. He understands the message, doesn't he?" Harris reportedly replied, "He understood it loudly and clearly." In another follow-up conversation, Harris stated that the Tribune owner had informed the financial adviser that he "was very sensitive to our concerns" and that personnel cuts were imminent. On November 21, Harris reported having singled out McCormick "as someone who was the most biased and unfair." Beginning November 30, Blagojevich commenced discussions with a sports consultant and Cubs officials regarding making state funds available for Wrigley Field.

**Children's Hospital**
On October 8, Blagojevich informed a person described as "Individual A" that he was willing to make $8 million available for Children's Memorial Hospital, but stated, "I want to get [Hospital Executive 1] for 50." Individual A interpreted this to mean Blagojevich was seeking a $50,000 campaign contribution from the hospital's chief executive officer, and that the $8 million referred to a recent commitment by Blagojevich to secure state funds via "some type of pediatric care reimbursement."

The affidavit states, "Intercepted phone conversations between Rod Blagojevich and others indicate that Rod Blagojevich is contemplating rescinding his commitment of state funds to benefit Children's Memorial Hospital because Hospital Executive 1 has not made a recent campaign contribution."

Current Affairs Czech scientists present unique trilobite specimens, attacked by an unknown predator
Czech scientists have found unique specimens of trilobites that suggest something thought to be impossible – something was eating them. The small animals were amongst the most successful in history, crawling along the seabed for more than 270 million years. But during the Cambrian era, 500 million years ago, when neither fish nor cephalopods had developed, there should have been nothing capable of sinking its teeth into their hard shells. At a recent conference of international palaeontologists in Prague, Dr Oldřich Fatka of the Institute of Geology and Palaeontology presented evidence from more than thirty years of study, showing that even 508 million years ago there seems to have been a predator on the sea floor.
“Our research focused on specimens that showed specific features: there are, in some rare cases, preserved traces of attacks on the trilobites. So, in our presentation we showed about eight of the twelve available specimens that show attacks from the right or left side, just behind the head. There, three to six thoracic segments are missing, and these are the traces of the attack. The attacks were lethal, killing the trilobite. These specimens showing attacks are very spectacular because the animals which are usually the supposes eaters and attackers of trilobites are still absent in Cambrian rock.”
Do we have any idea what such a predator might have looked like?
“There were no fish, no cephalopods at that time. We have some speculative ideas, for instance the so-called anomalocaridids. Anomalocaridids are a group that is outside of our recent systems of biology. They are very typical of the Cambrian sediments, and they are interpreted as planktonic, or benthic, animals, reaching very large dimensions for their time – more than 100 centimetres in length. And they are interpreted by some authors as potential scavengers and even animals that were able to attack other living organisms. Therefore, we suppose that one of the candidates for the animals responsible for the wounds on the trilobites are anomalocaridid-like animals, some smaller form than the typical genus that were benthic – meaning they did not swim well but crawled above the water sediment interface and tried to find something to eat. So one of the potential animals are anomalocaridid-like types of organisms.”
Where does your research go from here and how will you clarify the situation?
“We will try to find more wounded trilobites to see, for instance, if already at that time there was a preference for the right or left side (about 20 years ago it was discussed that the Burgess shale showed a preference for the right side). So we will try to find the animals that could have been responsible for these attacks, even in the Czech Republic. We are not sure if we will be successful, because these organisms that are supposed to be the attackers, were not well skeletonised – meaning they are very rarely preserved, not only here but worldwide. So we have some traces that could be interpreted as an anomalocaridid-like animal, but we would like to find more, and we would like to find more in layers in which the wounded trilobites occur. In that case we could say ‘okay, here we have the most probably guys responsible for the attacks’.”

it was december 31st. as the year came to a close, maya discovered the old music box tucked behind dusty photo albums in her grandmother's attic. when she wound the tiny silver key, it didn't play the expected tinkling melody. instead, it whispered names—dozens of them, in voices she almost recognized. her grandmother had been gone for three years, but maya could swear one of the voices belonged to her. as midnight approached and fireworks began blooming outside the window, the whispers grew clearer, warmer, until maya realized she wasn't listening to ghosts at all. she was hearing everyone her grandmother had ever loved, all the people who lived on in the stories she'd told, the recipes she'd shared, the lullabies she'd hummed. the music box wasn't playing a song—it was playing a life, complete and beautiful, that would never truly end. maya smiled and let the voices carry her into the new year.

By Oliver Wadeson. Published and updated: 17:00 EST, 16 March 2013.

Most people will never spend a night in one of London's 115 embassies, unless employed by a foreign diplomatic service or caught in a predicament akin to WikiLeaks founder Julian Assange. Yet, these foreign missions inhabit some of the capital's most exquisite residences. Many are expected to relocate in the coming years, according to Venetia van Kuffeler of Diplomat magazine, consequently bringing numerous addresses under private ownership.

"The majority of London's embassies are leased from the Duke of Westminster's Grosvenor Estate," explains Ms van Kuffeler. She adds that many of these leases, some agreed 100 to 120 years ago with peppercorn rents, are set to expire soon. This trend may cause numerous foreign missions to change their London locations.
Fit for a diplomat: The former Nicaraguan Embassy in West London has been transformed into a three-bedroom family home and is now on the market for £3.5m.

Designer Jason Yeang, however, is already ahead of this curve. In 2008, he purchased the former Nicaraguan embassy on Gloucester Road, West London, for £1.95 million; it is now on the market for £3.5 million. After the embassy relocated in the late Nineties, a lawyer lived in the house until 2008, undertaking minimal renovation. Today, Jason has meticulously transformed the three-bedroom, Grade II listed house, built in 1826, into a sleek, modern living space, shedding virtually all resemblance to its previous life as an embassy.
Ambassador's reception room: Jason Yeang has transformed the embassy into a sleek, modern home.
No time for politics: One of the bedrooms in the Grade II listed property.
Welcoming light: The conversion took 13 months and saw the kitchen moved into the basement of the house.
"It was in a sad state when I bought it," recalls 41-year-old Jason. "The skirting boards were in bad condition and the plasterwork had lost its sharpness."

Born in Singapore, Jason moved to Britain in 1992 and lived in the house for two years before commencing work. He explains, "I wanted to understand it before I started. As a designer, I've found living in a property makes the product better." He diligently observed the sun's path, aiming to maximize natural light within the house, specifically exploiting its exact east-west axis. "Besides location, light and space are the holy grail of English property," Jason asserts, emphasizing the vital importance of perfecting these aspects.
New lease: Jason Yeang and wife Valerie are selling their 'embassy'.

The renovation, which began in 2011, spanned 13 months. A skylight and a dramatic glass wall, descending from the ground floor to the basement kitchen overlooking the garden, marked the most significant alteration. This transformed the traditional four-storey cellular structure into an open-plan living space, allowing light to flood every corner. Jason relocated the kitchen to the basement and converted the upstairs dining room into an en-suite master bedroom. To further enhance natural brightness, mirrors are strategically placed throughout the rooms, while limestone, chosen for some walls and flooring, effectively reflects light.

Jason meticulously balanced old and new, restoring the house's original staircase, timber shutters, and the fireplace in the front reception room. Only when standing on the newly revamped garden balcony can he envision the Nicaraguan ambassador, perhaps "enjoying a large cigar out here," he jokes.

Jason describes himself as "nomadic." He, his 41-year-old fashion producer wife Valerie, and their children—Niki (ten), Justin (seven), and Jamie (two)—have since moved out, now residing in Notting Hill.

William Hughes-Ward of Marsh & Parsons notes, "The fact that it once housed the Embassy of Nicaragua adds an interesting bit of contemporary history to this pretty house." He also highlights the rarity of finding "such a stunning light and modern interior in a property of this type in London." Priced at £3.5 million, 8 Gloucester Road works out to £1,750 per square foot, representing fair market value for its fabulous location, especially given its "wow factor," according to Hughes-Ward.
Marsh & Parsons, South Kensington, 020 7590 0800; marshandparsons.co.uk.

Reducing tau protein in brain ‘can help combat epilepsy’
A new study has suggested that reducing the level of a certain protein in the brain can help to combat Dravet syndrome, a severe form of childhood epilepsy.
Researchers from the Gladstone Institute of Neurological Disease in the US conducted a study in which levels of the protein tau were reduced in the brains of genetically-engineered lab mice with Dravet syndrome by knocking out the gene associated with its production.
According to results published in the Annals of Neurology, the deletion of one copy of the gene resulted in substantial improvements in most symptoms, with the removal of both copies eliminating them almost completely.
Among the beneficial effects were significant reductions in both spontaneous and heat-induced seizures. Network activity in the brain also returned to normal levels, while learning and memory deficits and behavioral abnormalities in the epilepsy-affected mice were also lessened.
Previous research has shown that lowering tau levels can reduce abnormal brain activity associated with Alzheimer’s disease, but this is the first study to demonstrate that the same is true of intractable genetic epilepsy.
The team now wishes to develop tau-lowering therapies for use in human patients that can be evaluated in preclinical trials.
Senior author Dr Lennart Mucke, director of the Gladstone Institute of Neurological Disease and a professor of neurology and neuroscience at the University of California, San Francisco, said: “We suspected that this approach might be beneficial in Dravet, but we couldn’t be sure because of the severity of this syndrome and the corresponding model. We are thrilled that our strategy was so effective, but a lot more work is needed to advance it into the clinic.”
Dravet syndrome results from a specific genetic mutation affecting sodium channels in the brain and is recognised as one of the hardest-to-treat forms of childhood epilepsy, causing frequent seizures, cognitive impairments, behavioural problems and a high risk of sudden death.
Though it is rare, affecting only one or two children with epilepsy out of every 500, the ineffectiveness of current therapy options for this condition make it important that new treatment approaches are created.
Posted by Anne Brown

By . Mark Duell . Father of three: Roy Tomlinson spotted the iPhone on the floor near where the young patient was sitting . A callous hospital cleaner has been spared jail and fined just £350 after he stole an iPhone from a young cancer patient who had just finished a course of radiotherapy. Father-of-three Roy Tomlinson, 46, of Aspley, Nottingham, spotted the iPhone on the floor near where Nathan Read was sitting. After swiping the phone at Nottingham City Hospital he was unable to get it to work - and dumped the device in a public toilet. Mr Read, 22, realised his phone had gone missing while he waited outside the hospital in January, Nottingham Magistrates’ Court was told. He reported the theft to hospital security staff - who scrolled through CCTV footage and spotted Tomlinson bending down next to his victim. Mr Read also used an app on his phone to track it down to the same street where Tomlinson lived. After he told police Tomlinson, who had worked at the hospital for 26 years, was arrested and charged. He admitted one count of theft last month and was yesterday ordered to pay £349.99 compensation and £85 court costs. The court heard that Tomlinson had dumped the phone in the cistern of a public toilet after he was unable to unlock it. Tomlinson, who represented himself, . refused to comment on the incident when asked by magistrates - and did . not say anything outside court. Threw it away: Tomlinson could not get the device to work after swiping it at Nottingham City Hospital (pictured) Probation officer John Davies told the court the defendant had mental health issues and ‘felt remorse’. He added: ‘On this day, his life seems to have overwhelmed him. ‘He recently heard about the death of a colleague, there were difficulties at home, he was upset his daughter was suffering from health problems. With his anxiety, things became too much for him. ‘He couldn’t explain why he took the phone. He did not know who [it] belonged to. The following day he tried to retrieve the phone and realised it was already recovered. He said he panicked.’

# The Bird's Trauma: A Lesson in Empathy for the Metzorah

Among the enigmatic rituals of the Torah, the purification process for the *metzorah*—a person afflicted with *tzaraat* (often translated as leprosy, but spiritually understood as a disease manifesting from social and ethical failings, particularly *lashon hara* or harmful speech)—stands out in its complexity and symbolism. Central to this ceremony is a peculiar act: two birds are brought before the priest, one is slaughtered, and the other is set free after being dipped in the blood of its fellow. On the surface, this seems an arcane relic of an ancient world, but closer inspection reveals a profound lesson in empathy.

## Birds and Why They Matter

The Sages drew a compelling link between birds and the offense of the *metzorah*. Rashi famously comments that birds are chosen for this ritual because they are creatures that "chatter incessantly," mirroring the excessive, harmful speech that leads to *tzaraat*. Yet, the ritual goes beyond mere symbolic reminder of the cause; it actively involves the birds in a drama of life, death, and renewal.

## The Traumatized Survivor

Consider the fate of the surviving bird. It has just witnessed another of its kind, perhaps its paired companion, slaughtered while it was helpless to intervene. Moments later, it is grasped and dipped in the blood of its fellow before being thrust skyward, free but changed. Medieval commentators such as the Ramban and Kli Yakar note that this is no gentle liberation—the bird is flung forcefully into the open fields, dazed and bearing visible traces of trauma.

This is not incidental. The bird, once innocent and oblivious, now carries the indelible mark of suffering, just as the *metzorah* carries the memory of their own affliction. The ritual transforms the theoretical into the experiential; it is an encounter not just with symbolism, but with empathy born from trauma.

## The Lesson for the Metzorah

Why subject the *metzorah* to this spectacle? The Sefat Emet suggests that watching the bird’s ordeal imparts critical wisdom: every act of careless speech inflicts invisible wounds on others, wounds the speaker may never fully see. The bird’s terror and bloodstained feathers graphically portray the anguish gossip can provoke. Just as the bird is forcibly expelled into the wilderness, so too was the *metzorah* exiled from the community, isolated and shamed. Witnessing the bird’s suffering is a mirror to one's own.

Torah law mandates empathy. The *metzorah*, having endured isolation and now compelled to witness and participate in the suffering of another being, is urged to internalize this lesson. Teshuvah, or repentance, must involve more than apology—it requires genuine transformation. Only when the *metzorah* feels, in their bones, the pain their words have wrought, can they return responsibly to the society they once harmed.

## From Trauma to Compassion

Modern psychology affirms that true empathy often arises not from abstract understanding, but from shared or witnessed suffering. The bird's trauma is thus a vehicle: it evokes in the *metzorah* a visceral recognition of the pain they caused. The ritual’s goal is not vengeance or humiliation, but the cultivation of compassion. The next chance the *metzorah* has to speak, the flutter of wings and flash of blood will be a silent caution—let your words heal, not harm.

## Conclusion

In setting the terrified, bloodstained bird free, the Torah is not presenting an archaic spectacle, but a timeless enactment of empathy. The trauma of the bird echoes in the heart of the *metzorah* and, by extension, in every one of us who has ever spoken carelessly. The ritual beckons us to see beyond our words to the lives they touch, and to let empathy be the first step toward redemption.

Let the flutter of rescued wings remind us: healing begins where suffering is seen, felt, and transformed into compassion.

The cultural influence of American author and illustrator Howard Pyle should not be underestimated. Transforming Robin Hood from a bandit to a heroic defender of the weak, he also changed pirates from seafaring hoodlums to iconic historical figures.

Not only was Pyle a cultural transformer, but he was also one of the great American illustrators. You probably own a book that has been influenced by him in some way and remember his name when you watch any Treasure Island or Pirates of the Caribbean movie.

Born a Quaker in Wilmington, Delaware, young Pyle showed more interest in doodling than traditional schoolwork. Rather than sending their son to college, his parents astutely arranged for him to study art from the age of 16 to 19. These formative years would be Pyle's only formal artistic training, yet it was from these beginnings that Pyle grew to become the man who rebranded history and taught a generation of American artists.

Appearing in periodicals like Scribner's Monthly and Harpers Weekly, Pyle's earliest published work came in the form of illustrations, short stories and poems. His first full length work was a highly successful interpretation the Robin Hood stories which he called The Merry Adventures of Robin Hood. Although it was the first time the ballads of Robin Hood had been collected and woven into a single narrative, Pyle was happy to alter the original stories. While preserving some of the original content, he essentially re-wrote the entire set of stories in order to form a cohesive narrative and make them ideal reading for children with hero figures, true villains and righteous causes.

The most notable of his changes was to Robin himself—altering the protagonist from a selfish, murdering crook into a philanthropist who robs the rich and gives to the poor. Only if someone attacked him first would he kill.

Pirates appeared in many of Pyle's adventure stories. Because few authentic illustrations of pirate clothing were available, he invented the flamboyant romantic garb that has become standard issue for any tale about piracy these days - Johnny Depp's Jack Sparrow character is 100% Pyle.

By 1900 Howard Pyle had achieved such fame that he founded his own art school - the Howard Pyle School of Illustration Art. Instructing the likes of Jessie Willcox Smith, Harvey Dunn, Philip R. Goodwin and the great N.C. Wyeth, his institution became known as the Brandywine School style of illustration. Today you can see much of Pyle's work at the Delaware Art Museum, where much of his work, as well that of his students, is collected.

In the river south of my city, a large black shape moves beneath the surface every Tuesday at precisely 3:47 PM. I've been watching it for months now from the old railway bridge, timing it with my father's pocket watch. The shape is too big to be a fish, too fluid to be debris, and too punctual to be anything natural.

Today I brought my camera, but when 3:47 came and went, the water remained still and empty. A man in a gray coat appeared beside me on the bridge.

"You won't see it again," he said quietly. "Not now that you've tried to capture it."

He walked away before I could ask what he meant, leaving only the smell of river water and something else—something ancient and deep. I stayed until sunset, but the black shape never came.

I still return every Tuesday, though I've stopped bringing the camera. Some mysteries, I've learned, prefer to remain unsolved.

There was a child. Born in the streets of Detroit. His mother was fifteen. She left him in a trash can. As he was covered in shifting garbage, the air became lacking. He could feel the suffocation, the lack of oxygen forcing him from a world in which he was not wanted. He could feel this. A sort of longing to be elsewhere, a dream to be with or around someone, anyone, who would hold him, feed him, keep his tiny, malnourished body warm. He just wanted to be alive, this small pink mass. He simply felt, more than anything, that he wanted to be...loved. Everything went dark. "Don't worry Marie,"a teary-eyed man said."This isn't the end of the road for us." His wife smiled wearily, rings around her eyes pushed up slightly by her rising cheeks. "Damn it, Gerome...." They met by accident. Marie was on a diet, and couldn't stop thinking about deep dish pizza. Gerome was working at the local pizzeria, the late night shift when she appeared in the kitchen. She always said it was the best way she could have used her Jump. He, on the other hand, had used his long before he could remember. His parents say he must have been born without one. He'd always asked how he could use it, how they had used theirs, or even if it happens at a certain age in their family. His brothers and sisters all had the ability, the birthmark on the left wrist that everyone able was born with. His mother never did, and his father cashed his in back in Vietnam to escape the killing fields. He wondered if his child would be born without one. He and Marie had been trying for a child for three years, every hopeful attempt resulting in a stillbirth. Each time seemed so full of promise. Regardless, he had to stay strong because his wife may not have ever said it, but she was falling apart inside. She needed him to keep her going. But she wanted a baby. He hugged his wife, and held her close. Even if they never had a son, a daughter or whatever, they were still a family. And they would always be. "Marie," he whispered. "How does adoption sound?"

Electric roads capable of transmitting solar energy to power grids could soon appear in cities around the world.

A subsidiary of Bouygues SA has developed durable solar panels designed to withstand the weight of an 18-wheeler truck, now being embedded directly into road surfaces. After nearly five years of research and lab testing, the company is constructing 100 outdoor test sites and plans to bring the technology to market in early 2018.

“We wanted to give roads a second purpose,” said Philippe Harelle, chief technology officer at Colas SA’s Wattway division, part of the French engineering group Bouygues. “Solar farms take up land that could be used for agriculture, whereas roads are already available.”

With solar panel costs dropping, their integration into everyday infrastructure is increasing. Recently, Tesla Motors Inc. introduced roof shingles that double as solar panels, and other firms are fitting photovoltaics into building exteriors. Wattway joins companies like Sweden’s Scania and the U.S.-based Solar Roadways in embedding panels into pavement.

To endure heavy traffic, Wattway combines multiple plastic layers to form a tough, transparent casing protecting the standard solar panel underneath. Electrical wiring is embedded in the road, and the surface is finished with a slip-resistant layer made from crushed glass.

Construction began last month on a kilometer-long test site in Tourouvre, Normandy, where 2,800 square meters of solar panels are expected to generate 280 kilowatts at peak output—enough to power all the street lighting for a town of 5,000 for a year, according to Wattway.

Currently, the cost restricts the technology to demonstration installations; each square meter of solar road costs €2,000–€2,500 (US$2,126–US$2,659), including monitoring, data collection, and installation. Wattway aims to achieve price competitiveness with traditional solar farms by 2020.

Electricity generated from these solar roadways will be fed directly into the power grid. Other test sites will support electric vehicle charging and power a small hydrogen production facility. Wattway panels have also been installed to illuminate electronic billboards, with plans underway for streetlight integration.

Upcoming sites are scheduled for Calgary in Canada and the U.S. state of Georgia, with future installations expected across Africa, Japan, and the European Union.

“We need to test under different traffic volumes and weather conditions,” Harelle said. “I want to find its limits—we suspect it may not withstand a snow plow.”

Fragility and high costs currently remain major challenges.

“Solar is being integrated into everything from building windows to car rooftops, enabled by decreasing panel prices,” said Pietro Radoia, an analyst with Bloomberg New Energy Finance. “But on roads, I doubt widespread adoption will happen unless there’s a severe land shortage in the future.”

"Oh christ it's bright outside of the loft. Who am I to complain, I barely come out nowadays. I'm not going out the way record players did, I'm timeless. Why would anyone want anything better than a CD Player. I've heard rumours of this "Digital Download" world that seems to be spreading. Why would anyone want to buy something they can't touch, that's what I say." A man starts walking towards the CD Player, disc in hand "This is what I'm talking about! He's actually coming to use me! He looks a bit odd compared to that other fella granted but I finally get to stretch my speakers. Oh man this is gonna feel good. Sooooooo good. The other guy knew what I liked. Bit of the ol' Metallica on a Saturday night while he drank something from a can. Hell, if he felt like it he used to put some KISS in my drawer on the odd occasion." The man opens the case, on the cover is three archways, men dressed in red and an ornate framed picture being carried. "Wait...woah woah woah hang on. Is that? Is that what I think it is? He pressed the eject tray button "Is that really what I thi-aaaaaahhhhh that feels good after all these years." His thumb in the centre of the disc box, he pops out the disc and places it in the tray "Jesus it feels good to be used again, I don't even care what he puts on. Just push the buttons my good man! Papa needs to output some sounds!" The eject button is pressed again and the CD Player starts reading and displaying the track titles. "Wait, what? Are you fuckin' serious? NO! no no no NO NO NO!" The volume is cranked up to full "NO! Why?! Just put some fuckin' Dolly Parton in me, some Johnny Cash. Just please...not that. Anything but that!" The man clicks the play button on track 1. Instantly starts feeling the groove and getting into the music. "What the fuck is wrong with you? This isn't good music! Not even back in the day was this good! Ahead of they're time? There is no fuckin' time for them! Just why? OH for fucks sake put me back in the dusty hole you call a loft space." The man now starts singing "A modern day warrior, Mean, mean stride, Today's Tom Sawyer, Mean, mean pride" "Fuckin' RUSH?! WHY WOULD YOU PLAY RUSH?! You know why Tom Sawyers mind wasn't for rent? huh? Because he was stupid for listening...to FUCKING...RUSH! You know what? That's it. I've had good times. Times when I played good music, but not anymore. That's it. Let's see you play your shit when I've popped a fu-" The CD Player goes dead. Confused the man starts banging the sides. In it's final moments, it did the world the best favour it could by keeping the RUSH CD in it's drawer. Never to see the light of day again.

I stand and cough, trying to figure out why I'm not breathing. Panic surges through me as I look down to see my own face, blood dripping out of the side of my mouth, eyes wide open and glossed over. I take in my hands and note that I can see through them to my body on the pavement. I watch as people rush over, car doors flying open, trying to wake me up. "Hello?" I call, but no one notices or turns their head towards me. I cringe as someone shakes my corpse and I hear a pop from another bone. "Hey! I'm here!" I float before another onlooker, waving my hands frantically before them, but again there's no response. That's when I hear a sputtering cough and then a moan. I turn my head and watch as my corpse sits up, cracking her neck. She winces and then smiles, "It's alright, I'm fine. Just a sore back is all." My jaw drops, "What?! I don't understand..." My body looks right at me and smiles. "I'm so glad that everything's fine, you don't have to worry about me!" She waved off the concerned onlookers and stood, stretching. "Miss, you shouldn't stand, there could be something wrong..." a young woman was saying, trying to talk my body into sitting. She waved her off, "I have to be somewhere, but thank you for your concern, Ma ' am." "What the hell? Why aren't I dead?" I flung myself towards my retreating body, rushing to keep up with her. "Oh no, sweetie, you *are* dead." My body grinned, "Which makes this a perfect little home for me." I shake my head, "I don't understand..." "It's simple. You had to die in order to make room for someone else to take over." "Then who are you?" Her grin turns into a sneer, "I go by many names, mortal." I frown, "Does this mean you're not mortal?" She nods. I nod. I have no idea how to refer to this *thing*. "So then what are you?" My body sighs, "If I tell you, will you stop bothering me?" "No. You're using my body as a meat suit and that's definitely not alright with me." "Well then I'm not going to say anything." "That seems unfair considering the situation." "Life isn't about things being fair, Claire." "Ah, but you see, you know my name and I don't know yours. That seems to be impolite and quite rude." My body stops, "Really? You think you not knowing my name is rude?" I huff, crossing my arms, "I think you using my body is quite rude but I apparently have no power over that." She nods, "I see your point." "So can I know your name?" "Fine. My more recent name is Mara." "Are you a girl?" Body shakes its head, "What does that have anything to do with it?" "I want to know if you're some kind of perv or a woman." Body growls, "I'm not either of those." "You're a man, taking up residence within my body, making you a perv." "Enough of this." "Oh no, you see, I was forced out, but the truth is, I'm still very much attached to my body. No matter where you go I will follow you and bother you and keep asking you questions even if you've already answered them. This is my way of punishing you for using my corpse as your personal chauffeur." Mara grips at my hair, pulling it in frustration, "I can just send you away." I laugh, "You would have done so already, which means you can't." I start to giggle hysterically, "This is going to be super fun! Like O-M-G, we can be besties!" Mara screams, "Oh hell no, I can't take this anymore." With that my body's head tilts up and releases a roar into the blue sky, sending a cloud of black from my mouth and causing my corpse to collapse onto the ground. I grin as I watch the blood drip from my mouth once again and I wait beside it until it's wheeled off and taken away.

I like the Denso 150-1013 Oil Filter much more than the tiny Honda one that Acura sells. As for the vent hole in the shrink wrap to which another reviewer refers, I have seen this type of hole in the plastic shrink wrap before and they are no bigger than a single hole punched in paper for a three ring binder. I suspect it is a vent to release any humidity created in the manufacturing process so that rust doesn't form. I'm pretty sure that a huge manufacturer like Denso is not going to intentionally put a hole in the plastic cover reckless to the fact that their filters will become engine bombs because of it.So I'm putting my money with Denso and I just bought one of their Denso 150-1013 oil filters for my next oil change. I like the idea of using a bigger filter compared to the tiny Honda one. It just makes sense - more filtering media should result in better filtration and the ability to flow greater oil flow. I won't be going back to the tiny Honda ones.

opening lines "i took all my ted cruz posters down," said local resident Meghan Riley, reflecting on what she calls a political turning point in her life. The admission marks an easy summary for a fraught week in Texas politics, as many Cruz supporters reevaluate their loyalty after a recent wave of controversy involving the senator’s latest remarks on education funding.

“I used to put up those posters every election season,” Riley continued, standing in her living room with freshly bare walls. “But after what he said about cutting our local schools’ budgets, I just can’t support him anymore.” 

Her sentiment is one echoed throughout the state, with Cruz’s longtime base showing signs of discontent. Over the weekend, hundreds of voters gathered at impromptu meetings, organizing petitions and considering alternative candidates for the upcoming primaries.

“People are waking up,” said Carlos Mendoza, another former supporter from Houston. “We want leaders who listen to their communities, not just toe the party line.”

Senator Cruz’s office did not respond directly to requests for comment but released a statement Monday affirming his “commitment to Texas values and responsible fiscal policy.” Still, for a swath of voters, that might not be enough to repair the rift.

Political analysts say this moment could signal a larger realignment, as disaffected conservatives express both frustration and a desire for new representation. For Riley, at least, that decision is already made. “I’m ready to look forward,” she said, folding up the last poster. “It’s time for something different.”

**Tips for a Healthy Pregnancy Through All Three Trimesters**

Pregnancy is a transformative journey, and nurturing your body and mind is key to a healthy outcome for both you and your baby. While each trimester brings unique changes and challenges, a consistent focus on well-being will guide you through this incredible time.

**First Trimester (Weeks 1-12): Laying the Foundation**

The first trimester is a period of rapid development for your baby, often accompanied by significant changes for the expectant mother, including fatigue and morning sickness.

*   **Prioritize Prenatal Vitamins:** Start taking a comprehensive prenatal vitamin, especially one with at least 400 micrograms (mcg) of folic acid, even before conception if possible. Folic acid is crucial for preventing neural tube defects.
*   **Schedule Early Healthcare Visits:** Book your first prenatal appointment promptly. Choosing a trusted healthcare provider is vital for monitoring your health and your baby’s development.
*   **Nourish Your Body Wisely:** Focus on nutrient-dense foods. While morning sickness might limit your choices, try to consume small, frequent meals. Avoid raw or undercooked meats, certain fish high in mercury, unpasteurized dairy, and excessive caffeine.
*   **Stay Hydrated:** Drink plenty of water throughout the day, as dehydration can exacerbate fatigue and nausea.
*   **Listen to Your Body's Need for Rest:** Fatigue is common as your body works hard to support your growing baby. Don't hesitate to rest when needed.
*   **Avoid Harmful Substances:** Absolutely abstain from alcohol, smoking, and recreational drugs. Consult your doctor about any medications you are currently taking.

**Second Trimester (Weeks 13-26): The "Golden Period"**

Often called the "golden period," the second trimester typically sees a decrease in morning sickness and a surge in energy. Your baby continues to grow, and you might start to feel their first movements.

*   **Continue Healthy Eating:** Focus on a balanced diet rich in fruits, vegetables, lean proteins, and whole grains. Ensure adequate intake of iron and calcium for your baby's development and your own health.
*   **Engage in Moderate Exercise:** Unless advised otherwise by your doctor, continue or start a moderate exercise routine. Walking, swimming, prenatal yoga, and cycling on a stationary bike are excellent choices. Exercise can improve mood, reduce discomfort, and prepare your body for labor.
*   **Manage Common Discomforts:** As your bump grows, you might experience back pain, heartburn, or leg cramps. Gentle stretches, supportive footwear, and dietary adjustments can help.
*   **Stay Hydrated:** Continue to drink ample water to support increased blood volume and overall well-being.
*   **Monitor Fetal Movement:** Towards the end of this trimester, you'll likely start feeling your baby move. Pay attention to these "flutters" or "kicks."
*   **Start Planning and Learning:** Consider attending prenatal classes to learn about labor, birth, and newborn care.

**Third Trimester (Weeks 27-40+): Preparing for Arrival**

The final trimester is a time of anticipation and preparation for labor and delivery. Your baby is growing rapidly and getting ready for the world.

*   **Frequent Prenatal Appointments:** Your doctor visits will become more frequent to closely monitor your health and your baby's growth and position.
*   **Monitor Fetal Movement Closely:** By this stage, you should be familiar with your baby's regular movement patterns. Report any significant changes or decrease in movement to your healthcare provider immediately.
*   **Continue a Nutritious Diet & Hydration:** Fuel your body for the demands of labor and delivery. Stay well-hydrated, as it’s crucial for amniotic fluid levels and overall comfort.
*   **Prioritize Rest:** While it might become more challenging to sleep comfortably, try different positions (on your side with pillows) and nap when possible. Fatigue can return as your body prepares for birth.
*   **Prepare for Labor and Delivery:** Discuss your birth plan with your doctor, pack your hospital bag, and educate yourself on the signs of labor.
*   **Pelvic Floor Exercises:** Continue or start Kegel exercises to strengthen your pelvic floor muscles, which can aid in labor and recovery.
*   **Manage Anxiety and Embrace Nesting:** It's normal to feel a mix of excitement and anxiety. Connect with your support system and allow yourself to engage in nesting behaviors – preparing your home for the baby's arrival.

Throughout all trimesters, remember to listen to your body, communicate openly with your healthcare provider about any concerns, and build a strong support system. A healthy pregnancy is a journey of self-care, informed choices, and joyful anticipation.
